topics.title: Architecture of PLC - Types of PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Architecture of PLC - Types of PLC ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of PLC A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, microprocessor-based, specialized industrial computer that uses programmable memory to store instructions and to implement specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations in order to control machines and processes automatically in real-time industrial environments. According to the **National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA)**, a PLC is defined as: > "A digitally operating electronic apparatus which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of instructions by implementing specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic to control, through digital or analog input/output modules, various types of machines or processes." > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PLC is the brain of modern automation. Its key distinguishing feature from a general-purpose computer is its **ruggedness**, **immunity to electrical noise**, and ability to operate in harsh industrial environments with extreme temperature, humidity, and vibration. ### 1.2 What is PLC Architecture? **PLC Architecture** refers to the systematic internal organization and arrangement of hardware and software components within a Programmable Logic Controller. The architecture defines how the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Memory System**, **Input/Output (I/O) Interface Modules**, **Power Supply Unit**, and the **Programming Device** communicate via an internal bus structure to execute the user-defined control logic. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Brain for a Factory Imagine a busy factory assembly line producing cars. The factory has hundreds of sensors (eyes and ears), motors (muscles), conveyor belts (nerves), and operators (arms). Without someone making decisions, the entire line would collapse. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: PLC as a Digital Factory Manager** > - **CPU** = The Manager's brain → makes all the decisions > - **Memory** = The Manager's notebook → remembers the rules and past actions > - **Input Modules** = The Manager's eyes/ears → receives signals from sensors > - **Output Modules** = The Manager's hands/voice → sends commands to motors, valves > - **Power Supply** = The Manager's heartbeat → keeps everything alive > - **Programming Device** = The Manager's training manual → the rules taught to him > - **Communication Ports** = The Manager's telephone → talks to other managers Just as a human brain continuously **senses → thinks → acts → repeats**, a PLC executes its control logic in a continuous loop called the **Scan Cycle**. This is the heartbeat of every PLC. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Metric | |---|---| | Operating Temperature | **0 °C to 60 °C** (industrial grade) | | Supply Voltage (Common) | **24 V DC** or **120/230 V AC** | | Scan Time | **1 ms to 50 ms** (application dependent) | | I/O Capacity (Small PLC) | **up to 128 I/O** | | I/O Capacity (Large PLC) | **over 2048 I/O** | | Memory Backup | Lithium battery / Flash / EEPROM | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block-by-Block Architecture of a PLC The standard architecture of a PLC consists of the following six major functional blocks: #### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU) The CPU is the heart of the PLC. It contains: - **Microprocessor/CPU chip** — executes the control program - **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — performs arithmetic and logical operations - **Control Unit (CU)** — decodes instructions and coordinates data flow - **Registers** — small, high-speed internal memory (Accumulator, Program Counter, Address Register, Status Register) **Function:** The CPU continuously reads input status, executes the user program stored in memory, and updates the output status. #### 2.1.2 Memory System The PLC memory is broadly classified into two categories: | Memory Type | Function | Volatility | |---|---|---| | **System Memory (ROM/Flash)** | Stores operating system, firmware | Non-volatile | | **User Program Memory (RAM/EEPROM)** | Stores ladder logic / FBD / STL | Battery-backed | | **Data Memory / I/O Image Table** | Stores current I/O status and intermediate results | Battery-backed | | **Flags / Internal Relays (M-coils)** | Software-based internal logic bits | Battery-backed | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Point:** The **I/O Image Table** is a unique PLC concept. The CPU does not read physical inputs continuously. Instead, it reads *all inputs* into an **Input Image Table (PII)** in one shot at the start of the scan, executes the program using these snapshots, and writes the results to the **Output Image Table (POI)**, which is then transferred to physical outputs at the end of the scan. #### 2.1.3 Input Interface Module This section accepts real-world signals from **field devices** and conditions them for the CPU. **Types of Inputs:** - **Digital (Discrete) Inputs:** 24 V DC, 120 V AC, 230 V AC — switch ON/OFF signals from limit switches, push-buttons, proximity sensors, photoelectric sensors - **Analog Inputs:** 4–20 mA current loop, 0–10 V DC voltage — from temperature transmitters, pressure transmitters, flow meters, level sensors - **Specialized Inputs:** High-speed counters (HSC), thermocouple, RTD, encoder inputs **Functions of Input Module:** 1. **Signal Conditioning** — filtering, debouncing 2. **Electrical Isolation** — Optocoupler / Transformer isolation between field and CPU 3. **Voltage Level Translation** — e.g., 230 V AC → 5 V DC for CPU 4. **Surge Protection** — Protects CPU from industrial surges #### 2.1.4 Output Interface Module The output module takes the low-level logic signals from the CPU and amplifies them to drive field actuators. **Types of Outputs:** - **Relay Outputs:** Electromechanical, rated up to 230 V AC, 2 A — slow but versatile - **Transistor Outputs:** DC only, fast switching (typically 0.5 ms to 1 ms) — used for solenoids, DC relays - **Triac Outputs:** AC only, fast and silent — used for AC loads - **Analog Outputs:** 4–20 mA or 0–10 V — to drive variable frequency drives (VFDs), control valves > [!IMPORTANT] > **Output Module Failure Modes:** Always check the **Three-State Operation** of PLC outputs: (1) **ON** (energized), (2) **OFF** (de-energized), and (3) **FAULT** (open or shorted). This diagnostic capability is critical for KTU and industrial practice. #### 2.1.5 Power Supply Unit (PSU) The PSU converts the available mains supply (e.g., 120/230 V AC) into: - **Internal DC Voltages** — typically +5 V DC (logic), +24 V DC (sensors), ±12 V DC (analog) - **Sensor Power** — supplies 24 V DC to field sensors Most PLCs have a **redundant/backup power supply** option for critical applications. #### 2.1.6 Programming Device / Communication Port This is the human-machine interface to program the PLC. Historical and modern options: - **Handheld Programmer** (deprecated today) - **Personal Computer (PC) with proprietary software** — e.g., Siemens TIA Portal, Allen-Bradley RSLogix 500, Mitsubishi GX Works - **HMI (Human-Machine Interface) panels** for on-site monitoring - **Communication Protocols:** RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet/IP, Profinet, Modbus TCP/RTU, Profibus ### 2.2 Internal Bus Architecture The internal bus structure of a PLC interconnects all components: $$\text{Bus} = \{\text{Address Bus}, \text{Data Bus}, \text{Control Bus}\}$$ - **Address Bus:** Carries the memory address of the data being accessed - **Data Bus:** Carries the actual binary data (8, 16, 32, or 64-bit wide) - **Control Bus:** Carries read/write, interrupt, and clock signals > [!NOTE] > In modern modular PLCs, the backplane itself acts as the bus. Modules plug into the backplane and communicate via **parallel backplane bus** or **serial high-speed bus** (e.g., Siemens backplane uses serial communication at 48 Mbps or higher). ### 2.3 The PLC Scan Cycle — Operational Heartbeat The CPU continuously performs four sequential steps in a cycle: | Step | Action | Duration | |---|---|---| | **1. Input Scan** | Reads all physical inputs → writes to PII | $t_{in}$ | | **2. Program Scan** | Executes user program logic sequentially | $t_{prog}$ | | **3. Output Scan** | Writes POI → physical output modules | $t_{out}$ | | **4. Housekeeping** | Diagnostics, communication, memory management | $t_{house}$ | **Total Scan Time Formula:** $$T_{scan} = t_{in} + t_{prog} + t_{out} + t_{house}$$ For a KTU-type problem: $$T_{scan} = \left( \frac{N_{in}}{k_{in}} \right) + \left( \frac{S_{prog}}{k_{proc}} \right) + \left( \frac{N_{out}}{k_{out}} \right) + t_{house}$$ where: - $N_{in}$ = Number of input points - $N_{out}$ = Number of output points - $S_{prog}$ = Program size in memory words - $k_{in}, k_{out}, k_{proc}$ = Throughput constants (manufacturer-specific) ### 2.4 Types of PLC PLCs are classified based on **physical structure**, **I/O capacity**, **functionality**, and **application**. #### 2.4.1 Classification Based on Physical Structure **A. Compact / Integrated (Fixed) PLC** - CPU, I/O, and PSU are built into a single unit - I/O count is **fixed** (cannot be expanded) - Lower cost, smaller footprint - Examples: Siemens LOGO!, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1100, Mitsubishi MELSEC-FX1S - Best for: Small machines, OEM applications, simple ON/OFF control **B. Modular (Rack-Mounted) PLC** - CPU, I/O, and PSU are separate modules that plug into a common rack/backplane - I/O count is **expandable** by adding more modules - Higher cost, complex setup - Examples: Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Schneider Modicon M580 - Best for: Large process plants, complex discrete and process control > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Distinguishing Point:** Compact PLCs use a **fixed I/O scan architecture** where I/O addressing is hardwired to terminal positions. Modular PLCs use a **configurable I/O scan** where each slot is assigned an address via software configuration (hardware configuration table). #### 2.4.2 Classification Based on I/O Capacity This is the **most commonly asked classification** in KTU exams: | PLC Class | I/O Capacity | Application Area | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Nano PLC** | < 16 I/O | Very small OEM, educational | Siemens LOGO! 8 | | **Micro PLC** | 16–32 I/O | Small machine control | Siemens S7-200, Mitsubishi FX1S | | **Small PLC** | 32–128 I/O | Conveyor, small process | Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1500 | | **Medium PLC** | 128–512 I/O | Medium plant, batching | Siemens S7-300, Allen-Bradley SLC-500 | | **Large PLC** | 512–2048+ I/O | Large process plant, power plant | Siemens S7-400, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix | #### 2.4.3 Classification Based on Output Type - **Relay Output PLC** — for AC and DC loads, slow response - **Transistor Output PLC** — DC only, fast switching, used for servo/encoder - **Triac Output PLC** — AC only, silent operation - **Analog Output PLC** — for proportional control #### 2.4.4 Classification Based on Function/Application - **Hard PLC** — physical hardware (most common) - **Soft PLC (PC-based PLC)** — runs on a personal computer with a real-time OS - **Safety PLC (Fail-Safe PLC)** — certified for SIL-1 to SIL-3 applications (e.g., emergency shutdown, fire and gas systems) - **Redundant PLC** — Dual CPU systems for high-availability applications ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use | |---|---|---| | Total Scan Time | $T_{scan} = t_{in} + t_{prog} + t_{out} + t_{house}$ | Calculate response time | | I/O Scan Time | $t_{I/O} = \dfrac{N_{I/O}}{f_{clock}} \times k$ | Estimate I/O throughput | | Maximum I/O | $N_{max} = \dfrac{T_{scan\_max}}{T_{per\_input}}$ | System design | | Memory Word Size | 1 Word = **16 bits** (most PLCs) | Memory sizing | | Response Time | $T_{response} = T_{scan(max)} + T_{input\_delay} + T_{output\_delay}$ | Critical control design | | I/O Bit Addressing | 1 Address = 1 physical channel | Programming | | Scan Resolution | $R = \dfrac{T_{scan}}{T_{process}}$ | Control quality check | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake in KTU:** Students often write $T_{scan} = t_{prog}$ only. The complete scan cycle includes input, program, output, and housekeeping. The CPU **does not** read inputs and outputs *during* program execution. This is the **Input/Output Image Table** concept. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Total Scan Time — Detailed Logical Walkthrough **Given:** A Siemens S7-200 PLC controls a small packaging line with the following: - 32 digital inputs - 24 digital outputs - Program size = 4 Kilo-Words (1 Kilo = 1024 words) - Input scan rate = 1 input per 10 microseconds - Output scan rate = 1 output per 15 microseconds - Processor executes at 2 microseconds per word - Housekeeping time = 5 milliseconds **Step 1: Calculate Input Scan Time ($t_{in}$)** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{in} &= N_{in} \times t_{per\_input} \\ t_{in} &= 32 \times 10 \;\mu s \\ t_{in} &= 320 \;\mu s \\ t_{in} &= 0.32 \;ms \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Each input requires 10 microseconds to be read, conditioned, and stored in the PII. With 32 inputs, multiply by 32. **Step 2: Calculate Output Scan Time ($t_{out}$)** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{out} &= N_{out} \times t_{per\_output} \\ t_{out} &= 24 \times 15 \;\mu s \\ t_{out} &= 360 \;\mu s \\ t_{out} &= 0.36 \;ms \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Each output requires 15 microseconds to be transferred from POI to the physical output module and latched. **Step 3: Calculate Program Scan Time ($t_{prog}$)** $$ \begin{aligned} S_{prog} &= 4 \times 1024 \;words \\ S_{prog} &= 4096 \;words \\ t_{prog} &= S_{prog} \times t_{per\_word} \\ t_{prog} &= 4096 \times 2 \;\mu s \\ t_{prog} &= 8192 \;\mu s \\ t_{prog} &= 8.192 \;ms \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* 4 Kilo-Words means 4 × 1024 = 4096 memory words. At 2 microseconds per word, the CPU needs 4096 × 2 = 8192 microseconds to execute the entire program sequentially. **Step 4: Housekeeping Time ($t_{house}$)** $$ t_{house} = 5 \;ms \quad \text{(given)} $$ **Step 5: Total Scan Time ($T_{scan}$)** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{scan} &= t_{in} + t_{prog} + t_{out} + t_{house} \\ T_{scan} &= 0.32 \;ms + 8.192 \;ms + 0.36 \;ms + 5 \;ms \\ T_{scan} &= 13.872 \;ms \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The total scan time is approximately **13.87 milliseconds**. This means the PLC updates outputs 1 / 0.01387 ≈ **72 times per second**. ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Compact vs. Modular PLC | Parameter | Compact PLC | Modular PLC | |---|---|---| | **Physical Form** | Single integrated unit | Separate plug-in modules | | **I/O Expansion** | Not possible (or very limited) | Highly flexible, slot-based | | **Cost** | Low (₹5,000 – ₹30,000) | High (₹50,000 – ₹5,00,000+) | | **Application** | Small machines, OEM | Large process plants | | **Programming Language** | Ladder, FBD | Ladder, FBD, SFC, ST, CFC | | **Memory Capacity** | Limited (few KB) | Large (MBs) | | **Redundancy Support** | Not available | CPU, PSU, and communication redundancy possible | | **Hot-Swap Modules** | No | Yes (in mid-to-high end) | | **Maintenance** | Replace whole unit | Replace individual module | | **Example** | Siemens LOGO! 8 | Siemens S7-1500 | ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — I/O Capacity Classes | Class | I/O Range | Typical Memory | Program Scan Speed | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Nano | < 16 | < 2 KB | 0.1–0.5 ms/K | Educational, home automation | | Micro | 16–32 | 2–4 KB | 0.5–1 ms/K | Small machine (vending, packaging) | | Small | 32–128 | 4–16 KB | 1–2 ms/K | Conveyor, filling line | | Medium | 128–512 | 16–256 KB | 0.1–0.5 ms/K | Batch process, SCADA interfacing | | Large | 512+ | 1 MB – 16 MB+ | < 0.05 ms/K | Power plant, paper mill, refinery | ### 3.4 Logical Flow — Why I/O Image Table is Used **Problem:** If the CPU reads each input *during* program execution, an input that changes mid-scan could be missed or read inconsistently. **Solution Derivation:** 1. At start of scan, CPU reads **all inputs** at once → stored in **Process Input Image (PII)** 2. Program execution uses **only PII values** (snapshot, not live) 3. During program execution, live input changes are **not seen** by the CPU 4. At end of scan, CPU writes **Process Output Image (POI)** to physical outputs 5. Output is **latched** and held until next cycle $$ \begin{aligned} \text{At } t = 0: \quad & PII \leftarrow \text{Physical Inputs} \\ \text{For } 0 < t \leq t_{scan}: \quad & \text{Execute program using } PII, POI \\ \text{At } t = t_{scan}: \quad & \text{Physical Outputs} \leftarrow POI \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Consequence:** Maximum delay between input change and output update = $1 \times T_{scan}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Engineering Implication:** This is why for **high-speed applications** (e.g., packaging at 1000 items/min), engineers must use **interrupt-based I/O handling** or **high-speed counter modules** instead of the regular scan cycle. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Overall PLC Architecture — Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB PS[Power Supply Unit
230V AC to 5V DC and 24V DC] CPU[Central Processing Unit
Microprocessor ALU and Control Unit] MEM[Memory System
ROM RAM EEPROM Flash] IN[Input Module
Digital and Analog Sensors] OUT[Output Module
Relay Transistor Triac] PG[Programming Device
PC or HMI] BUS[Internal Backplane Bus
Address Data Control] F1[Field Sensors
Limit Switch Proximity RTD] F2[Field Actuators
Motor Valve Solenoid] PS --> BUS CPU --> BUS MEM --> BUS IN --> BUS OUT --> BUS PG --> BUS F1 --> IN IN --> CPU CPU --> OUT OUT --> F2 PG -.RS232 Ethernet.-> CPU ``` ### 4.2 PLC Scan Cycle Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start of Scan Cycle] --> B[Read All Physical Inputs] B --> C[Store into Process Input Image PII] C --> D[Execute User Program Sequentially] D --> E[Use PII as Input Values] E --> F[Store Results in Process Output Image POI] F --> G[Write POI to Physical Outputs] G --> H[Housekeeping Diagnostics and Communication] H --> I[Wait for Next Scan Trigger] I --> A style A fill:#ffeb99 style G fill:#99ccff style H fill:#cc99ff ``` ### 4.3 Compact vs Modular PLC — Visual Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph C[Compact PLC] C1[Power Supply] C2[CPU] C3[Fixed Inputs] C4[Fixed Outputs] C5[Communication Port] C1 --- C2 --- C3 --- C4 --- C5 end subgraph M[Modular PLC with Rack] R[Backplane Bus] M1[PSU Module] M2[CPU Module] M3[DI Module] M4[DO Module] M5[AI Module] M6[AO Module] M7[Communication Module] R --- M1 R --- M2 R --- M3 R --- M4 R --- M5 R --- M6 R --- M7 end style C fill:#ffe4b5 style M fill:#b5e4ff ``` ### 4.4 Classification of PLC Types — Hierarchical Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD PLC[Programmable Logic Controller] PLC --> A1[Based on Physical Structure] PLC --> A2[Based on I/O Capacity] PLC --> A3[Based on Output Type] PLC --> A4[Based on Application] A1 --> B1[Compact Integrated] A1 --> B2[Modular Rack Mounted] A2 --> C1[Nano below 16 I/O] A2 --> C2[Micro 16 to 32 I/O] A2 --> C3[Small 32 to 128 I/O] A2 --> C4[Medium 128 to 512 I/O] A2 --> C5[Large above 512 I/O] A3 --> D1[Relay Output] A3 --> D2[Transistor Output] A3 --> D3[Triac Output] A3 --> D4[Analog Output] A4 --> E1[Hard PLC] A4 --> E2[Soft PLC] A4 --> E3[Safety PLC] A4 --> E4[Redundant PLC] style PLC fill:#ffcc66 ``` ### 4.5 Functional Architecture Flow — Signal Path ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Sensor 24V DC] -->|Signal| IM[Input Module with Optocoupler] IM -->|5V Logic| PII[Process Input Image RAM] PII -->|Snapshot Read| CPU[CPU executes Ladder Logic] CPU -->|Writes Results| POI[Process Output Image RAM] POI -->|5V Logic| OM[Output Module Driver Circuit] OM -->|24V DC or 230V AC| ACT[Actuator Motor Valve] style CPU fill:#ff9966 style PII fill:#ffff99 style POI fill:#ffff99 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a PLC. List the major functional blocks of a PLC.** **Model Answer:** A Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) is a digital electronic device that uses a programmable memory to store instructions and to implement functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations for controlling industrial processes in real-time. The six major functional blocks of a PLC are: 1. Central Processing Unit (CPU) 2. Memory System 3. Input Interface Module 4. Output Interface Module 5. Power Supply Unit 6. Programming Device / Communication Port *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for listing with brief explanation)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Differentiate between Compact PLC and Modular PLC.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Compact PLC | Modular PLC | |---|---|---| | Construction | All blocks in one unit | Separate plug-in modules | | I/O Expansion | Fixed, not expandable | Expandable via additional modules | | Cost | Low | High | | Application | Small machines | Large process plants | | Maintenance | Replace whole unit | Replace faulty module | | Example | Siemens LOGO! | Siemens S7-1500 | *(3 marks — any 3 valid differences with example)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a PLC. Describe the function of each block. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Classify PLCs based on I/O capacity. Explain each class with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** The architecture of a PLC consists of six main functional blocks interconnected via an internal bus structure. **1. Power Supply Unit (PSU):** Converts 120/230 V AC mains into regulated DC voltages — typically +5 V DC for internal logic circuits, +24 V DC for sensor power, and ±12 V DC for analog circuits. It also provides isolation and protection against voltage fluctuations. **2. Central Processing Unit (CPU):** The brain of the PLC. It contains a microprocessor, Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), Control Unit (CU), and registers. The CPU fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, executes them, and stores results. It manages the scan cycle: read inputs → execute program → update outputs. **3. Memory System:** Divided into System Memory (ROM/Flash for OS and firmware) and User Memory (RAM/EEPROM for ladder logic and data). The I/O image tables (PII and POI) are part of the data memory. **4. Input Module:** Accepts signals from field sensors (limit switches, proximity sensors, push-buttons, analog transmitters). Provides signal conditioning, electrical isolation via optocouplers, and voltage level translation. **5. Output Module:** Drives field actuators (contactors, solenoid valves, motors, VFDs). Available in relay, transistor, and triac variants. Provides amplification and isolation between the CPU and the high-power field devices. **6. Programming Device:** A PC or handheld unit used to write, edit, download, and monitor the user program. Communicates via RS-232, RS-485, USB, or Ethernet using vendor-specific protocols (e.g., PPI for Siemens S7-200, EtherNet/IP for Allen-Bradley). **Interconnection:** All blocks communicate via a parallel backplane bus (address, data, and control lines) for high-speed data transfer. *(7 marks — 2 for diagram + 5 for block-wise description)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** PLCs are classified into five major categories based on I/O capacity: **1. Nano PLC (< 16 I/O):** - Used in very small OEM applications and educational labs - Memory: < 2 KB - Example: Siemens LOGO! 8 **2. Micro PLC (16–32 I/O):** - Used in small standalone machines such as vending machines, small conveyors - Memory: 2–4 KB - Example: Siemens S7-200 SMART, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1100 **3. Small PLC (32–128 I/O):** - Used in packaging lines, filling stations, and material handling - Memory: 4–16 KB - Example: Allen-Bradley SLC-500 (low-end), Mitsubishi FX3U **4. Medium PLC (128–512 I/O):** - Used in batch processes, SCADA interfacing, and sub-process control - Memory: 16–256 KB - Example: Siemens S7-300, Schneider Modicon M340 **5. Large PLC (512 – 2048+ I/O):** - Used in large process industries like refineries, power plants, and paper mills - Memory: 1 MB to several MB - Supports redundancy and complex languages (SFC, CFC, Structured Text) - Example: Siemens S7-400/S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix *(7 marks — classification table 3 + examples and applications 4)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Explain the working of a PLC with the help of a scan cycle diagram. Derive the total scan time formula. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare relay output, transistor output, and triac output modules in a PLC. Which is preferred for high-speed DC applications and why? **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** The PLC operates on a **cyclic scan principle**, repeating four steps continuously: **Step 1: Input Scan** The CPU reads the status of all physical input devices and stores the result in the **Process Input Image (PII)** table in RAM. This takes time $t_{in}$. **Step 2: Program Scan (Logic Execution)** The CPU executes the user program (e.g., ladder logic) **top to bottom, left to right**, using the values stored in the PII (not the live inputs). The results are written to the **Process Output Image (POI)**. This takes time $t_{prog}$. **Step 3: Output Scan** The contents of the POI are transferred to the output modules, which energize or de-energize the connected field actuators. This takes time $t_{out}$. **Step 4: Housekeeping** The CPU performs self-diagnostics, handles communication with the programming device/HMI, updates timers, and manages interrupts. This takes time $t_{house}$. **Derivation of Total Scan Time:** $$T_{scan} = t_{in} + t_{prog} + t_{out} + t_{house}$$ Expanding further: $$T_{scan} = \left( \frac{N_{in}}{k_{in}} \right) + \left( \frac{S_{prog}}{k_{proc}} \right) + \left( \frac{N_{out}}{k_{out}} \right) + t_{house}$$ The scan time determines the **maximum response time** of the system. For high-speed processes, the scan time must be less than the process time constant. *(7 marks — 4 for scan cycle explanation + 3 for derivation)* --- **Model Solution for (b):** | Parameter | Relay Output | Transistor Output | Triac Output | |---|---|---|---| | **Output Type** | Electromechanical contact | Solid-state (BJT/MOSFET) | Solid-state (SCR-based) | | **Switching Speed** | Slow (10–20 ms) | Very fast (0.1–1 ms) | Fast (1–10 ms) | | **Load Type** | AC and DC | DC only | AC only | | **Current Rating** | Up to 8 A per point | 0.5–2 A per point | 1–2 A per point | | **Lifetime** | Limited (mechanical wear) | Very long (no moving parts) | Long | | **Silent Operation** | No (clicking sound) | Yes | Yes | | **Leakage Current** | Zero | Very small | Small (needs snubber) | | **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Moderate | | **Example Use** | Motor starter, contactor | Solenoid valve, LED, DC motor | AC lamp, heater | **Preferred for High-Speed DC Applications:** **Transistor Output Module** **Reasons:** 1. Very high switching speed (microseconds), ideal for high-speed counting and pulse applications 2. Long operational life with no mechanical wear 3. DC-only operation matches the load 4. Bounce-free operation 5. Compatible with PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signals for proportional DC control Relay outputs are too slow (10–20 ms) and Triac outputs do not work on DC. *(7 marks — 4 for comparison table + 3 for selection with justification)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU PLC Architecture Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the I/O Image Table concept** — Many students write that the CPU reads inputs "during" program execution. This is **wrong**. Inputs are read in a snapshot at the start of the scan. > 2. **Confusing system memory with user memory** — ROM/Flash is for OS, RAM/EEPROM is for the user program. Do not interchange them. > 3. **Writing "scan time = program time"** — This is incomplete. Always include all four components. > 4. **Mixing up relay and triac outputs** — Relay = AC & DC, mechanical; Triac = AC only, solid-state. > 5. **Not giving examples** — KTU examiners expect at least one brand example (Siemens LOGO!, S7-200, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix, etc.) for full marks in classification questions. > 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always mention milliseconds (ms) or microseconds (μs) for scan time calculations. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Architecture of PLC — Types of PLC:** - **PLC** = Industrial computer for real-time control, defined by NEMA. - **Architecture = 6 blocks:** CPU, Memory, I/O Modules, PSU, Programming Device, Bus. - **CPU** = Microprocessor + ALU + Control Unit + Registers. - **Memory** = ROM/Flash (system) + RAM/EEPROM (user) + I/O Image Tables. - **PII** (Process Input Image) and **POI** (Process Output Image) are **snapshot tables** updated only at the boundaries of the scan. - **Scan Cycle = 4 Steps:** Input → Program → Output → Housekeeping. - **Total Scan Time:** $T_{scan} = t_{in} + t_{prog} + t_{out} + t_{house}$. - **Compact PLC** = Single unit, fixed I/O, low cost, small applications. - **Modular PLC** = Plug-in modules, expandable, high cost, large process plants. - **5 Classes of PLC by I/O:** Nano (<16), Micro (16–32), Small (32–128), Medium (128–512), Large (512+). - **Output types:** Relay (AC&DC, slow), Transistor (DC, fast), Triac (AC, fast). - **Input types:** Digital (24V DC, 110V AC) and Analog (4–20 mA, 0–10V). - **Modern PLCs** also include **Soft PLCs** (PC-based) and **Safety PLCs** (SIL-1 to SIL-3). - **Communication:** RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet/IP, Profinet, Modbus TCP/RTU, Profibus. - **Key brands to remember:** Siemens (LOGO!, S7-1200, S7-1500), Allen-Bradley (MicroLogix, ControlLogix), Mitsubishi (MELSEC-FX), Schneider (Modicon).

topics.title: PLC Modules, Input and Output Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PLC Modules, Input and Output Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# PLC Modules, Input and Output Modules — Core Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, industrially hardened digital computer specifically designed to perform control functions in industrial environments. It continuously monitors input devices, executes a user-defined logic program stored in its memory, and generates corresponding output commands to actuators — all in real time using a **cyclic scan architecture**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A PLC is a microprocessor-based controller with a modular architecture, comprising a **CPU**, **Memory**, **Power Supply**, **Input**, and **Output** modules interconnected via a common **backplane** or fieldbus, executing logic via a deterministic **ladder/structured text** program.

The physical PLC system is therefore not a single chip but a **collection of interchangeable, rack-mounted modules**, each performing a specific role.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine a factory's control system as a **human body**:

| Body Part | PLC Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Brain** | CPU Module | Decides what to do |
| **Memory (Long-term & Short-term)** | ROM/RAM Modules | Stores program and live data |
| **Heart (Power Supply)** | Power Supply Module | Pumps energy to all organs |
| **Sensory Nerves (Skin, Eyes, Ears)** | **Input Modules** | Receive signals from the world |
| **Motor Nerves (Muscles)** | **Output Modules** | Send commands to actuators |
| **Spinal Cord / Nervous System** | Backplane / Communication Bus | Carries signals between parts |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as nerves can only handle a certain voltage before burning out, PLC I/O modules are designed for specific voltage/current ranges (e.g., **24 V DC**, **120 V AC**, **4–20 mA**) and provide electrical isolation to protect the delicate CPU.

## 1.3 Why Modular Architecture?

PLC designers chose modular construction because industrial systems require:

1. **Scalability** — adding more I/O as the plant grows
2. **Maintainability** — swapping one faulty module without halting the plant
3. **Flexibility** — mixing digital, analog, high-speed, and communication modules
4. **Safety** — galvanic isolation between field wiring and CPU logic
5. **Standardization** — IEC 61131-2 defines electrical and mechanical standards

> [!TIP]
> Think of it like a **LEGO set**. The CPU is the baseplate. You snap on power supply, then input modules (sensors), then output modules (motor starters, valves). Each LEGO brick has a standard connector — in PLCs, this connector is the **rack backplane** or **DIN-rail bus connector**.

## 1.4 The Five Major PLC Modules

1. **CPU (Processor) Module** — Houses the microprocessor (e.g., ARM Cortex, Intel x86) and executes the scan cycle: *Read Inputs → Execute Program → Update Outputs → Housekeeping*.
2. **Memory Module** — Split into:
   - **System ROM/Flash** — Firmware, OS, fixed functions
   - **User Program Memory (RAM/EEPROM)** — Ladder logic, function blocks
   - **Data Memory (RAM)** — Timers, counters, flags, I/O image tables
3. **Power Supply Module** — Converts mains (**230 V AC** / **120 V AC**) to regulated DC voltages (**+5 V DC**, **+24 V DC**, **±15 V DC**) for internal circuitry and sometimes for sensor power.
4. **Input Modules** — Interface **field input devices** (sensors, switches, transmitters) to the low-voltage CPU.
5. **Output Modules** — Interface the **low-power CPU** to **field actuators** (contactors, solenoid valves, VFDs, indicator lamps).

## 1.5 Quick-Reference I/O Categories

| Category | Examples | Typical Signal Levels |
|---|---|---|
| **Discrete (Digital) Inputs** | Push buttons, limit switches, proximity sensors | **24 V DC**, **120 V AC**, **230 V AC** |
| **Discrete (Digital) Outputs** | Relays, solenoids, lamps, motor contactors | **24 V DC**, **120 V AC**, **230 V AC**, Relay contacts |
| **Analog Inputs** | RTDs, thermocouples, pressure transmitters | **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V DC**, **±10 V DC**, **0–5 V DC** |
| **Analog Outputs** | Variable speed drives, control valves, dampers | **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V DC** |
| **Specialty I/O** | High-speed counters, motion, servo, PID, HART | Per module spec |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The PLC Scan Cycle — Foundational Concept

Every PLC module operates under a strict repeating cycle. Understanding this is **non-negotiable** for KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 Cyclic Scan Steps

1. **Input Scan (Read Phase)** — CPU reads the status of every input module and copies it into the **Process Image Input (PII)** memory table. This takes **microseconds to milliseconds**.
2. **Program Execution** — CPU processes the user program (ladder, FBD, ST, IL, SFC) from top to bottom, using values from the PII. Logic results are written into the **Process Image Output (PIO)** table.
3. **Output Scan (Write Phase)** — CPU writes the contents of the PIO to the physical output modules, energizing/de-energizing field actuators.
4. **Housekeeping (Overhead Tasks)** — Communication servicing, self-diagnostics, timer/counter updates, and memory housekeeping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Total Scan Time:** **5 ms to 50 ms** for small to medium PLCs. High-performance PLCs achieve **< 1 ms**.

### 2.1.2 Why the Process Image Exists

The PII/PIO provides **consistency**. If inputs were read directly during program execution, a signal that changes mid-scan could cause non-deterministic logic. Snapshotting inputs at the start of the scan guarantees **deterministic** behavior.

## 2.2 Input Module — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Functional Block Structure

A typical AC or DC input module contains:

| Internal Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| **Terminal Block** | Field wiring termination (screw/spring) |
| **Optical Isolator (Optocoupler)** | Galvanic isolation; ~**2.5 kV to 5 kV** withstand |
| **RC / Debounce Filter** | Removes contact bounce and EMI |
| **Threshold Detector (Schmitt Trigger)** | Converts analog voltage to clean logic level |
| **Status LED** | Indicates input state |
| **Backplane Interface** | Communicates with CPU |

### 2.2.2 Sinking vs Sourcing (Critical for KTU)

The terms describe current flow direction relative to the input terminal:

- **Sinking Input** — Current **flows INTO** the input terminal. The input module acts as a **load to ground**. Requires a **sourcing (PNP)** sensor.
- **Sourcing Input** — Current **flows OUT OF** the input terminal. The input module acts as a **source of current**. Requires a **sinking (NPN)** sensor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** "**P**NP **S**ources, **N**PN **S**inks."
> Sinking inputs are most common in **Asia/Europe**; sourcing inputs are more common in **North America**.

### 2.2.3 Analog Input Module Internals

Analog inputs require more sophistication than digital inputs:

| Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Multiplexer (MUX)** | Time-shares one ADC across many channels |
| **Instrumentation Amplifier** | High CMRR differential amplification |
| **Anti-Aliasing Filter** | Low-pass filter, typically **fc = 1/(2·Ts)** |
| **ADC** | Converts analog to digital, often **16-bit** resolution |
| **Isolation** | Optical or transformer isolation |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Scaling, linearization (RTD/TC curves) |

### 2.2.4 Resolution and Quantization

For an analog input with range **0–10 V** and an **n-bit ADC**:

$$
Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}
$$

For **n = 16** and **V_FS = 10 V**:

$$
Q = \frac{10}{65536} \approx 152.6 \, \mu V / LSB
$$

### 2.2.5 Current Loop Signal (4–20 mA) Conversion

For a **250 Ω** sense resistor:

$$
V_{out} = I_{in} \times R_{sense} = 20 \text{ mA} \times 250 \, \Omega = 5 \text{ V (full scale)}
$$

This **5 V** then feeds the ADC.

## 2.3 Output Module — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Output Stage Technologies

| Output Type | Construction | Switching Speed | Typical Load |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Relay (Electromechanical)** | SPDT/DPDT contacts, coil-driven | **10 ms** (slow) | Universal AC/DC up to **2 A / 250 V AC** |
| **Triac (AC Output)** | Solid-state AC switch | **< 1 ms** | AC loads only, **0.5 A to 2 A** |
| **Transistor — Bipolar (DC)** | BJT open-collector | **< 0.1 ms** | DC solenoids, lamps |
| **Transistor — MOSFET (DC)** | Low-R<sub>DS(on)</sub> FET | **< 1 µs** | High-speed DC loads |

> [!NOTE]
> **Relay outputs are universal but slow** (mechanical wear). **Transistor outputs are fast but DC-only** and **polarity-sensitive**.

### 2.3.2 Flyback Diode Protection

When switching **inductive DC loads** (solenoids, relays), the collapsing magnetic field generates a high reverse voltage spike. A **flyback diode** in **reverse-bias** across the load absorbs this energy:

$$
V_{spike} = -L \frac{di}{dt}
$$

The diode clamps this to approximately **−0.7 V**, protecting the output transistor.

### 2.3.3 Analog Output Module Internals

| Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **DAC** | Converts digital word to analog voltage/current |
| **Output Amplifier** | Buffers and drives the field |
| **V/I Converter** | Produces **4–20 mA** from **0–10 V** reference |
| **Isolation** | Optical or transformer-based |
| **Short-Circuit Protection** | Current-limiting foldback |

## 2.4 The Backplane / Communication Bus

The backplane is the **highway** connecting all modules. Common standards:

| Standard | Type | Speed |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel Backplane** | Proprietary (Siemens S7, Allen-Bradley) | Very high, deterministic |
| **PROFIBUS-DP** | Serial fieldbus | **12 Mbps** |
| **DeviceNet** | CAN-based | **500 kbps** |
| **EtherNet/IP** | Industrial Ethernet | **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps** |
| **PROFINET** | Real-time Ethernet | **100 Mbps** |

Modern **distributed I/O** (e.g., Siemens ET 200, Allen-Bradley POINT I/O) allows modules to be located remotely on the fieldbus, dramatically reducing wiring costs.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table for ESE — at least one numerical appears every year.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quantization step (ADC) | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | V / LSB |
| 2 | 4–20 mA to Voltage | $V = I \times R_{sense}$ | V (use **250 Ω** for 1–5 V) |
| 3 | % of span from current | $\% = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Process variable from 4–20 mA | $PV = PV_{min} + \dfrac{I - 4}{16} (PV_{max} - PV_{min})$ | Engineering units |
| 5 | Scan time (approx.) | $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{program} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$ | ms |
| 6 | Energy stored in inductor | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ | Joules |
| 7 | Input filter time constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | seconds |
| 8 | Sinking vs Sourcing current | Sink = current into terminal; Source = current out | mA |
| 9 | Resolution in bits | $n = \log_{2} \left( \dfrac{V_{FS}}{Q} \right)$ | bits |
| 10 | % Resolution | $\% = \dfrac{1}{2^{n}} \times 100$ | % |

### 2.5.1 Worked Numerical — 4–20 mA Signal Interpretation

A level transmitter has a range of **0 to 10 meters** and outputs **4–20 mA**. The PLC measures **12 mA**. Find the level.

Using the standard formula:

$$
PV = PV_{min} + \frac{I - 4}{16}(PV_{max} - PV_{min})
$$

$$
PV = 0 + \frac{12 - 4}{16}(10 - 0)
$$

$$
PV = \frac{8}{16} \times 10 = 5 \, \text{meters}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The tank is **50% full**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | PLC Module Application |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Assembly** | Robotic cell control, conveyor sequencing, weld timers |
| **Water Treatment** | Pump alternation, level/flow PID, chemical dosing |
| **Power Plants** | Turbine governing, boiler control, grid synchronization |
| **Oil & Gas** | ESD systems, wellhead control, custody transfer |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Batch reactors, CIP (clean-in-place) sequencing |
| **Food & Beverage** | Bottle filling, pasteurizer control, recipe management |
| **Cement & Steel** | Kiln control, rolling mill drives, raw mill blending |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — ADC Resolution and Quantization Error

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A PLC analog input module has a full-scale range of **0 to 10 V DC** with a **14-bit ADC**. Compute:
1. The **quantization step (LSB size)**
2. The **% resolution**
3. The **quantization error** as both voltage and as % of span

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**

$$
V_{FS} = 10 \text{ V}, \quad n = 14 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute number of discrete codes.**

$$
N_{codes} = 2^{n} = 2^{14} = 16384
$$

**Step 3 — Compute quantization step (size of one LSB).**

$$
Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10}{16384}
$$

$$
Q \approx 6.104 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 0.6104 \text{ mV / LSB}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage resolution.**

$$
\%_{res} = \frac{1}{2^{n}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{16384} \times 100
$$

$$
\%_{res} \approx 6.10 \times 10^{-3} \, \% = 0.00610 \, \%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute maximum quantization error.**

The maximum quantization error is **±½ LSB** because any analog value falls within half an LSB above or below its nearest digital code:

$$
\epsilon_{max} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm 0.3052 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 6 — Express error as % of full scale.**

$$
\%_{\epsilon} = \pm \frac{0.3052 \text{ mV}}{10000 \text{ mV}} \times 100 = \pm 0.00305 \, \%
$$

### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix — Resolution Comparison

| ADC Bits | Codes (2ⁿ) | LSB Size (10 V FS) | % Resolution | % Max Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **8** | 256 | 39.06 mV | 0.391 % | ±0.195 % |
| **10** | 1024 | 9.77 mV | 0.0977 % | ±0.0488 % |
| **12** | 4096 | 2.44 mV | 0.0244 % | ±0.0122 % |
| **14** | 16384 | 0.610 mV | 0.0061 % | ±0.0031 % |
| **16** | 65536 | 0.153 mV | 0.00153 % | ±0.00076 % |
| **24** (Σ-Δ) | 16,777,216 | 0.596 µV | 5.96 × 10⁻⁶ % | ±2.98 × 10⁻⁶ % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** Doubling the number of ADC bits **doubles resolution** but also increases cost and noise sensitivity. For industrial analog inputs, **12 to 16 bits** is the engineering sweet spot.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — 4–20 mA Loop Scaling for a Temperature Transmitter

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A thermocouple-based temperature transmitter (TC range **0–500 °C**) outputs a current proportional to temperature. The PLC has an analog input module with a **250 Ω** sense resistor. The measured current is **13.6 mA**. Find:

1. The **voltage** seen by the ADC
2. The **temperature** in °C
3. The **% of span**

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute voltage across sense resistor.**

$$
V = I \times R = 13.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \times 250 \, \Omega
$$

$$
V = 3.4 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert current to % of span.**

Using the standard 4–20 mA linearization:

$$
\% = \frac{I - 4}{16} \times 100 = \frac{13.6 - 4}{16} \times 100
$$

$$
\% = \frac{9.6}{16} \times 100 = 60 \, \%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the engineering temperature.**

$$
T = T_{min} + \frac{\%}{100} \times (T_{max} - T_{min})
$$

$$
T = 0 + 0.60 \times (500 - 0) = 300 \, ^{\circ} C
$$

### 3.2.4 Verification by Direct Linear Equation

Since 4 mA = 0 °C and 20 mA = 500 °C, the linear mapping is:

$$
T = \frac{I - 4}{20 - 4} \times 500 = \frac{9.6}{16} \times 500 = 300 \, ^{\circ} C \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Scan Time Estimation

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A PLC has:
- **256 digital inputs**, each scan: **5 µs**
- **256 digital outputs**, each scan: **3 µs**
- **Analog input scan**: **2 ms** total
- **Program execution**: **3.5 ms**
- **Communication overhead**: **1.2 ms**

Compute the **total scan time**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Input scan time.**

$$
T_{in} = 256 \times 5 \, \mu s = 1280 \, \mu s = 1.28 \, ms
$$

**Step 2 — Output scan time.**

$$
T_{out} = 256 \times 3 \, \mu s = 768 \, \mu s = 0.768 \, ms
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all phases.**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{program} + T_{out} + T_{comm} + T_{analog}
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 1.28 + 3.5 + 0.768 + 1.2 + 2.0
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 8.748 \, ms
$$

### 3.3.3 Interpretation

$$
f_{scan} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{8.748 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 114.3 \, \text{Hz}
$$

The PLC updates its outputs ~114 times per second, which is **more than adequate** for most discrete control applications (motor starters, valves).

> [!WARNING]
> For high-speed motion control (e.g., CNC, packaging), scan times < **1 ms** are required. This is achieved with **dedicated high-speed counter modules** or **interrupt-driven subroutines** that bypass the main scan.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Sinking/Sourcing Compatibility Check

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

A **PNP (sourcing) proximity sensor** must be connected to a **sinking DC input module** rated **24 V DC**. The sensor's output is **active high** (24 V when target detected).

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Verification

**Step 1 — Identify current direction.**

A **PNP sensor** sources current: current **flows out** of its output terminal to the load. A **sinking input module** allows current to **flow into** its terminal — completing the loop back to the sensor's common (0 V).

**Step 2 — Circuit check.**

$$
\text{Sensor Output (+24 V)} \longrightarrow \text{PLC Input Terminal} \longrightarrow \text{Input Optocoupler LED} \longrightarrow \text{Sensor Common (0 V)}
$$

This forms a **closed loop** when the sensor is ON. ✅ **Compatible**.

**Step 3 — If sensor were NPN (sinking) instead.**

An NPN sensor sinks current. Connecting to a sinking input would attempt to **draw current to 0 V** from a sensor already at 0 V — **no voltage difference, no current, no operation**. ❌ **Incompatible**.

### 3.4.3 Compatibility Matrix

| Sensor Type | Sinking Input | Sourcing Input |
|---|---|---|
| **PNP (Sourcing)** | ✅ Works | ❌ No current flow |
| **NPN (Sinking)** | ❌ No current flow | ✅ Works |

> [!TIP]
> **Easy memory trick:** "Same first letter, never works."
> PNP + P-input ✗ | NPN + N-input ✗
> PNP + N-input ✓ | NPN + P-input ✓

---

## 3.5 Engineering Decision Matrix — Module Selection

| Selection Criterion | Relay Output | Transistor Output | Triac Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switching Speed** | Slow (~10 ms) | Fast (< 1 µs) | Medium (< 1 ms) |
| **AC Load Compatibility** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **DC Load Compatibility** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Leakage Current When OFF** | Zero | Small (µA) | ~1–2 mA |
| **Lifetime (Operations)** | ~10⁵ mechanical | >10⁹ solid-state | >10⁷ |
| **Cost per Point** | Higher | Lower | Medium |
| **Use Case** | Universal, low-cycle | High-speed DC | AC heating, lamps |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Modular PLC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fieldIn["FIELD INPUT DEVICES\nPushbuttons\nLimit Switches\nRTD\nThermocouple\nPressure Tx"] --> inpMod["INPUT MODULE\nOptocoupler\nFilter\nThreshold Detector"]
    inpMod --> bplane["BACKPLANE / FIELDBUS\nData + Address + Power"]
    bplane --> cpuMod["CPU MODULE\nMicroprocessor\nProgram Scan"]
    memMod["MEMORY MODULE\nROM / RAM / EEPROM"] <--> cpuMod
    psu["POWER SUPPLY MODULE\n230 VAC to 24 VDC\n5 VDC Logic Rails"] -.powers.-> bplane
    cpuMod --> bplane
    bplane --> outMod["OUTPUT MODULE\nDAC or Relay\nIsolation"]
    outMod --> fieldOut["FIELD OUTPUT DEVICES\nMotor Contactor\nSolenoid Valve\nVFD\nIndicator Lamp"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows show **logical signal flow** — actual current and data paths are bidirectional on the backplane.

---

## 4.2 Internal Block Diagram — Digital Input Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    term1["Terminal Block\nField Wiring +24 V / 0 V"] --> fbFilter["RC Filter\nEMI Suppression\nDebounce"]
    fbFilter --> schmitt["Schmitt Trigger\nThreshold Detector"]
    schmitt --> opto["Optocoupler\n2.5 kV Isolation"]
    opto --> ttl["Logic Level Translator\n24 V to 5 V"]
    ttl --> ledStat["Status LED"]
    ttl --> bplaneConn["Backplane Connector\nBus Interface"]
```

---

## 4.3 Internal Block Diagram — Digital Output Module (Transistor Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    bplaneIn["Backplane Interface\nDigital Latch"] --> driver["Driver Stage\nPre-amplifier"]
    driver --> mosfet["MOSFET Switch\nOpen Drain"]
    mosfet --> termOut["Output Terminal"]
    diode["Flyback Diode\nReverse Biased"] -.protects.-> mosfet
    termOut --> load["Inductive Load\nSolenoid or Relay Coil"]
    load --> ps["External 24 V DC Supply"]
```

---

## 4.4 Internal Block Diagram — Analog Input Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    aiTerm["Analog Input Terminal\nMulti-channel 4 to 20 mA"] --> mux["Analog Multiplexer\n1 ADC shared"]
    mux --> ia["Instrumentation Amplifier\nHigh CMRR"]
    ia --> filter["Anti-Aliasing Filter\nLow-Pass"]
    filter --> adc["16 bit ADC\nSigma Delta"]
    adc --> iso["Digital Isolation\nOptical or Transformer"]
    iso --> bplaneA["Backplane Bus"]
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Distributed I/O System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuRack["Main PLC CPU\nControl Cabinet"] -->|EtherNet/IP\n100 Mbps| sw1["Network Switch\nManaged Industrial"]
    sw1 --> remoteA["Remote I/O Island 1\nDigital Inputs 16 ch"]
    sw1 --> remoteB["Remote I/O Island 2\nAnalog Inputs 8 ch"]
    sw1 --> remoteC["Remote I/O Island 3\nDigital Outputs 16 ch"]
    sw1 --> hmi["HMI Panel\nTouchscreen"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Distributed I/O reduces long analog/digital signal cable runs, which are susceptible to noise and voltage drop. Each remote island can be located **hundreds of meters** from the CPU using a single Ethernet cable.

---

## 4.6 Signal Flow Diagram — 4–20 mA Current Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pSensor["Process Sensor\nTemperature or Pressure"] --> tx["Transmitter\nConverts PV to 4 to 20 mA"]
    tx --> wire["2 Wire Shielded Cable\nHazardous Area Approved"]
    wire --> senseR["Sense Resistor\n250 Ohm"]
    senseR --> adcIn["ADC Input\n0 to 5 V Equivalent"]
    adcIn --> plc["PLC CPU\nScaling Block"]
    plc --> display["HMI Display\nEngineering Units"]
```

---

## 4.7 PLC Scan Cycle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Power ON or Cycle Start"] --> selfTest["Self Diagnostic\nMemory Check\nI/O Check"]
    selfTest --> inputScan["INPUT SCAN\nRead all inputs to PII"]
    inputScan --> programExec["PROGRAM EXECUTION\nLadder FBD ST Logic\nRead PII Write PIO"]
    programExec --> outputScan["OUTPUT SCAN\nWrite PIO to physical outputs"]
    outputScan --> commService["COMMUNICATION SERVICE\nHMI Fieldbus Messaging"]
    commService --> housekeep["HOUSEKEEPING\nTimer Update\nCounter Update\nWatchdog"]
    housekeep -.loops back.-> inputScan
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** List and briefly explain any three major modules of a PLC.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A PLC consists of the following major modules:

1. **CPU Module (1 mark):** Contains the microprocessor that executes the user program in a cyclic manner — reading inputs, processing logic, and updating outputs. It controls all module operations and handles communication.

2. **Power Supply Module (1 mark):** Converts the AC mains supply (typically 230 V AC or 120 V AC) into regulated DC voltages (e.g., +5 V DC for logic, +24 V DC for I/O and sensors). Provides clean, isolated power to all backplane modules.

3. **Input Module (1 mark):** Interfaces field input devices such as pushbuttons, limit switches, and sensors to the CPU. Provides electrical isolation (via optocouplers), signal conditioning, and noise filtering. Accepts discrete or analog signals.

> [!TIP]
> *Memory Module and Output Module are also acceptable as the third module.*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** With a neat sketch, explain the working of an optocoupler used in PLC input modules.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **optocoupler (opto-isolator)** provides **galvanic isolation** between the high-voltage field input circuit and the low-voltage CPU logic circuit.

**Construction (1 mark):**
- An **infrared LED** on the input (primary) side
- A **phototransistor** or phototriac on the output (secondary) side
- Both encapsulated in a single package with no electrical connection between them — only **optical coupling** through a transparent insulator

**Working (1 mark):**
- When the field input is energized (e.g., 24 V applied), the LED emits IR light.
- The IR light falls on the phototransistor base, causing it to conduct.
- This produces a corresponding low-voltage logic signal (5 V) on the CPU side.
- **Isolation voltage:** typically **2.5 kV to 5 kV** — protects the CPU from field-side transients.

**Signal diagram (1 mark):**

```
Field side:    24 V ---->|>|----[ LED ]---- 0 V
                              |   (IR light)
Logic side:    +5 V --[ R ]--|<--- Photo-Tx --- 0 V
                          Output to CPU
```

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style, 14 Marks]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a modular PLC. List the function of each module. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the construction and working of a digital **DC input module**, including the concepts of **sinking and sourcing**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Modular PLC Architecture (7 marks):**

A PLC consists of five functionally distinct modules interconnected via a backplane.

| Module | Function (mark distribution) |
|---|---|
| **Power Supply (1 mark)** | Converts mains AC to regulated DC (5 V, 24 V) for internal electronics and sensor power. |
| **CPU (2 marks)** | Hosts the microprocessor; executes the scan cycle (Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping); manages communication. |
| **Memory (1 mark)** | Stores system firmware (ROM), user program (RAM/EEPROM), and live data (RAM, process image tables). |
| **Input Module (1.5 marks)** | Receives signals from field sensors; provides isolation, debounce, threshold detection; converts to logic-level. |
| **Output Module (1.5 marks)** | Drives field actuators based on CPU decisions; uses relays, transistors, or triacs; provides isolation and protection. |

**Block diagram (drawn in exam):** (Refer to Section 4.1 above — reproduce the modular PLC block diagram showing CPU at the center, surrounded by Power Supply, Memory, Input, Output, all interconnected via the backplane.)

---

**(b) DC Digital Input Module (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):**

A typical DC input module has the following internal stages (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):

1. **Terminal Block** — accepts field wires
2. **RC Filter / Debounce Circuit** — eliminates contact bounce and EMI
3. **Schmitt Trigger** — provides clean threshold detection with hysteresis
4. **Optocoupler** — galvanic isolation (~2.5 kV)
5. **Logic-level translator** — converts to 5 V TTL for the backplane
6. **Status LED** — visual indication of input state

**Working (2.5 marks):**
- When a field device (e.g., PNP sensor) closes, +24 V is applied to the input terminal.
- The RC filter smooths out any contact bounce or high-frequency noise.
- The Schmitt trigger compares the voltage against a preset threshold (typically > 15 V = ON; < 5 V = OFF), producing a clean square wave.
- The optocoupler LED turns ON, the phototransistor conducts, and the resulting signal is sent to the CPU via the backplane.

**Sinking vs Sourcing (2.5 marks):**

Refer to Section 2.2.2 and 3.4 above for full theoretical discussion. Key points to include:

- **Sinking Input** — current flows **into** the PLC input terminal; requires a **PNP sourcing sensor**
- **Sourcing Input** — current flows **out of** the PLC input terminal; requires an **NPN sinking sensor**
- **Compatibility rule** — "Same first letter, never works" (mismatch breaks the current loop)
- **Example diagram** — show the current flow arrows in each configuration

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout (Examiner's Warning):**
> 1. Students often confuse "sinking input" with "sinking current" — the **PLC terminal** sinks, but the **external sensor** must source.
> 2. In exam answers, **always draw both configurations** with explicit current arrows.
> 3. Mentioning the **optocoupler isolation voltage (~2.5 kV)** earns an extra valuation point.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the **PLC scan cycle** with a flowchart. Compute the **total scan time** for a PLC with: 128 DI × 3 µs, 128 DO × 2 µs, 8 AI × 250 µs, program execution 2.8 ms, and communication overhead 0.9 ms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **4–20 mA analog input module**. A pressure transmitter has range **0–10 bar** and outputs 4–20 mA. The PLC reads **14.4 mA**. Find the pressure and the percentage of span. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) PLC Scan Cycle (7 marks):**

**Flowchart (4 marks):** (Reproduce the scan cycle flowchart from Section 4.7.)

**Phases:**
1. **Self-Diagnostic** — Memory, I/O, communication checks
2. **Input Scan** — Read all physical inputs into Process Image Input (PII)
3. **Program Execution** — Execute ladder/FBD/ST using PII, write results to PIO
4. **Output Scan** — Write PIO to physical outputs
5. **Communication Service** — Handle HMI, fieldbus messages
6. **Housekeeping** — Update timers, counters, watchdog

**Scan Time Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
T_{DI} = 128 \times 3 \, \mu s = 384 \, \mu s = 0.384 \, ms
$$

$$
T_{DO} = 128 \times 2 \, \mu s = 256 \, \mu s = 0.256 \, ms
$$

$$
T_{AI} = 8 \times 250 \, \mu s = 2000 \, \mu s = 2.000 \, ms
$$

$$
T_{scan} = T_{DI} + T_{program} + T_{DO} + T_{AI} + T_{comm}
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 0.384 + 2.8 + 0.256 + 2.0 + 0.9
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 6.34 \, ms
$$

$$
f_{scan} = \frac{1}{6.34 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 157.7 \, Hz
$$

**Conclusion:** ~158 updates per second — suitable for most industrial control loops.

---

**(b) 4–20 mA Analog Input Module (7 marks):**

**Working (4 marks):**

1. **Field transmitter** senses process variable (e.g., pressure) and converts it linearly to a **4–20 mA** current signal.
2. The current flows through a **250 Ω precision sense resistor** in the analog input module, converting it to **1–5 V** voltage.
3. **Instrumentation amplifier** boosts this small differential signal with high **CMRR**.
4. **Anti-aliasing low-pass filter** removes frequencies above half the sampling rate (Nyquist).
5. **16-bit (or 12-bit) ADC** digitizes the voltage.
6. The CPU's **scaling function block** linearizes the digital count back into engineering units.
7. **Optical isolation** separates the field circuit from the CPU.

**Block diagram:** (Reproduce Section 4.6.)

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given: Range **0–10 bar**, **I = 14.4 mA**

$$
\% = \frac{I - 4}{16} \times 100 = \frac{14.4 - 4}{16} \times 100 = \frac{10.4}{16} \times 100
$$

$$
\% = 65 \, \% \text{ of span}
$$

$$
P = 0 + 0.65 \times (10 - 0) = 6.5 \, \text{bar}
$$

**Result:** The process pressure is **6.5 bar**, representing **65 % of full span**.

---

## Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in PLC Modules Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing sinking/sourcing direction** — always specify whether the **PLC terminal** or the **sensor** is sinking/sourcing. (Lose 1–2 marks per occurrence.)
> 2. **Forgetting galvanic isolation** — most input/output modules are described in textbooks with isolation; omitting it loses a key concept mark.
> 3. **Scan cycle skipped or described vaguely** — must list all 4–5 phases in correct order; KTU expects explicit naming.
> 4. **Numerical errors in ADC quantization** — using wrong $V_{FS}$ (full-scale voltage, not peak-to-peak) is a classic trap.
> 5. **No units in 4–20 mA problems** — write **mA**, **V**, **°C**, **bar** explicitly.
> 6. **Not drawing a diagram** — KTU valuation policy gives **1–2 marks** for neat labeled diagrams. Always include at least one block diagram.
> 7. **Mixing up triac vs transistor vs relay** — know which output type works with AC vs DC loads.

---

## Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ A PLC has **5 modules**: CPU, Memory, Power Supply, Input, Output (sometimes Communication).
- ☐ **Power Supply** converts AC mains to **+5 V DC** (logic) and **+24 V DC** (I/O/sensors).
- ☐ **CPU** runs a **cyclic scan**: Input Scan → Program Execute → Output Scan → Housekeeping.
- ☐ **Process Image Input (PII)** and **Process Image Output (PIO)** ensure deterministic behavior.
- ☐ **Input Module** stages: Terminal → RC filter → Schmitt trigger → Optocoupler → Logic level → Backplane.
- ☐ **Optocoupler isolation voltage:** typically **2.5–5 kV**.
- ☐ **Sinking input** ↔ **PNP (sourcing) sensor** ✅; **Sourcing input** ↔ **NPN (sinking) sensor** ✅.
- ☐ **ADC quantization:** $Q = V_{FS} / 2^{n}$; max error = **±½ LSB**.
- ☐ **4–20 mA loop:** 4 mA = 0 %, 20 mA = 100 %; % = (I − 4)/16 × 100.
- ☐ **Sense resistor 250 Ω** converts 4–20 mA to **1–5 V** for the ADC.
- ☐ **Output types:** Relay (universal, slow), Transistor (DC, fast), Triac (AC, medium).
- ☐ **Flyback diode** is mandatory across inductive DC loads.
- ☐ **Distributed I/O** uses fieldbus (PROFIBUS, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP) to place I/O modules remotely.
- ☐ **Scan time** formula: $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{program} + T_{out} + T_{comm} + T_{housekeeping}$.
- ☐ **Resolution comparison:** 12-bit ADC → 0.024 %; 16-bit ADC → 0.0015 %.
- ☐ Industrial typical scan rate: **50–500 Hz**; high-speed applications need dedicated fast modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital and Analog Input/Output: Examples of Digital and Analog Inputs/Outputs in PLC, AC-DC Power Supplies in PLC, Isolators, PLC Configuration, Scan Cycle, Capabilities of PLC, Selection Criteria for PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital and Analog Input/Output: Examples of Digital and Analog Inputs/Outputs in PLC, AC-DC Power Supplies in PLC, Isolators, PLC Configuration, Scan Cycle, Capabilities of PLC, Selection Criteria for PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Digital and Analog I/O — Foundational Definitions

### Digital Inputs/Outputs (Discrete I/O)
> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Digital I/O signals are **two-state (binary)** electrical signals that exist in only **two logical conditions** — either **ON (Logic 1 / TRUE)** or **OFF (Logic 0 / FALSE)**. These are also termed *discrete signals* because they vary in *discrete steps*, not continuously.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a **regular wall light switch** in your home. The switch is either UP (ON, light glows) or DOWN (OFF, light is dark). There is no in-between. A digital input to a PLC is exactly like that — a sensor tells the PLC "the door is open" (1) or "the door is closed" (0), with no intermediate state.

**Standard Voltage Levels in Industrial PLCs:**
- **DC Inputs:** Typically **24 V DC** (Logic 1 ≈ 15–30 V; Logic 0 ≈ 0–5 V)
- **AC Inputs:** Typically **120 V AC** or **230 V AC** (50/60 Hz)
- **Relay/Transistor Outputs:** **24 V DC** or **230 V AC** switching

### Analog Inputs/Outputs (Continuous I/O)
> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Analog I/O signals are **continuously variable** electrical signals whose magnitude is **directly proportional** to the physical parameter being measured or controlled. The signal can take any value within a defined operating range, producing an **infinite set of possible values** between two limits.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine the **volume knob on a music system** — you can turn it smoothly to any position from 0 to 10. It is not just "loud" or "silent" but can be at 3.7, 5.2, 8.1, etc. An analog input works the same way: a temperature sensor doesn't just say "HOT/COLD" — it returns a precise voltage representing the exact temperature, like 23.5 °C.

**Standard Industrial Analog Signal Ranges:**
- **Voltage Signals:** **0–10 V DC**, **±10 V DC**, **0–5 V DC**
- **Current Signals:** **4–20 mA** (industry standard), **0–20 mA**, **0–10 mA**
- **Resolution:** Typically **12-bit (4096 steps)**, **14-bit (16384 steps)**, or **16-bit (65536 steps)** ADC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The **4–20 mA current loop** is the **dominant industrial standard** for analog transmission because it is **noise-immune** (current signals don't drop over long cable runs) and the **"live zero" at 4 mA** distinguishes a true zero reading from a broken wire (which would read 0 mA).

---

## 1.2 Real-World Examples of Digital & Analog I/O

### Table 1.1 — Examples Classified by Type

| **Type** | **Examples of Physical Devices** | **Signal Form** |
|---|---|---|
| Digital Input | Pushbutton, Limit Switch, Proximity Sensor, Photoelectric Sensor, Pressure Switch, Float Switch, Selector Switch | ON/OFF (1 bit) |
| Digital Output | Contactor Coil, Solenoid Valve, Indicator Lamp, Buzzer, Heater Element, Motor Starter, Relay | ON/OFF (1 bit) |
| Analog Input | RTD / Thermocouple (temperature), Pressure Transducer, Flow Meter, Level Sensor, pH Probe, Load Cell, LVDT, Humidity Sensor | Continuous V or mA |
| Analog Output | Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) speed ref., Control Valve Positioner, Chart Recorder, Variable Damper Actuator, Analog Meter | Continuous V or mA |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to give examples, always mention the **specific signal standard**. For instance, instead of just "temperature sensor," write "**RTD (PT100) connected via 4–20 mA transmitter to analog input module**." This shows the examiner you understand the *signal conditioning chain*.

---

## 1.3 AC-DC Power Supplies in PLC

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A PLC power supply converts the **mains AC voltage** (typically **230 V AC, 50 Hz** in India) into the **low-voltage DC levels** required by the PLC's internal logic, processors, and I/O modules. It also provides **isolation and regulation** to protect sensitive electronics.

**Power Supply Architecture (Block-Level Intuition):**

Think of the PLC power supply as a **refinery that converts crude oil (mains AC) into usable fuel (clean DC)**. The "crude" 230 V AC is stepped down, rectified, filtered, and regulated into multiple clean DC rails.

**Typical DC Output Rails:**
- **+5 V DC** → CPU, memory, logic ICs
- **+24 V DC** → Discrete I/O modules, sensor power
- **±15 V DC** or **±12 V DC** → Analog modules, op-amps
- **+3.3 V DC** → Modern low-power processors

**Types of PLC Power Supplies:**
1. **Integral (Built-in)** — Small/medium PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7-200, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix)
2. **Modular (Slot-mounted)** — Large PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7-1500, AB ControlLogix)
3. **Redundant (1+1 or N+1)** — Critical process industries

---

## 1.4 Isolators in PLC Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** An **isolator** (or *galvanic isolator*) is a device that provides **complete electrical separation** between two circuits while still allowing signal transfer. There is **no metallic (conductive) path** between input and output — energy is transferred via **optical, magnetic, or capacitive coupling**.

**Why is Isolation Needed?**
- Protects the **CPU from damaging voltages** (lightning, transients, ground loops)
- Prevents **noise coupling** from field devices into logic circuits
- Allows **different grounds** between field side and logic side
- Mandatory for **safety** per IEC 61131-2 and IEC 61010

**Common Isolation Techniques:**
1. **Optical Isolation (Optocoupler):** Light-emitting diode + phototransistor across an insulating gap
2. **Transformer Isolation:** Magnetic flux through an insulating barrier
3. **Capacitive Isolation:** High-frequency signal across a capacitor dielectric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isolation Voltage Ratings (Industrial Standard):**
> - **Standard:** **1500 V AC RMS** for 1 minute
> - **Reinforced (Safety):** **3000 V AC RMS** or higher
> - Always verify per **IEC 61131-2** for PLC I/O modules.

---

## 1.5 PLC Configuration, Scan Cycle, Capabilities & Selection — Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **PLC Configuration:** The *physical and logical arrangement* of the CPU, power supply, input modules, output modules, communication modules, and racks/backplanes that make up a complete PLC system.

**Scan Cycle:** The **repetitive, sequential operation** the CPU performs continuously: **(1) Read Inputs → (2) Execute Logic → (3) Update Outputs → (4) Housekeeping**.

**Capabilities of PLC:** The **functional envelope** of what a PLC can do — including I/O count, scan time, memory size, instruction set, communication protocols, and special functions.

**Selection Criteria:** The **engineering methodology** for choosing the right PLC for an application, considering I/O count, type, environment, expandability, programming, and cost.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy for the full topic:** A **PLC is like a digital brain inside a factory machine**. Its *eyes and ears* are the **inputs** (sensors), its *hands and mouth* are the **outputs** (actuators), the *heart and circulatory system* is the **power supply**, the *immune system* is **isolation**, the *body layout* is the **configuration**, the *breathing rhythm* is the **scan cycle**, the *skills* are its **capabilities**, and the *resume that lands the job* is built using proper **selection criteria**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive — Digital I/O Architecture

### 2.1.1 Digital Input Module Internals

A digital input module performs **three sequential functions** for every input channel:

1. **Signal Conditioning** — Voltage scaling, debouncing
2. **Isolation** — Optocoupler or transformer-based
3. **Indication & Latching** — LED status + buffer register

**Operational Logic for a 24 V DC Sourcing (PNP) Input:**

$$
V_{in} \geq V_{TH} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Logic} = 1 \text{ (ON)}
$$

$$
V_{in} < V_{TH} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Logic} = 0 \text{ (OFF)}
$$

where $V_{TH}$ is the threshold voltage (typically **15 V DC** for 24 V inputs per IEC 61131-2).

### 2.1.2 Digital Output Module Internals

Three switching technologies are used:

| **Output Type** | **Switching Device** | **Switching Speed** | **Leakage Current** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Relay (Electromechanical)** | SPDT / DPDT contacts | 10 ms (mechanical) | Zero (open contact) | AC/DC universal, high inrush |
| **Transistor (BJT/MOSFET)** | Semiconductor | < 1 µs (electronic) | < 1 mA | High-speed DC switching |
| **TRIAC** | Bidirectional thyristor | < 10 ms (zero-cross) | < 5 mA | AC loads only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "4 mA live zero" matters:** In a 4–20 mA loop, the *minimum* operational current is 4 mA, not 0 mA. This means:
> - **0 mA** = **broken wire / sensor failure** (diagnostic alarm)
> - **4 mA** = **live zero** (legitimate 0% reading)
> - **20 mA** = **100%** of the calibrated range
> - **> 20 mA** = **over-range or fault**

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Analog I/O Architecture

### 2.2.1 Analog Input Signal Chain

The **complete signal path** for an analog input is:

> **Sensor → Transducer → Signal Conditioner → Multiplexer → ADC → Isolation → CPU Memory**

Each stage has a defined role:

- **Multiplexer (MUX):** Allows **one ADC** to serve **multiple channels** (e.g., 8 or 16 channels) by time-sharing — reduces cost.
- **ADC Resolution:** $N$ bits → $2^N$ discrete codes.
- **Sampling Rate:** Measured in **samples per second (SPS)** or **Hz**.

### 2.2.2 Key Analog Performance Metrics

| **Metric** | **Symbol** | **Definition** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution | $N$ | Number of bits in ADC | bits |
| Full-Scale Range | $FS$ | Max − Min input range | V or mA |
| LSB (Least Significant Bit) | $LSB$ | Smallest detectable change | V or mA |
| Sampling Rate | $f_s$ | Conversion speed | SPS |
| Accuracy | — | Closeness to true value | % of FS |
| Linearity | — | Deviation from ideal straight line | % of FS |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $CMRR$ | Ability to reject common-mode noise | dB |

---

## 2.3 Power Supply — Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 Power Supply Block Diagram (Functional Stages)

**Stage 1 — Input Protection & EMI Filter:**
- **MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor)** for surge protection
- **X/Y Capacitors** for EMI filtering
- **Inrush Current Limiter (NTC thermistor)**

**Stage 2 — Rectification:**
- **Bridge Rectifier** converts AC to pulsating DC
- Output: $V_{DC,pk} = V_{AC,RMS} \times \sqrt{2}$

**Stage 3 — Filtering:**
- **Bulk Capacitor** smooths the pulsating DC
- Ripple voltage is reduced: $V_{ripple} \approx \dfrac{I_{load}}{f \times C}$

**Stage 4 — Regulation:**
- **Linear Regulator (e.g., LM7805):** Simple, low noise, inefficient
- **SMPS (Switch-Mode Power Supply):** High efficiency (>85%), uses PWM at high frequency (20–500 kHz)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Point — SMPS vs Linear:** SMPS is **preferred in modern PLCs** because of:
> - Higher efficiency (less heat, smaller size)
> - Wide input voltage tolerance
> - Better hold-up time during mains dips
> However, it requires **EMI filtering** because of high-frequency switching.

### 2.3.2 Hold-Up Time & Battery Backup

**Hold-up time** is the duration the PLC continues to operate after mains power loss. A typical requirement is **20–30 ms** to ride through brief mains sags.

For **battery-backed SRAM / RTC (Real-Time Clock)**:

$$
t_{hold} \approx \frac{C_{bulk} \times (V_{initial}^2 - V_{min}^2)}{2 \times P_{load}}
$$

---

## 2.4 Isolation — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 Optocoupler Operation

An optocoupler (e.g., **PC817, 6N137**) consists of:
- **Input side:** Infrared LED
- **Output side:** Phototransistor (or photodarlington, phototriac)
- **Insulating gap:** Typically **0.4 mm to 4 mm**

**Current Transfer Ratio (CTR):**

$$
CTR = \frac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100\%
$$

where $I_F$ = LED forward current, $I_C$ = collector current. Typical CTR = **20% to 600%** depending on type.

### 2.4.2 Why Isolation Is Mandatory in PLCs

| **Hazard** | **Without Isolation** | **With Isolation** |
|---|---|---|
| Ground Loop | Erroneous readings, signal drift | Eliminated |
| Transient Surge | CPU damage, module failure | CPU protected |
| Common-Mode Voltage | ADC saturation, component damage | Rejected |
| Noise Coupling | Unreliable I/O | Clean signal |

---

## 2.5 PLC Configuration — System Architecture

### 2.5.1 Physical Configurations

1. **Compact (Brick) PLC:**
   - All components in one unit
   - Fixed I/O count (e.g., 20 I/O)
   - Examples: Siemens LOGO!, AB MicroLogix 1100, Delta DVP-SS2
   - **Cannot be expanded** (or limited expansion)

2. **Modular (Rack-based) PLC:**
   - Separate modules for CPU, PS, I/O, Communication
   - Plugged into a **backplane** or **rack**
   - Highly expandable (up to thousands of I/O)
   - Examples: Siemens S7-1500, AB ControlLogix, Schneider M580

3. **Distributed I/O:**
   - CPU at central location, I/O stations distributed in field
   - Connected via **fieldbus** (PROFIBUS, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP)
   - Reduces cabling cost, enables modular plant design

### 2.5.2 Logical Configuration

A PLC is logically divided into:

- **Process Image (PI):** Memory-mapped area where I/O states are stored
- **System Memory (SM):** Internal flags, timers, counters, special bits
- **User Memory:** Holds the ladder/FBD/ST program
- **Retentive Memory:** Battery-backed; survives power cycle

---

## 2.6 PLC Scan Cycle — Detailed Theory

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (IEC 61131-1):** The *program scan* is the **continuous, cyclic execution** by the CPU of the following phases: **Input Scan → Program Execution → Output Scan → Housekeeping**.

### 2.6.1 The Four Phases

**Phase 1 — Input Scan (Read Phase):**
- CPU reads the **physical state** of all input modules
- Stores values into the **Process Image Input (PII)** memory
- Duration: typically **1–10 ms** depending on I/O count and bus type

**Phase 2 — Program Execution (Logic Solve):**
- CPU executes the user program **sequentially**
- For ladder logic, it scans **rung by rung, top to bottom, left to right**
- Uses **current values** from the PII (not the live inputs)
- Writes output decisions to the **Process Image Output (PIO)**

**Phase 3 — Output Scan (Write Phase):**
- CPU writes PIO values to the **physical output modules**
- Outputs are latched until the next scan

**Phase 4 — Housekeeping (Overhead):**
- Communication processing (HMI, peer-to-peer)
- Diagnostics, error handling
- Self-tests, watchdog reset
- Memory management

### 2.6.2 Scan Time Calculation

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}
$$

Where:
- $T_{in}$ = Input scan time (depends on number of inputs and bus protocol)
- $T_{prog}$ = Program execution time (depends on number/type of instructions)
- $T_{out}$ = Output scan time
- $T_{house}$ = Housekeeping time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Watchdog Timer:** The PLC has an internal **watchdog timer** (typically **100–500 ms**). If the scan exceeds this limit, the CPU faults and all outputs are forced OFF (fail-safe).

---

## 2.7 Capabilities of PLC

A modern PLC is capable of:

1. **Discrete I/O Handling** — Thousands of digital I/O
2. **Analog I/O Handling** — High-resolution ADC/DAC
