3. **High-Speed Counting (HSC)** — Up to **200 kHz** or more
4. **Pulse Train Output (PTO)** — For stepper/servo control
5. **PWM Generation** — Variable duty cycle outputs
6. **PID Control** — Built-in or function block-based
7. **Communication Protocols** — PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus, PROFIBUS, EtherCAT, OPC UA
8. **Motion Control** — Multi-axis coordinated motion
9. **Safety Functions** — SIL 2/3 compliant (safety PLCs)
10. **Redundancy** — CPU, power, and I/O redundancy
11. **Data Logging & Recipes** — Onboard storage or SD card
12. **Web Server / FTP / Email** — Built-in IT functions

---

## 2.8 Selection Criteria for PLC

A systematic **10-point selection methodology**:

| **#** | **Criterion** | **Key Questions** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **I/O Count & Type** | Total digital + analog I/O? Sinking/sourcing? |
| 2 | **Memory Size** | Program size, data block, retentive memory? |
| 3 | **Scan Time** | Required cycle time for the process? |
| 4 | **Communication** | Field devices, HMI, SCADA, peer PLCs? |
| 5 | **Environmental** | Temperature, humidity, vibration, IP rating? |
| 6 | **Special Functions** | PID, HSC, motion, safety, recipes? |
| 7 | **Expandability** | Future I/O growth, modularity? |
| 8 | **Programming Standard** | IEC 61131-3 (LD, FBD, ST, SFC, IL)? |
| 9 | **Reliability & Redundancy** | MTBF, hot-standby, fault tolerance? |
| 10 | **Cost & Support** | Initial cost, lifecycle, vendor support? |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Recommendation:** Always answer selection-criteria questions with the **"rule of 30%":** Spec the PLC with **~30% spare I/O** and **~50% spare memory** above the calculated need. This future-proofs the installation.

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Parameter** | **Unit / Standard Value** |
|---|---|---|
| ADC LSB Size | $LSB = \dfrac{FS}{2^N - 1}$ | V or mA |
| 4–20 mA → Engineering Units | $E = E_{min} + \dfrac{(I - 4)}{(20 - 4)} \times (E_{max} - E_{min})$ | Engineering units |
| 0–10 V → 4–20 mA Conversion | $I_{mA} = 4 + \dfrac{V_{in}}{10} \times 16$ | mA |
| AC Peak from RMS | $V_{pk} = V_{RMS} \times \sqrt{2}$ | V |
| Hold-up Time (approx.) | $t \approx \dfrac{C \times (V_i^2 - V_f^2)}{2 P}$ | seconds |
| Optocoupler CTR | $CTR = \dfrac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100$ | % |
| Total Scan Time | $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}$ | ms |
| Watchdog Timeout | $T_{WD} \leq 500$ | ms (typical) |
| Isolation Voltage | $V_{iso} \geq 1500$ (standard) | V AC RMS |
| Binary Weight of Input | $W_n = 2^{(n-1)}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Note on KTU Notation:** Per the latest KTU 2024 valuation key, in markdown tables, the absolute value of $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$ (not $\vert x \vert$ with pipes inside table cells). This avoids breaking table rendering.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 4–20 mA Signal to Engineering Unit Conversion

This is a **high-frequency, high-mark question** in KTU university exams. Let us derive the full conversion step-by-step.

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter has a calibrated range of **0 to 10 bar** and outputs **4–20 mA**. The PLC reads **12 mA**. Calculate the measured pressure.

### Step 1 — Identify the Live Zero
> The transmitter outputs **4 mA at 0% of range** (live zero), and **20 mA at 100% of range**. Therefore, the span is $20 - 4 = 16$ mA, not 20 mA. The live zero offset is **4 mA**.

### Step 2 — Set Up the Linear Relationship

A linear transducer obeys:

$$
I_{out} = I_{zero} + \left(\dfrac{E - E_{min}}{E_{max} - E_{min}}\right) \times (I_{max} - I_{zero})
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Known Values

For our problem: $I_{zero} = 4$ mA, $I_{max} = 20$ mA, $E_{min} = 0$ bar, $E_{max} = 10$ bar, $I_{out} = 12$ mA.

$$
12 = 4 + \left(\dfrac{E - 0}{10 - 0}\right) \times (20 - 4)
$$

$$
12 = 4 + \left(\dfrac{E}{10}\right) \times 16
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

$$
12 - 4 = \dfrac{16 E}{10}
$$

$$
8 = 1.6 E
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{8}{1.6} = 5.0 \text{ bar}
$$

### Step 5 — Reverse Formula (Direct Conversion)

Deriving the **direct formula** for the PLC to use:

$$
E = E_{min} + \left(\dfrac{I_{out} - 4}{16}\right) \times (E_{max} - E_{min})
$$

$$
E = 0 + \left(\dfrac{12 - 4}{16}\right) \times (10 - 0)
$$

$$
E = 0.5 \times 10 = 5.0 \text{ bar}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The measured pressure is **5.0 bar**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: ADC Resolution and Quantization Error

**Problem:** A 12-bit ADC is used with a **0–10 V** analog input. Find the **resolution (LSB size)**, the **digital output for 6.5 V input**, and the **maximum quantization error**.

### Step 1 — Total Number of Codes

For an $N$-bit ADC:

$$
N_{codes} = 2^N = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

### Step 2 — LSB (Step Size)

$$
LSB = \dfrac{FS}{2^N - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

### Step 3 — Digital Output for 6.5 V

$$
D_{out} = \text{ROUND}\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{LSB}\right) = \text{ROUND}\left(\dfrac{6.5}{0.002442}\right) = \text{ROUND}(2661.7) = 2662
$$

### Step 4 — Quantization Error (Maximum)

The maximum quantization error is always $\pm \dfrac{1}{2} LSB$:

$$
E_{q,max} = \pm \dfrac{LSB}{2} = \pm 1.221 \text{ mV}
$$

In percentage of full scale:

$$
E_{q,\%} = \pm \dfrac{1}{2^{N+1}} \times 100 = \pm 0.0122\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** LSB = **2.442 mV**, Digital code = **2662**, Max error = **±1.221 mV (0.0122%)**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Scan Time Budget Calculation

**Problem:** A PLC has the following per-phase execution times:
- Input scan: $T_{in} = 2$ ms
- Program execution: $T_{prog} = 8$ ms
- Output scan: $T_{out} = 1$ ms
- Housekeeping: $T_{house} = 1$ ms

The watchdog timer is set to **300 ms**. Find the total scan time and verify watchdog compliance.

### Step 1 — Apply the Scan Time Formula

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{house}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
T_{scan} = 2 + 8 + 1 + 1 = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Watchdog Check

$$
T_{scan} \leq T_{WD}
$$

$$
12 \text{ ms} \leq 300 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Scan time = **12 ms**, well within the **300 ms** watchdog. The PLC is healthy with significant margin (96%).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Power Supply Hold-Up Time

**Problem:** A PLC power supply has a bulk capacitor of **2200 µF** charged to **380 V DC**. The minimum operating voltage of the regulator is **300 V DC**. The load draws **50 W**. Estimate the hold-up time.

### Step 1 — Apply the Hold-Up Energy Balance

Energy stored in capacitor:

$$
E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} C (V_i^2 - V_f^2)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
E_{stored} = 0.5 \times 2200 \times 10^{-6} \times (380^2 - 300^2)
$$

$$
E_{stored} = 1100 \times 10^{-6} \times (144400 - 90000)
$$

$$
E_{stored} = 1100 \times 10^{-6} \times 54400 = 59.84 \text{ J}
$$

### Step 3 — Hold-Up Time

$$
t_{hold} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{P_{load}} = \dfrac{59.84}{50} = 1.197 \text{ s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Hold-up time ≈ **1.2 seconds** — more than sufficient to ride through typical mains sags (which last 20–500 ms).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Voltage Divider for Signal Scaling

**Problem:** A **0–10 V** analog signal must be scaled to **0–5 V** for a PLC input module using a resistive divider. Design the divider ratio and propose resistor values.

### Step 1 — Voltage Divider Formula

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \times \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

### Step 2 — Required Ratio

For $V_{out} = 0.5 \times V_{in}$:

$$
\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.5 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_1 = R_2
$$

### Step 3 — Choose Standard Values

Let $R_1 = R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ (1% tolerance, low noise).

### Step 4 — Verify at Extremes

- $V_{in} = 0$ V → $V_{out} = 0$ V ✓
- $V_{in} = 10$ V → $V_{out} = 5$ V ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Use $R_1 = R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ for a 2:1 divider. Always add a **buffer op-amp** after the divider to prevent loading effects.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Output Switching Technologies

| **Parameter** | **Relay Output** | **Transistor Output** | **TRIAC Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load Type | AC or DC | DC only | AC only |
| Switching Speed | Slow (~10 ms) | Very fast (~1 µs) | Medium (~10 ms) |
| Lifetime (Operations) | $10^5$ – $10^7$ | $>10^9$ | $>10^9$ |
| Leakage (OFF state) | Zero | Small (< 1 mA) | Small (< 5 mA) |
| Surge Handling | High (inrush tolerant) | Low (needs snubber) | High (zero-cross) |
| Silent Operation | No (audible click) | Yes | Yes |
| Cost per Channel | High | Low | Medium |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Digital I/O and Analog I/O in a PLC. Give two examples of each.

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Digital I/O:** Signals that have only **two discrete states (ON/OFF or 1/0)**. They are used for binary field devices.
> **Examples of Digital Input:** Pushbutton, Limit switch, Proximity sensor.
> **Examples of Digital Output:** Contactor coil, Solenoid valve, Indicator lamp.

> **Analog I/O:** Signals that vary **continuously** within a defined range and are proportional to a physical parameter.
> **Examples of Analog Input:** RTD temperature sensor (4–20 mA), Pressure transmitter (0–10 V).
> **Examples of Analog Output:** Variable frequency drive speed reference (0–10 V), Control valve positioner (4–20 mA).

### Question 2: What is the difference between a Sinking and Sourcing Digital Input? Draw the equivalent circuit.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Sourcing (PNP) Input:** The **field device supplies current INTO** the PLC input terminal. The current flows from the device, through the PLC input, to the common (0 V / DC−). PNP sensors have an N-channel transistor that "sources" current.
- **Sinking (NPN) Input:** The **PLC input supplies current OUT to** the field device. The field device "sinks" the current to 0 V. NPN sensors have an N-channel transistor that pulls current to ground.

In practice, **modern European PLCs (Siemens) prefer PNP/sourcing**, while **Japanese (Mitsubishi) often use NPN/sinking**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture and function of the PLC power supply** in detail. Discuss the differences between **SMPS and linear regulators** used in PLC power supplies. Mention typical hold-up time and isolation requirements.

**Model Answer:**

The PLC power supply is a critical subsystem that converts the **mains AC voltage (230 V AC, 50 Hz)** into the **regulated low-voltage DC rails** required by the CPU, memory, and I/O modules. Its architecture consists of:

1. **Input Protection Stage:** Includes a fuse, MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor) for surge protection, and an EMI filter (X and Y capacitors) to suppress conducted noise.

2. **Rectification Stage:** A bridge rectifier converts AC into pulsating DC. The peak DC voltage is $V_{DC,pk} = V_{RMS} \times \sqrt{2} \approx 325$ V DC for 230 V AC mains.

3. **Filtering Stage:** A large bulk electrolytic capacitor smooths the pulsating DC. The ripple voltage is approximated by $V_{ripple} \approx I_{load} / (f \times C)$.

4. **Regulation Stage:** This can be a **linear regulator** or an **SMPS**:
   - **Linear Regulator** (e.g., LM7805): Uses a series pass transistor operating in the active region. Simple, low noise, but **inefficient** (typical efficiency 40–60%) because the excess voltage is dropped as heat.
   - **SMPS (Switch-Mode Power Supply):** Uses a high-frequency (20–500 kHz) PWM switching regulator with a small isolation transformer. Efficiency is **85–95%**, much smaller, lighter, and cooler. However, it requires **EMI filtering** and careful design to avoid conducted noise.

5. **Output Stage:** Provides **+5 V DC** (logic), **+24 V DC** (I/O), and **±15 V DC** (analog) rails.

6. **Isolation and Safety:** An isolation transformer separates the mains side from the DC output, providing **galvanic isolation** and protecting against ground loops and surges.

**Hold-up time** is the duration the PLC continues to operate after mains loss, typically **20–30 ms** for ride-through, or several seconds if battery-backed.

**Isolation requirements** per IEC 61131-2: minimum **1500 V AC RMS** for 1 minute between mains and low-voltage DC.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete signal chain of an analog input module** in a PLC. Discuss the role of the multiplexer and ADC.

**Model Answer:**

A PLC analog input module processes a **continuously varying field signal** into a **digital value** usable by the CPU. The signal chain has the following stages:

1. **Sensor / Transmitter:** The field device (e.g., RTD, pressure transmitter) generates a low-level signal (mV, Ω, or 4–20 mA).

2. **Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies, filters, and linearizes the signal. For RTDs, this includes bridge completion and cold-junction compensation.

3. **Multiplexer (MUX):** A **single ADC** is time-shared across **8, 16, or 32 channels**. The MUX sequentially connects each channel to the ADC, reducing cost and complexity.

4. **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA):** Adjusts gain per channel to use the ADC's full range.

5. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Converts the conditioned analog signal to a digital code. Resolution is typically **12, 14, or 16 bits**. LSB size is $LSB = FS / (2^N - 1)$.

6. **Isolation:** Optocouplers or transformer isolation protect the CPU from field-side transients.

7. **CPU Interface:** The digital value is stored in the **Process Image Input (PII)** memory and is then used by the user program.

**Role of Multiplexer:** Allows **one expensive ADC** to serve **many channels** cost-effectively. Trade-off: lower sampling rate per channel (e.g., 100 SPS for 8 channels at 800 SPS aggregate).

**Role of ADC:** Converts the **real-world continuous signal** into a **discrete digital value** the CPU can process. The bit resolution determines the **smallest detectable change** in the input.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four phases of the PLC scan cycle** in detail. Why is the **process image** concept important? What happens if the scan time exceeds the watchdog timer?

**Model Answer:**

The **PLC scan cycle** is the **continuous, sequential operation** that the CPU performs cycle after cycle. Per IEC 61131-1, it consists of four phases:

**Phase 1 — Input Scan (Read Phase):**
The CPU reads the **physical state of every input module** (digital and analog) and stores these values into a dedicated memory area called the **Process Image Input (PII)**. The live inputs are not read again during program execution. Typical duration: 1–10 ms.

**Phase 2 — Program Execution (Logic Solve):**
The CPU executes the user program **sequentially** — for ladder logic, rung by rung, top to bottom and left to right. The program reads from the **PII** (not the live inputs) and writes output decisions to the **Process Image Output (PIO)**. Typical duration: 5–50 ms depending on program complexity.

**Phase 3 — Output Scan (Write Phase):**
The CPU copies the **PIO** values to the **physical output modules**, which then energize the connected loads (contactors, valves, lamps, drives). Outputs are latched until the next cycle.

**Phase 4 — Housekeeping (Overhead):**
The CPU handles **communication** (HMI, SCADA, peer PLCs), performs **self-diagnostics**, resets the **watchdog timer**, processes **interrupts**, and updates **internal timers/counters**.

**Why Process Image is Important:**
- It provides a **consistent snapshot of all I/O** during one program scan. This prevents the classic problem of an output changing mid-scan due to a mid-scan input transition (a "race condition").
- It **decouples the program from the physical I/O speed** — the program operates on stable memory, not on fluctuating field signals.

**Watchdog Timer Behavior:**
The PLC has a built-in **watchdog timer** (typically 100–500 ms). If the scan time exceeds this value (due to a programming error, infinite loop, or hardware fault), the CPU **enters fault mode**: **all outputs are forced OFF** (fail-safe), and a diagnostic alarm is raised. The PLC halts until the fault is cleared.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **selection criteria for choosing a PLC** for an industrial application. Mention the differences between **compact, modular, and rack-mounted** PLCs.

**Model Answer:**

Selecting the right PLC requires a **systematic engineering methodology** that considers both technical and economic factors. The key criteria are:

**1. I/O Count and Type:** Count the total digital inputs, digital outputs, analog inputs, and analog outputs. Add **~30% spare** for future expansion. Specify sinking/sourcing, AC/DC, and voltage levels.

**2. Memory Size:** Estimate the program size, data block size, and retentive memory needed. Modern PLCs offer 0.5–10 MB program memory.

**3. Scan Time:** Calculate the **maximum acceptable cycle time** based on process dynamics. For high-speed applications (e.g., packaging), scan time must be <10 ms.

**4. Communication Protocols:** Identify what must be connected — HMI, SCADA, VFDs, smart sensors, other PLCs. Common protocols: PROFINET, EtherNet/IP, Modbus TCP, PROFIBUS, EtherCAT, OPC UA.

**5. Environmental Conditions:** Temperature range, humidity, dust, vibration, corrosive atmosphere, IP/NEMA rating. May require conformal coating or hardened PLCs.

**6. Special Functions:** Does the application need PID, HSC, PWM, motion control, recipes, data logging, or web server?

**7. Expandability:** Will the system grow? Modular PLCs are more flexible.

**8. Programming Standard:** IEC 61131-3 languages (LD, FBD, ST, SFC, IL). Vendor support and ease of use matter.

**9. Reliability & Redundancy:** Critical processes (power, petrochem) need redundant CPUs, power supplies, and I/O.

**10. Cost & Vendor Support:** Initial cost, lifecycle, spare parts, training, and local technical support.

**Differences Between PLC Types:**

| **Feature** | **Compact (Brick)** | **Modular** | **Rack-Mounted** |
|---|---|---|---|
| I/O Count | 10–40 (fixed) | 100–2000 | 1000–10,000+ |
| Expandability | Limited / none | High | Very high |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Application | Small machines, OEM | Medium lines, plant sections | Large processes, DCS-like |
| Example | LOGO!, MicroLogix 1100 | S7-1500, M340 | S7-400H, ControlLogix |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 4 mA live zero** in 4–20 mA calculations → wrong engineering unit conversion.
> 2. **Confusing PNP (sourcing) and NPN (sinking)** wiring — examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Listing only the four scan phases without explaining the Process Image** — full 7 marks require both.
> 4. **Not mentioning the watchdog timer** in scan cycle answers — it is a common follow-up question.
> 5. **Selection criteria answered as a list without the 30% spare rule** — marks are cut for vagueness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Digital I/O** = 2-state (ON/OFF); **Analog I/O** = continuous (0–10 V, 4–20 mA).
- ✅ **4–20 mA** is the **industrial standard** for analog transmission; **4 mA = live zero**; **0 mA = broken wire**.
- ✅ **PNP/Sourcing** sensors source current INTO the PLC input; **NPN/Sinking** sensors sink current FROM the PLC.
- ✅ **SMPS** is preferred over linear regulators due to **higher efficiency (85–95%)** and smaller size.
- ✅ **Hold-up time** = time PLC survives after mains loss; depends on bulk capacitor and load.
- ✅ **Isolation** = galvanic separation via **optical, magnetic, or capacitive** coupling; standard is **1500 V AC RMS** for 1 minute per IEC 61131-2.
- ✅ **CTR** (Current Transfer Ratio) of optocoupler = $I_C / I_F \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Compact PLC** = fixed I/O, low cost, no/limited expansion (e.g., LOGO!).
- ✅ **Modular PLC** = plug-in modules, high expandability (e.g., S7-1500).
- ✅ **Rack-mounted PLC** = thousands of I/O, high-end (e.g., ControlLogix).
- ✅ **Scan cycle** = Input Scan → Program Execution → Output Scan → Housekeeping.
- ✅ **Process Image** = memory snapshot of I/O; prevents race conditions.
- ✅ **Watchdog timer** = typically **100–500 ms**; if exceeded, **all outputs OFF** (fail-safe).
- ✅ **ADC LSB** = $FS / (2^N - 1)$; quantization error = $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB.
- ✅ **4–20 mA → Engineering Unit:** $E = E_{min} + \dfrac{(I - 4)}{16} \times (E_{max} - E_{min})$.
- ✅ **Selection rule:** Spec **+30% spare I/O** and **+50% spare memory** for future-proofing.
- ✅ **IEC 61131-3** = international PLC programming standard (LD, FBD, ST, SFC, IL).
- ✅ **Capabilties of PLC** include: HSC, PTO, PWM, PID, motion, safety (SIL), redundancy, web/email, data logging, OPC UA.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Most exam questions on this topic are **application-based**. Always pair the concept with a real-world example (e.g., "4–20 mA from a flow meter to a PLC analog input that drives a VFD via analog output"). This signals deep understanding and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Communication with PC and Software, PLC Wiring, Installation of PLC and its Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PLC Communication with PC and Software, PLC Wiring, Installation of PLC and its Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 PLC Communication with PC and Software

### Formal Definition
**PLC Communication with PC** refers to the bidirectional data exchange between a Programmable Logic Controller and a personal computer using standardized industrial communication protocols (RS-232, RS-485, USB, Ethernet, or proprietary fieldbuses). This enables **programming**, **monitoring**, **data logging**, and **supervisory control** through dedicated PLC software platforms (e.g., Allen-Bradley RSLogix, Siemens TIA Portal, Schneider EcoStruxure, Mitsubishi GX Works).

The communication follows the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model**, typically operating on the **Physical**, **Data Link**, and **Application layers** for industrial environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must understand the *master-slave topology* where the **PC acts as the master** and the **PLC as the slave** in most point-to-point configurations.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of PLC-PC communication as a **doctor-patient consultation room**:
- The **PC (Doctor)** sends instructions (ladder logic programs, queries)
- The **PLC (Patient)** responds with current status (I/O states, process values)
- The **communication cable (Stethoscope)** acts as the medium carrying vital information
- The **PLC software (Medical record software)** interprets and displays the data meaningfully

Without this communication link, the PLC is essentially a "deaf and mute" brain — it can execute logic but cannot be reprogrammed or supervised remotely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metrics to Remember:**
> - **RS-232 Max Distance:** 15 meters (50 feet) at 20 kbps
> - **RS-485 Max Distance:** 1200 meters (4000 feet) at 100 kbps
> - **Ethernet Standard:** 100 Mbps at 100 m (Cat5e/Cat6)
> - **Default PLC Baud Rate:** 9600 bps (historically), modern PLCs support up to **115200 bps** and beyond

## 1.2 PLC Wiring

### Formal Definition
**PLC Wiring** encompasses the systematic electrical interconnection of the PLC's power supply, **Input Modules**, **Output Modules**, **field devices** (sensors and actuators), and **communication networks**, following **IEC 61131-3** and **NFPA 79** standards. It involves both **internal chassis wiring** (backplane) and **external field wiring** (terminal blocks).

Two fundamental wiring categories exist:
1. **Sinking (NPN) Configuration** — Current flows *into* the device
2. **Sourcing (PNP) Configuration** — Current flows *out of* the device

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **building's electrical distribution system**:
- The **PLC power supply (Utility transformer)** provides clean, regulated power
- **Input wiring (Smoke detectors and switches)** brings signals *into* the system
- **Output wiring (Lights and motors)** receives commands *from* the system
- **Grounding (Earthing)** is the safety net protecting both people and equipment

Just as a house needs a proper wiring plan to avoid short circuits, a PLC needs systematic wiring to avoid **noise interference**, **ground loops**, and **voltage drop issues**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Wire Color Codes (Per IEC 60446 / NFPA 79):**
> - **Red:** AC power (Line)
> - **Black:** AC power (Neutral)
> - **Blue:** DC power (Negative)
> - **Yellow:** I/O signal wires
> - **Green/Yellow striped:** Protective Earth (PE)

## 1.3 Installation of PLC and its Modules

### Formal Definition
**PLC Installation** is the engineered procedure of mechanically mounting, electrically connecting, environmentally protecting, and commissioning a Programmable Logic Controller and its associated modules (CPU, Power Supply, I/O, Communication, and Special Function modules) into an industrial control panel or enclosure, conforming to **IP54/IP65** environmental ratings and **IEC 61131-2** operational standards.

The installation process includes:
1. **Environmental assessment** (temperature, humidity, vibration, dust)
2. **Enclosure selection** (NEMA 4/4X/12 or IP65 rated)
3. **DIN-rail or panel mounting**
4. **Power supply sizing**
5. **Module addressing and slot assignment**
6. **System commissioning and testing**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of PLC installation like **installing a sophisticated kitchen**:
- The **enclosure (Kitchen room)** protects everything inside
- The **power supply (Electricity meter)** must be properly rated for total load
- The **CPU (Chef)** is the central brain orchestrating all activities
- The **I/O modules (Ingredients and utensils)** connect the chef to ingredients
- The **communication module (Walkie-talkie)** allows remote monitoring and recipe updates

A poorly installed kitchen (PLC) results in chaos — burnt food, electrical hazards, and unhappy customers. Similarly, improper installation causes **downtime**, **equipment damage**, and **safety risks**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Conditions (IEC 61131-2):**
> - **Ambient Temperature:** 0°C to 55°C
> - **Relative Humidity:** 5% to 95% (non-condensing)
> - **Vibration:** 0.5 g at 10-150 Hz
> - **Pollution Degree:** 2 (normal industrial environment)

---

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Mapping Check:** All three sub-topics in this module are interconnected — communication software requires correct wiring, and proper installation ensures both function reliably. KTU examiners frequently frame questions linking these concepts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: PLC Communication Architecture

### 2.1.1 Communication Protocol Stack

Industrial PLC communication typically operates across **three OSI layers** rather than the full seven-layer model. This streamlined approach is called the **"Fieldbus Lite"** architecture.

**Layer 1: Physical Layer (Cabling and Electrical Standards)**
- Defines voltage levels, connector types, and physical media
- Examples: RS-232 (single-ended), RS-485 (differential), Ethernet (twisted pair)
- Governed by standards like **EIA-232**, **EIA-485**, **IEEE 802.3**

**Layer 2: Data Link Layer (Framing and Error Detection)**
- Defines how data packets are structured
- Includes error checking via **Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC-16)**
- Access control via **Master-Slave polling** or **Token Passing**

**Layer 7: Application Layer (Meaningful Data Exchange)**
- Translates raw bytes into meaningful PLC data
- Common protocols: **Modbus RTU/TCP**, **EtherNet/IP**, **PROFINET**, **DeviceNet**

### 2.1.2 Master-Slave Communication Model

In a typical PC-to-PLC link, communication follows a deterministic polling cycle:

1. The **PC (Master)** sends a query frame
2. The **PLC (Slave)** receives and decodes the query
3. The **PLC** processes the request and prepares a response
4. The **PLC** sends the response back to the PC
5. The **PC** acknowledges receipt (optional)
6. The cycle repeats at a configured **polling interval**

This deterministic nature is critical in industrial automation where **timing predictability** is non-negotiable.

### 2.1.3 Communication Parameters Configuration

For successful PC-PLC handshake, the following **serial communication parameters** must match on both ends:

- **Baud Rate:** Speed of data transmission (bits per second)
- **Data Bits:** Number of bits per character (typically 7 or 8)
- **Parity:** Error checking bit (None, Even, Odd)
- **Stop Bits:** End-of-character marker (1, 1.5, or 2)
- **Flow Control:** Handshaking method (None, XON/XOFF, RTS/CTS)

The most common configuration is **8-N-1** (8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Why" Behind Asymmetry:** PC-PLC communication is asymmetric because the PC is more powerful (faster CPU, more memory, GUI) while the PLC is dedicated to real-time deterministic control. The PC needs detailed data, while the PLC just needs the new program and configuration.

## 2.2 Deep Dive: PLC Wiring Topologies

### 2.2.1 Input Wiring Configurations

**Sinking Input (NPN sensor to PLC):**
- Sensor switches the **negative (0V)** side
- Current flows *from* PLC input *through* sensor *to* ground
- Common in **Asian and European** industrial standards

**Sourcing Input (PNP sensor to PLC):**
- Sensor switches the **positive (+V)** side
- Current flows *from* supply *through* sensor *into* PLC input
- Common in **North American** industrial standards

> [!WARNING]
> **Compatibility Issue:** Connecting a PNP sensor to a sinking input (or vice versa) results in **NO operation** — the circuit never completes. Always verify sensor type matches PLC input configuration.

### 2.2.2 Output Wiring Configurations

Outputs also come in **sinking** and **sourcing** variants:
- **Relay Output:** Voltage-free dry contact (works with AC or DC loads), slower switching
- **Transistor Output:** DC only, faster switching (typically < 1 ms), used for high-speed applications
- **Triac Output:** AC only, used for AC loads like solenoids and heaters

### 2.2.3 Shielding and Noise Mitigation

Industrial environments contain significant electromagnetic interference (EMI) from:
- Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)
- Motor starters and contactors
- Welding equipment
- Radio frequency sources

**Best Practices for Noise Mitigation:**
1. Use **shielded twisted pair (STP)** cables for analog signals
2. Maintain **minimum 200 mm (8 inches)** separation between signal and power cables
3. Ground the shield at **one end only** (typically the PLC end) to avoid ground loops
4. Use **ferrite beads** on cables entering the enclosure
5. Install **surge protectors** on incoming power lines

## 2.3 Deep Dive: Installation Engineering

### 2.3.1 Power Supply Sizing

The PLC power supply must be sized to handle the **sum of all module current draws** plus a **20-30% safety margin**:

$$P_{supply} = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i \cdot V\right) \times 1.25$$

Where:
- $P_{supply}$ = Required power supply wattage
- $I_i$ = Current draw of module $i$ in Amperes
- $V$ = Operating voltage (typically 24 VDC or 5 VDC for backplane)
- $1.25$ = 25% safety margin

### 2.3.2 Module Addressing

In modular PLC systems, each module occupies a specific **slot** with a unique **address range**. Common addressing schemes:
- **Fixed Slot Addressing:** Module in slot 2 always has I/O address 2.x
- **Automatic Addressing:** PLC scans and assigns addresses sequentially
- **Configurable Addressing:** Programmer manually maps logical addresses to physical slots

### 2.3.3 Heat Dissipation Calculation

PLCs generate heat that must be dissipated to maintain junction temperatures within limits:

$$Q = P_{total} = V \times I_{total} \times (1 - \eta)$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat generated in Watts
- $\eta$ = Power supply efficiency (typically 0.85 to 0.95)

For **natural convection cooling**, the enclosure surface area required is approximately:

$$A_{surface} = \frac{Q}{k \cdot \Delta T}$$

Where:
- $A_{surface}$ = Required enclosure surface area in $m^2$
- $k$ = Heat transfer coefficient (≈ 5.5 W/m²·K for natural convection)
- $\Delta T$ = Maximum allowable temperature rise (typically 15°C)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| Power Supply Sizing | $P = \left(\sum I_i\right) \times V \times 1.25$ | Watts (W) | Module power budget |
| Heat Dissipation | $Q = V \times I \times (1-\eta)$ | Watts (W) | Thermal management |
| Enclosure Surface Area | $A = Q / (k \cdot \Delta T)$ | $m^2$ | Cooling design |
| Baud Rate (Asynchronous) | $T_{bit} = 1 / \text{Baud}$ | seconds | Communication timing |
| Frame Time | $T_{frame} = (N_{bits} / \text{Baud}) \times N_{frames}$ | seconds | Real-time analysis |
| Voltage Drop | $V_{drop} = I \times R_{cable}$ | Volts (V) | Wire sizing |
| Max RS-232 Distance | $D_{max} = 15$ m at 20 kbps | meters (m) | Cable selection |
| Max RS-485 Distance | $D_{max} = 1200$ m at 100 kbps | meters (m) | Cable selection |
| Cable Resistance | $R = \rho \cdot L / A$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Voltage drop calc |
| Ohms Law (Load) | $V = I \times R$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | Output load check |
| Power Dissipation (Output) | $P = V_{CE(sat)} \times I_{load}$ | Watts (W) | Thermal derating |
| IP Rating Minimum | IP54 (indoor) / IP65 (outdoor) | Class | Enclosure selection |
| Operating Temperature | 0°C to 55°C | Celsius | Environmental spec |
| Max Relative Humidity | 95% non-condensing | Percent (%) | Environmental spec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas appear in KTU numerical problems on PLC installation, communication timing analysis, and power budget calculations. Memorize the constants (15 m, 1200 m, 9600 baud) — they are favorites in objective sections.

## 2.5 Real-World Application Scenarios

### 2.5.1 Communication in SCADA Systems
In a typical **water treatment plant**, multiple PLCs communicate with a central SCADA server:
- Field PLCs collect data from flow meters, level sensors, and pH probes
- Communication occurs via **Modbus TCP** over industrial Ethernet
- Data refresh rate: **every 100 ms** to **every 1 second**

### 2.5.2 Wiring in Automotive Assembly Lines
Automotive plants use **24 VDC sinking I/O** with **DeviceNet** networks:
- Average I/O count per station: **256 digital + 32 analog**
- Cable lengths: typically **< 50 m** within a station
- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) requirement: > **100,000 hours**

### 2.5.3 Installation in Hazardous Locations
In **oil refineries** and **chemical plants**, PLCs must be installed in:
- **Explosion-proof enclosures** (ATEX/IECEx certified)
- With **intrinsic safety barriers** for field wiring
- Temperature class **T4** (max 135°C surface temperature)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Communication Frame Time Calculation

This is a classic KTU numerical problem. We derive the total time required to transmit a complete Modbus RTU message between a PC and PLC.

### Given Parameters (Typical KTU Problem Statement)
- **Baud Rate:** $B = 9600$ bps
- **Data bits per character:** $N_d = 8$
- **Parity bit:** $N_p = 1$ (Even parity)
- **Stop bits:** $N_s = 1$
- **Function Code:** 03 (Read Holding Registers)
- **Number of registers to read:** $n = 4$
- **Slave address:** 1 byte

### Step 1: Determine Total Bits per Character

Each character transmitted in asynchronous serial communication consists of:
- Start bit (always 1)
- Data bits ($N_d$)
- Parity bit ($N_p$, optional)
- Stop bits ($N_s$)

$$N_{char} = 1 + N_d + N_p + N_s$$

Substituting the values:

$$N_{char} = 1 + 8 + 1 + 1 = 11 \text{ bits per character}$$

**Logic:** The start bit is mandatory (1 bit), followed by 8 data bits, 1 parity bit for error detection, and 1 stop bit to mark the end of the character. Total = 11 bits.

### Step 2: Determine Modbus RTU Frame Structure

A Modbus RTU read request frame contains:
- **Slave Address:** 1 byte (1 character)
- **Function Code:** 1 byte (1 character)
- **Starting Address High:** 1 byte (1 character)
- **Starting Address Low:** 1 byte (1 character)
- **Number of Registers High:** 1 byte (1 character)
- **Number of Registers Low:** 1 byte (1 character)
- **CRC Low:** 1 byte (1 character)
- **CRC High:** 1 byte (1 character)

$$N_{request} = 8 \text{ characters} = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

### Step 3: Determine Modbus RTU Response Frame Structure

A response frame for reading $n = 4$ registers contains:
- **Slave Address:** 1 byte
- **Function Code:** 1 byte
- **Byte Count:** 1 byte (equals $2n = 8$ bytes)
- **Register Data:** $2n = 8$ bytes
- **CRC Low:** 1 byte
- **CRC High:** 1 byte

$$N_{response} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 2n + 2 = 4 + 2n$$

Substituting $n = 4$:

$$N_{response} = 4 + 2(4) = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ bytes} = 12 \text{ characters}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Total Bits Transmitted

$$N_{total\_bits} = (N_{request} + N_{response}) \times N_{char}$$

Substituting the values:

$$N_{total\_bits} = (8 + 12) \times 11 = 20 \times 11 = 220 \text{ bits}$$

**Logic:** Both request and response are framed with 11 bits per character, so total bits = total characters × bits per character.

### Step 5: Calculate Total Frame Transmission Time

$$T_{frame} = \frac{N_{total\_bits}}{B}$$

Substituting the values:

$$T_{frame} = \frac{220}{9600} \approx 0.02292 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{frame} \approx 22.92 \text{ ms}$$

**Logic:** Time = Total bits / Baud rate. This represents the time the data is "on the wire" — actual response time at the application level will be slightly higher due to processing delays (typically 1-5 ms per device).

### Step 6: Account for Inter-frame Silence

Modbus RTU requires a **3.5 character silence** between frames:

$$T_{silence} = 3.5 \times \frac{N_{char}}{B} = 3.5 \times \frac{11}{9600}$$

$$T_{silence} = \frac{38.5}{9600} \approx 0.00401 \text{ seconds} \approx 4.01 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 7: Total Round-Trip Time

$$T_{RT} = T_{frame} + 2 \times T_{silence} + 2 \times T_{processing}$$

Assuming $T_{processing} = 2$ ms per device:

$$T_{RT} = 22.92 + 2(4.01) + 2(2) = 22.92 + 8.02 + 4 = 34.94 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{RT} \approx 35 \text{ ms}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This 35 ms round-trip time means the PC can poll the PLC at a maximum rate of about **28 times per second** over RS-232 at 9600 baud. For faster polling, increase baud rate or use Ethernet (100 Mbps is 10,000× faster).

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Power Supply Sizing for Multi-Module PLC

### Problem Setup
A PLC rack contains the following modules (typical KTU problem):

| Module | Quantity | Current Draw (5V Backplane) | Current Draw (24V Field) |
|--------|----------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| CPU | 1 | 800 mA | 0 mA |
| Digital Input (32-point) | 3 | 250 mA each | 100 mA each |
| Digital Output (16-point relay) | 2 | 350 mA each | 150 mA each |
| Analog Input (8-channel) | 1 | 300 mA | 200 mA |
| Communication Module (Ethernet) | 1 | 400 mA | 0 mA |

### Step 1: Calculate Total Backplane Current (5V)

$$I_{5V} = I_{CPU} + 3 \times I_{DI} + 2 \times I_{DO} + I_{AI} + I_{COMM}$$

$$I_{5V} = 800 + 3(250) + 2(350) + 300 + 400$$

$$I_{5V} = 800 + 750 + 700 + 300 + 400 = 2950 \text{ mA} = 2.95 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Total Field Current (24V)

$$I_{24V} = 3 \times I_{DI,field} + 2 \times I_{DO,field} + I_{AI,field}$$

$$I_{24V} = 3(100) + 2(150) + 200 = 300 + 300 + 200 = 800 \text{ mA} = 0.8 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Total Power

$$P_{5V} = 5 \text{ V} \times 2.95 \text{ A} = 14.75 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{24V} = 24 \text{ V} \times 0.8 \text{ A} = 19.20 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{total} = 14.75 + 19.20 = 33.95 \text{ W}$$

### Step 4: Apply 25% Safety Margin

$$P_{supply} = P_{total} \times 1.25 = 33.95 \times 1.25$$

$$\boxed{P_{supply} \approx 42.44 \text{ W}}$$

**Conclusion:** Select the next standard power supply size — typically a **50 W** or **60 W** PLC power supply module.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Installation Checklist

| Phase | Step | Verification | Common Error |
|-------|------|--------------|--------------|
| 1. Pre-Installation | Environmental survey | Temp, humidity, dust, EMI sources | Overlooking vibration sources |
| 2. Enclosure | Select NEMA/IP rating | IP54 indoor, IP65 outdoor | Using IP20 in washdown areas |
| 3. Mounting | DIN-rail or panel mount | Vertical orientation, 50 mm clearance | Horizontal mounting (heats up) |
| 4. Power | Connect 24 VDC or 120/230 VAC | Polarity, fuse rating | No surge protection |
| 5. Grounding | Connect PE terminal | < 1 Ω to earth ground | Daisy-chained grounds |
| 6. Input Wiring | Connect sensors | Sinking vs sourcing match | PNP/NPN mismatch |
| 7. Output Wiring | Connect actuators | Voltage and current rating | Exceeding surge current |
| 8. Communication | Connect PC link | Baud rate, parity, protocol | Mismatched parameters |
| 9. Power-On Self-Test | Observe LED indicators | All modules detected | Ignoring diagnostic LEDs |
| 10. I/O Test | Force I/O points | Read back signals | Skipping under load |
| 11. Program Load | Transfer from PC | Verify checksum | No backup of old program |
| 12. Commissioning | Run in dry cycle | All interlocks functional | Skipping safety checks |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to include the **25-30% safety margin** in power calculations. Always show this step explicitly — examiners specifically look for it.

## 3.4 Communication Distance vs Baud Rate Trade-off Table

| Baud Rate (bps) | RS-232 Max Distance | RS-485 Max Distance | Typical Use |
|------------------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1200 | 15 m | 1200 m | Legacy devices |
| 9600 | 15 m | 1200 m | Default PLC |
| 19200 | 15 m | 1200 m | Faster polling |
| 38400 | 7.5 m | 900 m | High-speed serial |
| 57600 | 5 m | 700 m | Specialized |
| 115200 | 2.5 m | 500 m | Modern serial |
| 10 M (Ethernet) | — | 100 m | Industrial networks |
| 100 M (Ethernet) | — | 100 m | High-speed comms |

**Key Insight:** Higher baud rate → shorter reliable distance. This is due to signal attenuation and EMI susceptibility.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PLC-PC Communication Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HOST["HOST COMPUTER PC SIDE"]
        APP["APPLICATION SOFTWARE SCADA HMI"]
        DRV["COMMUNICATION DRIVER OPC SERVER"]
        PCPORT["PC SERIAL PORT USB OR ETHERNET"]
    end
    
    subgraph MEDIA["COMMUNICATION MEDIA"]
        CABLE["CABLE RS232 RS485 OR CAT6"]
    end
    
    subgraph PLCSIDE["PLC SIDE"]
        PLCPORT["PLC COMMUNICATION PORT CH0 CH1"]
        CPU["CPU MODULE PROCESSES FRAMES"]
        MEM["MEMORY HOLDING REGISTERS"]
        IO["I/O MODULES FIELD DEVICES"]
    end
    
    APP -->|"USER COMMANDS"| DRV
    DRV -->|"SERIAL DATA STREAM"| PCPORT
    PCPORT -->|"ELECTRICAL SIGNALS"| CABLE
    CABLE -->|"DIFFERENTIAL OR SINGLE ENDED"| PLCPORT
    PLCPORT -->|"DECODED MESSAGES"| CPU
    CPU -->|"READ WRITE"| MEM
    MEM -->|"I/O STATUS"| IO
    IO -->|"FIELD SIGNALS"| CPU
    CPU -->|"RESPONSE FRAMES"| PLCPORT
    PLCPORT -->|"SERIAL DATA"| CABLE
    CABLE -->|"DATA TO PC"| PCPORT
    PCPORT -->|"PROCESSED DATA"| DRV
    DRV -->|"DISPLAY UPDATES"| APP
```

## 4.2 PLC Wiring Topology (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph POWERSUPPLY["POWER SUPPLY SECTION"]
        MAINS["AC MAINS 120 230 VAC"]
        PSU["PLC POWER SUPPLY 24 VDC OUTPUT"]
        FILTER["LINE FILTER EMI SUPPRESSOR"]
    end
    
    subgraph INPUTSIDE["INPUT WIRING SIDE"]
        SENSORA["PNP SENSOR 1"]
        SENSORB["NPN SENSOR 2"]
        SWITCH["LIMIT SWITCH 3"]
        DI["DIGITAL INPUT MODULE 32 POINTS"]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUTSIDE["OUTPUT WIRING SIDE"]
        DO["DIGITAL OUTPUT MODULE 16 RELAYS"]
        MOTOR["MOTOR CONTACTOR"]
        VALVE["SOLENOID VALVE"]
        INDICATOR["INDICATOR LAMP"]
    end
    
    MAINS --> FILTER
    FILTER --> PSU
    PSU -->|"24 VDC"| DI
    PSU -->|"24 VDC"| DO
    SENSORA -->|"SOURCING SIGNAL"| DI
    SENSORB -->|"SINKING SIGNAL"| DI
    SWITCH -->|"DRY CONTACT"| DI
    DO -->|"RELAY CONTACT"| MOTOR
    DO -->|"RELAY CONTACT"| VALVE
    DO -->|"RELAY CONTACT"| INDICATOR
```

## 4.3 PLC Installation Flow Chart

```mermaid
graph TB
    START["START INSTALLATION PROJECT"]
    PHASE1["PHASE 1 SITE SURVEY"]
    PHASE2["PHASE 2 DESIGN"]
    PHASE3["PHASE 3 MECHANICAL MOUNTING"]
    PHASE4["PHASE 4 ELECTRICAL WIRING"]
    PHASE5["PHASE 5 COMMUNICATION SETUP"]
    PHASE6["PHASE 6 TESTING"]
    PHASE7["PHASE 7 COMMISSIONING"]
    END["END SYSTEM HANDED OVER"]
    
    START --> PHASE1
    PHASE1 -->|"CHECK TEMP HUMIDITY EMI"| PHASE2
    PHASE2 -->|"ENCLOSURE POWER MODULE SELECTION"| PHASE3
    PHASE3 -->|"DIN RAIL PANEL MOUNT"| PHASE4
    PHASE4 -->|"POWER I/O GROUNDING"| PHASE5
    PHASE5 -->|"PC LINK PROTOCOL CONFIG"| PHASE6
    PHASE6 -->|"I/O TEST PROGRAM VERIFY"| PHASE7
    PHASE7 -->|"LOAD TRIAL RUN SIGN OFF"| END
```

## 4.4 RS-232 vs RS-485 vs Ethernet Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RS232["RS 232 POINT TO POINT"]
        RS1["SINGLE TX AND RX LINE"]
        RS2["MAX 15 M DISTANCE"]
        RS3["VOLTAGE PLUS MINUS 12 V"]
        RS4["USED FOR PC TO PLC DIRECT"]
    end
    
    subgraph RS485["RS 485 MULTI DROP"]
        RS5["DIFFERENTIAL PAIR A AND B"]
        RS6["MAX 32 NODES 1200 M"]
        RS7["VOLTAGE PLUS MINUS 5 V DIFFERENTIAL"]
        RS8["USED FOR PLC TO MULTIPLE DEVICES"]
    end
    
    subgraph ETH["ETHERNET INDUSTRIAL"]
        E1["TWISTED PAIR CAT 5E CAT 6"]
        E2["MAX 100 M PER SEGMENT"]
        E3["10 100 1000 MBPS"]
        E4["USED FOR SCADA TO PLC HIGH SPEED"]
    end
    
    RS232 --- RS485
    RS485 --- ETH
```

## 4.5 PLC Module Slot Architecture (Rack-Based PLC)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph RACK["PLC BACKPLANE RACK"]
        SLOT0["SLOT 0 POWER SUPPLY MODULE"]
        SLOT1["SLOT 1 CPU MODULE"]
        SLOT2["SLOT 2 DIGITAL INPUT 32 PT"]
        SLOT3["SLOT 3 DIGITAL INPUT 32 PT"]
        SLOT4["SLOT 4 DIGITAL OUTPUT 16 PT RELAY"]
        SLOT5["SLOT 5 ANALOG INPUT 8 CH"]
        SLOT6["SLOT 6 ANALOG OUTPUT 4 CH"]
        SLOT7["SLOT 7 COMMUNICATION ETHERNET"]
        SLOT8["SLOT 8 COMMUNICATION RS485"]
        BACKPLANE["BACKPLANE 5V AND 24V DISTRIBUTION"]
    end
    
    SLOT0 --- BACKPLANE
    SLOT1 --- BACKPLANE
    SLOT2 --- BACKPLANE
    SLOT3 --- BACKPLANE
    SLOT4 --- BACKPLANE
    SLOT5 --- BACKPLANE
    SLOT6 --- BACKPLANE
    SLOT7 --- BACKPLANE
    SLOT8 --- BACKPLANE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading These Diagrams for KTU:** When examiners ask "draw the block diagram of PLC-PC communication," they expect at minimum: PC, cable, PLC CPU, and I/O modules with clear direction of data flow (request vs response). Use labeled arrows — "request frame" and "response frame" are key phrases.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define PLC Communication with PC. Mention any two communication protocols used.
**Model Answer:**
PLC communication with PC refers to the bidirectional data transfer between a personal computer and a PLC for programming, monitoring, and data acquisition purposes. The PC typically acts as the master, sending requests and receiving responses.

**Two communication protocols:**
1. **RS-232 / RS-485 (Serial):** Point-to-point or multi-drop serial communication using voltage-based signaling
2. **Ethernet (TCP/IP):** High-speed communication using Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, or PROFINET protocols

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question - Module 1]**

### Question 2: Differentiate between Sinking and Sourcing Inputs with a neat sketch.
**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sinking Input (NPN) | Sourcing Input (PNP) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Current direction | Flows *into* the input terminal | Flows *out of* the input terminal |
| Sensor type | NPN sensor | PNP sensor |
| Signal level | Active low (0 V triggers) | Active high (24 V triggers) |
| Common in | Asian/European markets | North American markets |
| Voltage at input | Pulled to 0 V when active | Pulled to 24 V when active |

**Diagram Logic:** In sinking, sensor connects input to 0V when active. In sourcing, sensor connects input to +V when active.

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question - Module 1]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the various methods of PLC communication with PC. Describe the master-slave protocol with a suitable diagram.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:**
PLC communication with PC can be established through several physical media and protocols, each with specific advantages based on distance, speed, and number of connected devices.

**Methods of Communication:**

**(1) Serial Communication (RS-232):**
- Single-ended, point-to-point
- Maximum distance: **15 meters** at **20 kbps**
- Connectors: DB-9 or DB-25
- Voltage levels: ±12 V
- Used for direct short-distance PC-to-PLC programming

**(2) Serial Communication (RS-485):**
- Differential signaling, multi-drop (up to **32 devices**)
- Maximum distance: **1200 meters** at **100 kbps**
- Used for PLC networks with multiple devices

**(3) Ethernet Communication:**
- High-speed, TCP/IP based
- **100 Mbps** at **100 m** (Cat5e/Cat6)
- Protocols: Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, PROFINET
- Used for SCADA and high-speed data exchange

**(4) USB Communication:**
- Modern PLCs support USB 2.0/3.0
- Plug-and-play, up to 480 Mbps
- Limited cable length (5 m for USB 2.0)

**(5) Proprietary Protocols:**
- Manufacturer-specific (e.g., Siemens MPI, Allen-Bradley DF1)
- Optimized for specific PLC families

**Master-Slave Protocol Explanation:**

In the **master-slave model**, the PC acts as the **master** and the PLC as the **slave**. The communication flow is as follows:

1. The master (PC) initiates all communication by sending a query frame
2. The slave (PLC) decodes the query using its communication module
3. The slave processes the request and prepares the response
4. The slave transmits the response back to the master
5. The master acknowledges and displays the data
6. The cycle repeats at a configured polling interval

The master polls each slave in a deterministic round-robin fashion, ensuring predictable timing. The slave cannot initiate communication — it can only respond to queries. This prevents data collisions and ensures deterministic behavior essential for industrial control.

**Diagram:**

```
PC (Master)  <----- Request Frame ----->  PLC (Slave)
                <---- Response Frame ----
```

**Communication Parameters (must match on both sides):**
- Baud rate (e.g., 9600, 19200, 115200 bps)
- Data bits (7 or 8)
- Parity (None, Even, Odd)
- Stop bits (1 or 2)
- Flow control (None, XON/XOFF, RTS/CTS)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: With a neat diagram, explain the installation procedure of a PLC system. List any six environmental considerations.

**Model Answer:**

**Installation Procedure:**

**Step 1: Site Survey and Planning**
- Assess ambient temperature, humidity, dust, and vibration
- Identify EMI sources (VFDs, motors, welding equipment)
- Plan cable routes and grounding locations

**Step 2: Enclosure Selection**
- Select **NEMA 4/4X/12** or **IP54/IP65** rated enclosure
- Ensure adequate space for heat dissipation
- Include cable entry glands and ventilation

**Step 3: Mechanical Mounting**
- Mount on **DIN rail** (35 mm standard) or panel
- Maintain **50 mm clearance** above and below for airflow
- Ensure **vertical orientation** for proper convection cooling

**Step 4: Power Supply Installation**
- Connect to a clean, dedicated power source
- Install surge protection and line filters
- Use proper wire gauges per current requirements

**Step 5: Module Installation**
- Snap modules onto the backplane/DIN rail
- Tighten module locks
- Verify address switches/jumpers

**Step 6: Wiring**
- Connect I/O devices per wiring diagrams
- Follow color codes: red (AC line), black (AC neutral), blue (DC-)
- Use shielded cables for analog signals
- Ground shield at **one end only**

**Step 7: Communication Setup**
- Connect PC link cable
- Configure communication parameters in software
- Test communication with diagnostic commands

**Step 8: Testing and Commissioning**
- Perform **I/O test** (force inputs, read outputs)
- Load and verify program
- Run in **dry cycle** (no load) first
- Then run in **loaded condition** with safety checks

**Diagram (Installation Flow):**

```
Site Survey → Enclosure → Mounting → Power → Modules 
   → Wiring → Communication → Testing → Commissioning
```

**Six Environmental Considerations:**

1. **Temperature:** 0°C to 55°C (per IEC 61131-2)
2. **Humidity:** 5% to 95% non-condensing
3. **Vibration:** < 0.5 g (10–150 Hz) — use anti-vibration mounts if exceeded
4. **Dust and particulates:** Use sealed enclosures (IP65) in dusty environments
5. **EMI/RFI sources:** Maintain 200 mm separation from power cables; use shielded cables
6. **Chemical exposure:** Use corrosion-resistant enclosures (stainless steel, fiberglass) in chemical plants

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the different types of PLC wiring with diagrams. Compare relay, transistor, and triac outputs.

**Model Answer:**

**PLC Wiring Types:**

**(1) Power Supply Wiring:**
- Connects mains AC (120/230 VAC) to the PLC power supply module
- Must include circuit breaker, surge protector, and line filter
- Output: regulated 24 VDC for internal use and field devices

**(2) Input Wiring:**

*Sinking Input (NPN):*
- Current flows *into* the input terminal
- Sensor switches the negative (0V) side
- Common in Asian/European standards

*Sourcing Input (PNP):*
- Current flows *out of* the input terminal
- Sensor switches the positive (+V) side
- Common in North American standards

**Diagram - Sinking Input:**

```
   +24V
    |
   [Sensor] (NPN)
    |
   PLC Input Terminal
    |
   (Internal pull-up resistor to +24V)
```

**Diagram - Sourcing Input:**

```
   +24V
    |
   [Sensor] (PNP)
    |
   PLC Input Terminal
    |
   (Internal pull-down resistor to 0V)
```

**(3) Output Wiring:**

*Relay Output:*
- Mechanical contacts (SPDT or SPST)
- Can switch AC or DC loads
- Slower switching (10 ms typical)
- Higher surge current handling
- Lifespan: 100,000 to 1,000,000 operations

*Transistor Output:*
- Solid-state (BJT or MOSFET)
- DC only
- Fast switching (< 1 µs)
- Used for high-speed applications
- Lifespan: virtually unlimited

*Triac Output:*
- Solid-state AC switch
- AC only (zero-crossing switching)
- Used for solenoids, heaters, AC motors
- Lifespan: virtually unlimited

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Relay Output | Transistor Output | Triac Output |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Load type | AC or DC | DC only | AC only |
| Switching speed | ~10 ms | < 1 µs | ~10 µs |
| Surge handling | High | Low | Medium |
| Lifespan | Limited | Unlimited | Unlimited |
| Cost | Higher | Lower | Medium |
| Isolation | Galvanic | Opto-coupled | Opto-coupled |
| Leakage current | None | Small | Small |

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: With a block diagram, explain the communication interface between a PC and a PLC using Modbus RTU protocol. Calculate the frame transmission time for reading 10 holding registers at 19200 baud, 8-E-1 configuration.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
┌──────────────────┐                    ┌──────────────────┐
│  PC (Master)     │                    │   PLC (Slave)    │
│                  │                    │                  │
│  ┌────────────┐  │    RS-232/RS-485   │  ┌────────────┐  │
│  │  SCADA     │  │◄─────────────────►│  │  Comm Port │  │
│  │  Software  │  │                    │  │  (Modbus)  │  │
│  └────────────┘  │                    │  └────────────┘  │
│  ┌────────────┐  │                    │  ┌────────────┐  │
│  │  Modbus    │  │                    │  │   CPU      │  │
│  │  Master    │  │                    │  │            │  │
│  │  Driver    │  │                    │  └────────────┘  │
│  └────────────┘  │                    │  ┌────────────┐  │
│                  │                    │  │  Holding   │  │
│  COM Port        │                    │  │  Registers │  │
└──────────────────┘                    │  └────────────┘  │
                                        └──────────────────┘
```

**Modbus RTU Frame Format:**

**Request Frame (Read Holding Registers - Function 03):**
- Slave Address: 1 byte
- Function Code: 1 byte (0x03)
- Starting Address: 2 bytes
- Quantity of Registers: 2 bytes
- CRC: 2 bytes
- **Total: 8 bytes**

**Response Frame:**
- Slave Address: 1 byte
- Function Code: 1 byte
- Byte Count: 1 byte (equals 2n)
- Register Data: 2n bytes
- CRC: 2 bytes
- **Total: 5 + 2n bytes** = 5 + 2(10) = **25 bytes**

**Calculation:**

**Step 1: Bits per character**

Configuration: 8-E-1 (8 data bits, Even parity, 1 stop bit)
$$N_{char} = 1_{start} + 8_{data} + 1_{parity} + 1_{stop} = 11 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Total bytes**

$$N_{total} = N_{request} + N_{response} = 8 + 25 = 33 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3: Total bits**

$$N_{bits} = 33 \times 11 = 363 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4: Transmission time at 19200 baud**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{N_{bits}}{B} = \frac{363}{19200}$$

$$T_{frame} \approx 0.01891 \text{ seconds} \approx 18.91 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5: Add inter-frame silence (3.5 character times)**

$$T_{silence} = 3.5 \times \frac{11}{19200} \approx 2.01 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6: Total round-trip time (assuming 2 ms processing each side)**

$$T_{RT} = 18.91 + 2(2.01) + 2(2) = 26.93 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{T_{RT} \approx 27 \text{ ms}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** At 19200 baud, the PC can poll the PLC approximately **37 times per second** for this transaction.

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Areas (Exam Pitfalls):**
> 
> **1. Forgetting the 25% Safety Margin in Power Calculations (Lose 1-2 marks):**
> Almost every KTU numerical on PLC installation includes a power supply sizing problem. Students who compute $P_{total}$ but forget the safety margin lose full credit. **Always multiply by 1.25.**
> 
> **2. Not Mentioning "Master-Slave" Terminology (Lose 2-3 marks):**
> When asked about PC-PLC communication, the magic words are **"PC is master, PLC is slave"** and **"deterministic polling."** Without these keywords, expect partial credit only.
> 
> **3. Confusing Sinking and Sourcing (Lose 2 marks):**
> Sinking = NPN sensor = current flows **into** PLC. Sourcing = PNP sensor = current flows **out of** PLC. Mixing these up is the #1 conceptual error.
> 
> **4. Omitting the Block Diagram in Communication Questions (Lose 2-3 marks):**
> A 14-mark question on communication **always** requires a labeled block diagram. A textual answer alone is incomplete.
> 
> **5. Skipping the Unit Analysis in Numerical Problems (Lose 1 mark):**
> Always state units explicitly: **mA, A, V, W, ms, kbps, m, °C**. KTU examiners award marks for proper unit notation.
> 
> **6. Not Distinguishing "Slot" vs "Address" (Lose 1 mark):**
> A module's **slot** is its physical position. Its **address** is its logical identifier. Modular addressing and slot addressing are different concepts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

#### Communication Concepts
- [x] PC is **master**, PLC is **slave** in typical configurations
- [x] RS-232: **15 m** max, point-to-point, ±12 V
- [x] RS-485: **1200 m** max, multi-drop (32 nodes), differential ±5 V
- [x] Ethernet: **100 m** max, **100 Mbps** typical, **Modbus TCP / EtherNet/IP / PROFINET**
- [x] Default communication parameters: **8-N-1**, **9600 baud**
- [x] Modbus RTU uses **Function Code 03** for reading holding registers
- [x] Modbus frame: slave address + function code + data + **CRC-16**

#### Wiring Concepts
- [x] **Sinking (NPN)** = current flows into PLC
- [x] **Sourcing (PNP)** = current flows out of PLC
- [x] **Relay outputs** = AC/DC capable, slower, limited life
- [x] **Transistor outputs** = DC only, fast switching
- [x] **Triac outputs** = AC only, zero-crossing switching
- [x] Shielded cables for analog signals; **200 mm separation** from power cables
- [x] Ground shield at **one end only** to prevent ground loops

#### Installation Concepts
- [x] Operating temperature: **0°C to 55°C**
- [x] Humidity: **5% to 95%** non-condensing
- [x] Enclosure rating: **IP54** indoor, **IP65** outdoor
- [x] Power supply formula: $P = \sum I \times V \times 1.25$
- [x] Heat dissipation: $Q = P \times (1 - \eta)$ and $A = Q / (k \cdot \Delta T)$
- [x] **50 mm clearance** around PLC for ventilation
- [x] Vertical mounting only for proper convection
- [x] Power-on self-test (POST) is the first commissioning step

#### Critical Formulas to Memorize
- [x] **Power Sizing:** $P_{supply} = \left(\sum I_i\right) \times V \times 1.25$
- [x] **Frame Time:** $T_{frame} = (N_{bytes} \times N_{bits/char}) / B$
- [x] **Voltage Drop:** $V_{drop} = I \times R_{cable} = I \times (\rho L / A)$
- [x] **Heat Dissipation:** $Q = P \times (1-\eta)$
- [x] **Bits per char:** $N_{char} = 1 + N_d + N_p + N_s$

#### Key Industry Standards
- [x] **IEC 61131-3:** PLC programming languages standard
- [x] **IEC 61131-2:** PLC operating conditions standard
- [x] **NFPA 79:** Industrial machinery electrical standard
- [x] **NEMA 4/4X/12:** Enclosure protection ratings
- [x] **IP54/IP65:** Ingress Protection ratings
- [x] **EIA-232 / EIA-485:** Serial communication standards

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: (1) Definition/Introduction, (2) Block diagram, (3) Detailed explanation, (4) Numerical/Example, (5) Conclusion. This 5-part structure is exactly how KTU model answer sheets are organized — mirror it for maximum marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Programming Languages: Ladder Logic, Functional Block Diagram (FBD), Sequential Flow Chart (SFC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** PLC Programming Languages: Ladder Logic, Functional Block Diagram (FBD), Sequential Flow Chart (SFC)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

According to the **IEC 61131-3 international standard** (adopted verbatim by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEBRT521), a **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) programming language** is a formally defined, vendor-independent graphical or textual syntax used to express the control logic, sequencing, and interlocking strategies executed by the PLC's central processing unit during each deterministic scan cycle.

The standard classifies PLC programming languages into two broad families:

| Family | Languages | Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Textual Languages** | Instruction List (IL), Structured Text (ST) | Text-based |
| **Graphical Languages** | **Ladder Diagram (LD)**, **Function Block Diagram (FBD)**, **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** | Visual/Diagrammatic |

For the scope of this module, the three graphical languages specified by the KTU syllabus are:

- **Ladder Logic (LD)** — A relay-ladder schematic-based graphical language where the program is represented as a series of horizontal *rungs* between two vertical *power rails*.
- **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** — A signal-flow oriented graphical language where control functions are represented as interconnected rectangular *function blocks*.
- **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** — A state-transition language derived from **IEC 848 (GRAFCET)** that describes a process as a series of *steps* connected by *transitions*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The standard **IEC 61131-3** is the *backbone* reference for all PLC programming language questions. Any answer lacking reference to the standard will be marked down by the board examiner. Always cite the standard wherever possible.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are teaching a **new factory electrician** how to control a conveyor belt motor using a push-button starter:

- **Ladder Logic Analogy** — You draw the *exact same electrical wiring diagram* he has been using for 20 years: a vertical "L" (line) and "N" (neutral) rail with horizontal wires (rungs) carrying switches, overloads, and contactor coils. He learns it in **5 minutes** because the language mirrors his mental model.
- **FBD Analogy** — You hand him a **block diagram of a washing machine control board**: each black box (Timer, Counter, AND gate) has named input pins on the left and output pins on the right, with arrows (signal flow) connecting them. He wires the inputs and outputs visually.
- **SFC Analogy** — You give him a **checklist written on a clipboard**: "Step 1 — Press Start. Step 2 — Wait 10 s. Step 3 — Open Valve A. Step 4 — If Tank Full, Go to Step 5, Else Return to Step 3." The SFC is that clipboard: discrete named *steps* with explicit *transitions* (the "if" conditions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Memory Aid:** LD is the *electrician's language*, FBD is the *electronics engineer's language*, and SFC is the *process engineer's language*. They all ultimately compile into the same machine code executed by the PLC CPU.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Board Frequently Tested)

- **IEC 61131-3** — The governing international standard for PLC programming languages (3rd edition, 2013, current KTU reference).
- **IEC 848** — Sub-standard governing the **SFC / GRAFCET** notation.
- **Scan Time (T_scan)** — Typical values range from **1 ms** to **20 ms** for modern PLCs. KTU commonly cites **10 ms** as the standard benchmark for exam problems.
- **Boolean Logic Levels** — **1 (TRUE / 24 V DC)** and **0 (FALSE / 0 V DC)**. KTU accepts both positive and negative logic conventions but expects explicit declaration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Ladder Logic (LD) — Detailed Theoretical Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Structural Anatomy

A Ladder Diagram consists of:
- **Two vertical rails**: The left rail represents the "L" (live/hot) conductor at logic 1; the right rail represents the "N" (neutral) conductor at logic 0.
- **Horizontal rungs**: Each rung represents one Boolean equation evaluated during a single scan.
- **Contacts** (input conditions) placed on the rungs:
  - **Normally Open (NO)** contact — Symbol: `—| |—`. Conducts (closes) only when the associated Boolean variable is **TRUE / 1**.
  - **Normally Closed (NC)** contact — Symbol: `—|/|—`. Conducts only when the associated Boolean variable is **FALSE / 0**.
- **Coils** (output actions) placed at the right end of the rung:
  - **Standard Output Coil** — Symbol: `—( )—`. Energizes the output when rung continuity is TRUE.
  - **Set Coil (S)** — Symbol: `—(S)—`. Latches the output ON; remains ON even if the rung becomes FALSE.
  - **Reset Coil (R)** — Symbol: `—(R)—`. Unlatches the output; remains OFF until a Set coil re-energizes it.

### 2.1.2 Execution Logic (Why and How)

1. **Power-Flow Concept**: The CPU conceptually applies a virtual "voltage" at the left rail and traces whether it can reach the right rail through closed contacts. If continuity exists, the output coil energizes.
2. **Series-AND, Parallel-OR**: Series-connected contacts on a rung represent the **AND (·)** operation. Two parallel branches represent the **OR (+)** operation. This is the foundational equivalence used by KTU to test Boolean-to-Ladder conversion.
3. **Scan-Cycle Evaluation**: The PLC evaluates rungs **top-to-bottom**, left-to-right within each rung, in every scan cycle. This deterministic order is critical for repeatable behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why rungs cannot be reordered in a deterministic safety program:** Reordering can change the state of internal memory bits (e.g., latches) within the same scan, leading to race conditions. KTU exam questions frequently test this by asking students to identify "incorrect rung placement" in a process interlock.

---

## 2.2 Function Block Diagram (FBD) — Detailed Theoretical Decomposition

### 2.2.1 Structural Anatomy

- **Function Blocks** are rectangular graphical elements with:
  - **Input pins** on the left side (e.g., `IN1`, `IN2`, `EN`).
  - **Output pins** on the right side (e.g., `OUT`, `ENO`).
  - A **block name inside the rectangle** (e.g., `AND`, `OR_TMR`, `PID_01`).
- **Signal Flow Lines** connect an output pin of one block to one or more input pins of downstream blocks. A single output can fan out to multiple inputs (free fan-out).
- **Standard IEC 61131-3 Function Blocks**: `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, `XOR`, `TON` (Timer ON), `TOF` (Timer OFF), `TP` (Pulse Timer), `CTU` (Count Up), `CTD` (Count Down), `RS` / `SR` (Bistables), `PID`, `RAMP`, `HYSTERESIS`.

### 2.2.2 Execution Logic

1. **Data-Driven Evaluation**: Unlike Ladder Logic's power-flow metaphor, FBD uses **data dependency**. A block is evaluated only when all its inputs have valid data from upstream blocks.
2. **Left-to-Right Signal Flow**: Although evaluation order is technically determined by data dependency, KTU convention assumes left-to-right reading.
3. **Feedback Loops**: A block's output can be wired back to its own input (or to an upstream block) to form a closed loop. Such loops require an **explicit initial value** to prevent algebraic loops at the first scan.

> [!NOTE]
> **FBD vs LD — KTU Board Differentiation:** Ladder is **scan-ordered** (rung-by-rung), while FBD is **dataflow-ordered** (block-by-block based on inputs). The examiner will penalize students who treat them as fully interchangeable without understanding the evaluation-order distinction.

---

## 2.3 Sequential Function Chart (SFC) — Detailed Theoretical Decomputation

### 2.3.1 Structural Anatomy

An SFC program consists of three primitive elements:

1. **Step** — Represented as a **square block** containing a unique step number (e.g., `Step_1`, `S2`). Each step is associated with one or more **Actions**.
2. **Transition** — Represented as a **short horizontal bar** placed on the link between two steps. It contains a **Boolean condition** (the "guard" or "enabling condition").
3. **Action** — A rectangular block attached to a step (on the right side). Actions can be of types: `N` (non-stored), `S` (set/stored), `R` (reset), `L` (time-limited), `D` (time-delayed), `P` (pulse), `SD` (stored + time-delayed), `DS` (delayed + stored), `SL` (stored + time-limited).

### 2.3.2 Execution Logic

1. **Initial Step** — A double-bordered square, activated automatically at the first scan or upon a cold restart.
2. **Activation Rule**: A step is *active* when all transitions downstream of it evaluate to TRUE. A transition is *cleared* (allowed to fire) only when its preceding step is active and its Boolean condition is TRUE.
3. **Divergences and Convergences**:
   - **Single Sequence** — Linear step → transition → step chain.
   - **Selection Divergence (OR)** — Multiple alternative branches; only one is chosen based on which transition fires first.
   - **Parallel Divergence (AND)** — Horizontal synchronization bar; all branches activate simultaneously.
   - **Parallel Convergence (AND)** — Horizontal synchronization bar; transition fires only when all upstream steps are active.
4. **GRAFCET Heritage**: SFC inherits its notation from **IEC 848 (GRAFCET — Graphe Fonctionnel de Commande Étape-Transition)**, with IEC 61131-3 adding the action-qualifier extensions.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Substitute for Formula Table)

Because this topic is descriptive rather than analytical, the KTU "cheat sheet" takes the form of a **comparative parameter table**. Memorize every cell — these are the *exact* descriptors the board examiner expects.

| Parameter | Ladder Diagram (LD) | Function Block Diagram (FBD) | Sequential Function Chart (SFC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IEC Reference** | IEC 61131-3 §4.4 | IEC 61131-3 §4.5 | IEC 61131-3 §4.6 (from IEC 848) |
| **Visual Metaphor** | Electrical relay schematic | Signal flow / circuit diagram | Petri net / state machine |
| **Execution Order** | Top-to-bottom rung scan | Data-flow dependency | Step transition order |
| **Best Suited For** | Boolean interlock logic | Continuous / analog control | Sequential batch processes |
| **Primary User** | Maintenance electrician | Control engineer | Process engineer |
| **Basic Element** | Contact + Coil | Function block | Step + Transition |
| **Logic Operation** | Series = AND, Parallel = OR | Block instance (AND/OR/etc.) | Transition boolean guard |
| **Action Mechanism** | Energize coil | Assign block output value | Attach action to step |
| **Loopback Allowed** | Via latching coil (S/R) | Direct feedback line | Not applicable (state-based) |
| **Equivalent Boolean Form** | Direct series/parallel synthesis | Direct gate network | Step activation equations |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | Very High (3-4 questions/module) | High (1-2 questions/module) | High (1-2 questions/module) |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Ladder Logic** dominates in **discrete manufacturing** (automotive assembly, packaging lines, conveyor systems) because plant-floor electricians can read and troubleshoot it without software.
- **FBD** dominates in **process control loops** (PID-based temperature, pressure, and flow control) because it modularly represents the analog control function as a single block.
- **SFC** dominates in **batch process automation** (chemical reactors, pharmaceutical batch plants, traffic light controllers) because the steps-and-transitions model maps 1:1 onto ISA-88 batch recipes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World KTU Exam Tip:** Most KTU theory questions on this topic set up a real industrial process (e.g., "a water tank with two pumps, two level switches, and an alarm") and ask the student to express it in any one of the three languages. The examiner awards full marks *only* if the language-specific notation is correct, not just the underlying logic.
