# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**List any THREE standard programming languages defined by IEC 61131-3 and classify them as textual or graphical.**

**Model Answer:**
The IEC 61131-3 standard defines the following five programming languages:
1. **Ladder Diagram (LD)** — *Graphical* — based on relay ladder logic schematics.
2. **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** — *Graphical* — based on interconnected function blocks carrying data signals.
3. **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** — *Graphical* — based on steps, transitions, and directed links for sequential processes.
4. **Structured Text (ST)** — *Textual* — a high-level language similar to PASCAL.
5. **Instruction List (IL)** — *Textual* — a low-level assembly-like language.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Differentiate between SFC Selection Divergence and Simultaneous Divergence with appropriate symbols.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Selection Divergence (OR) | Simultaneous Divergence (AND) |
|---------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Symbol | **Single horizontal bar** | **Double horizontal bar** |
| Activation | Only **ONE** branch is activated based on the transition condition | **ALL** branches are activated simultaneously in the same scan |
| Use case | Choosing between mutually exclusive process paths (e.g., HEAT vs COOL mode) | Running parallel processes (e.g., STIR A and STIR B together) |
| Convergence | Single-bar convergence: first branch to arrive continues | Double-bar convergence: system waits until ALL branches arrive |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice A]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **fundamental elements of a Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** as per IEC 61131-3. List the action qualifiers `N`, `S`, `R`, `L`, `D`, and `P` with their meanings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A chemical mixing tank process has the following sequence: **FILL** the tank with liquid A until level reaches 80%, then **ADD** liquid B for 10 seconds, then **STIR** for 30 seconds, then **DRAIN** until level reaches 5%. The cycle repeats automatically. Draw the complete SFC diagram for this process, label all steps, transitions, and actions, and write the step state equations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

The fundamental elements of an SFC as per IEC 61131-3 are:

1. **Step** — A rectangle representing a stable state of the process. Each step has a Boolean flag that is TRUE when the step is active.
2. **Initial Step** — A rectangle with a **double border**, representing the step that becomes active at program start (cold restart or warm restart).
3. **Transition** — A small **horizontal line** on the directed link, representing a Boolean condition that controls progression from one step to the next.
4. **Action Block** — A rectangle attached to a step, containing the instructions executed while the step is active. May include qualifiers.
5. **Directed Link** — A line with an arrow showing the direction of sequence flow.

**Action Qualifiers:**

| Qualifier | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| `N` | **Non-stored:** Action active only while the step is active. |
| `S` | **Stored (Set):** Action latched TRUE when step becomes active, remains TRUE even after step deactivates. |
| `R` | **Reset:** Clears an `S`-latched action. |
| `L` | **Time-Limited:** Action active for at most N time units or until step deactivates, whichever is first. |
| `D` | **Time-Delayed:** Action becomes active only after the step has been active for N time units. |
| `P` | **Pulse:** Action executes for one scan cycle when the step becomes active. |

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**SFC Diagram (textual description for exam):**

The process is modelled as a **linear SFC with four steps** and **four transitions**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sequence:} \quad & S_0^{\text{INIT}} \xrightarrow{T_1} S_1^{\text{FILL}} \xrightarrow{T_2} S_2^{\text{ADD\_B}} \xrightarrow{T_3} S_3^{\text{STIR}} \xrightarrow{T_4} S_4^{\text{DRAIN}} \xrightarrow{T_4} S_0^{\text{INIT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step Definitions and Actions:**

| Step | Name | Action | Action Qualifier |
|------|------|--------|------------------|
| $S_0$ | INITIAL | No action (idle) | — |
| $S_1$ | FILL | Open Valve_A | `N` (non-stored) |
| $S_2$ | ADD_B | Open Valve_B; Start Timer_T2 (10 s) | `N` |
| $S_3$ | STIR | Start Motor_Stirrer; Start Timer_T3 (30 s) | `N` |
| $S_4$ | DRAIN | Open Valve_Drain | `N` |

**Transition Conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: \text{START\_PB} = 1 \quad \text{(manual start)} \\
T_2 &: \text{Level\_Sensor} \geq 80\% \quad \text{(tank level reached)} \\
T_3 &: \text{Timer\_T2.ET} \geq 10\text{ s} \\
T_4 &: \text{Timer\_T3.ET} \geq 30\text{ s} \\
T_5 &: \text{Level\_Sensor} \leq 5\% \quad \text{(tank drained)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step State Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1(t+1) &= \big[S_0(t) \land T_1(t)\big] \lor \big[S_1(t) \land \neg T_2(t)\big] \\
S_2(t+1) &= \big[S_1(t) \land T_2(t)\big] \lor \big[S_2(t) \land \neg T_3(t)\big] \\
S_3(t+1) &= \big[S_2(t) \land T_3(t)\big] \lor \big[S_3(t) \land \neg T_4(t)\big] \\
S_4(t+1) &= \big[S_3(t) \land T_4(t)\big] \lor \big[S_4(t) \land \neg T_5(t)\big] \\
S_0(t+1) &= \big[S_4(t) \land T_5(t)\big] \lor \big[S_0(t) \land \neg T_1(t)\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes on SFC Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting action qualifiers** — KTU examiners specifically check whether the student has mentioned qualifiers like `N`, `S`, `D`, `L` for action blocks. Omitting them costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing single-bar (OR) and double-bar (AND) divergence** — Drawing the wrong symbol type is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Writing transition as constant `1`** — This is a **3-mark penalty** as it indicates the student has not understood the SFC firing rule.
> 4. **Not showing the loop-back from the last step to the initial step** — For a repetitive process, the loop arrow is mandatory.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3 Internal Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** as per IEC 61131-3. Discuss the roles of standard functions, function blocks, and the EN/ENO mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design an FBD program to implement the following equation and explain how the data flows through the network:

$$
Y = \frac{(A \cdot B) + C}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{with a 2-second output delay (use TON)}
$$

**Inputs:** `A = 5.0`, `B = 3.0`, `C = 7.0`, `D = 16.0`. Compute the expected output $Y$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Structure of FBD:**

An FBD program is composed of:
- **Standard Functions:** Built-in elementary operations defined by IEC 61131-3 (e.g., `ADD`, `MUL`, `GT`, `AND`, `OR`, `SEL`, `MUX`). They have a fixed function and operate on standard data types (`BOOL`, `INT`, `REAL`).
- **Function Blocks (FB):** Reusable software units with internal memory (e.g., `TON`, `CTU`, `SR`, `PID`). They retain state between scans.
- **Connections:** Lines carrying signals between blocks. The signal is a variable value; the connection has a **single source** but may fan out to **multiple destinations**.
- **Negation bubbles** (small circles) invert boolean signals at a block input.
- **Connectors** (small labelled circles) allow linking across networks without physical wire crossings.

**EN/ENO Mechanism:**

- `EN` (Enable Input): A boolean input that controls whether the block executes. If `EN = FALSE`, the block is skipped, and outputs retain their previous value.
- `ENO` (Enable Output): A boolean output that is TRUE only if `EN = TRUE` AND the block executed without error. Used to chain execution control: an upstream block's `ENO` can feed the next block's `EN` so that blocks execute sequentially only when the previous succeeded.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**FBD Network Design (textual block list):**

1. **Block 1 — MUL:** $P_1 = A \times B$
2. **Block 2 — ADD:** $P_2 = P_1 + C$
3. **Block 3 — SQRT:** $P_3 = \sqrt{D}$
4. **Block 4 — DIV:** $Y_{\text{raw}} = P_2 / P_3$
5. **Block 5 — TON:** Apply 2-second delay; output $Y = Y_{\text{raw}}$ after 2 s of stable input.

**Numerical Computation (Inputs given):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= A \cdot B = 5.0 \times 3.0 = 15.0 \\
P_2 &= P_1 + C = 15.0 + 7.0 = 22.0 \\
P_3 &= \sqrt{D} = \sqrt{16.0} = 4.0 \\
Y_{\text{raw}} &= \frac{P_2}{P_3} = \frac{22.0}{4.0} = 5.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output (after 2-second TON delay):**

$$
Y = 5.5
$$

(after the TON block's `ET` has reached 2 seconds of continuous TRUE input).

**Data-Flow Trace:**

$$
A,B \xrightarrow{\text{MUL}} P_1 \xrightarrow{\text{ADD}} P_2 \xrightarrow{\text{DIV}} Y_{\text{raw}} \xrightarrow{\text{TON}} Y
$$

$$
C \xrightarrow{\phantom{xxxxxxx}} \text{ADD input 2}
$$

$$
D \xrightarrow{\text{SQRT}} P_3 \xrightarrow{\phantom{xxxxxxx}} \text{DIV input 2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes on FBD Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong block selection** — Using `MUL` where `ADD` is required (or vice versa) costs full marks for that sub-part. Always re-read the equation carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting operator precedence** — For $(A \cdot B + C) / \sqrt{D}$, multiplication binds before addition. The student must compute $A \cdot B$ first, then add $C$, then divide by $\sqrt{D}$. Reversing the order gives a wrong numerical answer.
> 3. **Not specifying data types** — IEC 61131-3 mandates explicit data types (`REAL`, `INT`, `BOOL`). Using wrong data types (e.g., `INT` for SQRT of 16) causes implicit conversion errors in some PLCs and is penalized.
> 4. **Confusing FBD with LD** — In FBD, there are NO power rails and NO contact/coil symbols. Drawing contacts in an FBD answer is an instant 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **IEC 61131-3** defines **5** standard PLC languages: LD, FBD, SFC, ST, IL.
- ✅ **SFC** is built from **Steps, Transitions, Directed Links, Actions, and Branches**.
- ✅ **Initial Step** uses a **double-bordered rectangle**; ordinary steps use a single border.
- ✅ **Selection Divergence (OR)** uses a **single horizontal bar**; only one branch fires.
- ✅ **Simultaneous Divergence (AND)** uses a **double horizontal bar**; all branches fire in parallel.
- ✅ A transition is fireable when **all preceding steps are active AND the transition condition is TRUE**.
- ✅ Action qualifiers: `N` (non-stored), `S` (set/latched), `R` (reset), `L` (time-limited), `D` (time-delayed), `P` (pulse).
- ✅ **FBD** is **data-flow oriented** — signals flow from inputs (left) to outputs (right).
- ✅ FBD uses **standard functions** (e.g., AND, ADD) and **reusable function blocks** (e.g., TON, PID).
- ✅ **EN/ENO** mechanism: EN controls execution; ENO propagates success/error to next block.
- ✅ **FBD for continuous control** (PID, scaling, math); **SFC for sequential/batch control** (state machines, recipes, traffic lights).
- ✅ SFC and FBD are **complementary** — modern PLCs allow embedding FBDs inside SFC step actions.
- ✅ SFC state equation: $S_i(t+1) = [S_{\text{pred}}(t) \land T_{\text{pred}}(t)] \lor [S_i(t) \land \neg T_i(t)]$.
- ✅ SR Bistable (Set-Dominant) equation: $Q(t+1) = S1(t) \lor [Q(t) \land \neg R(t)]$.
- ✅ TON timer: $Q = 1$ when $ET \geq PT$ while $IN = 1$.
- ✅ **NEVER** use a constant `1` (TRUE) as a transition condition — it causes scan-cycle lockup.
- ✅ IEC 61131-3 is the **ONLY** globally accepted PLC programming standard — applies across Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Mitsubishi, Schneider, ABB, and others.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networking PLC: Using RS232, RS485
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Networking PLC: Using RS232, RS485 # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to PLC Networking In modern industrial automation, a single PLC is rarely sufficient. Complex processes require multiple PLCs, HMIs, drives, and sensors communicating seamlessly. **PLC Networking** refers to the systematic interconnection of these devices using standardized serial communication protocols so that data (inputs, outputs, setpoints, alarms) can be exchanged reliably across the plant floor. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Networking of PLCs* involves interconnecting PLCs using standardized serial communication interfaces — primarily **RS-232** for short-distance point-to-point links and **RS-485** for longer-distance, multi-drop industrial networks — to enable distributed control, peer-to-peer data exchange, and centralized supervisory monitoring. --- ## 1.2 What is RS-232? **RS-232 (Recommended Standard 232)** is a legacy serial communication standard introduced by the **EIA (Electronic Industries Alliance)** in 1962. It defines the electrical, functional, and mechanical characteristics of a point-to-point serial interface between a **DTE (Data Terminal Equipment)** — such as a PLC or computer — and a **DCE (Data Communication Equipment)** — such as a modem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition — RS-232** > A single-ended, voltage-defined, **point-to-point** serial interface operating with logic levels between **+3 V to +25 V (Logic 0 / SPACE)** and **−3 V to −25 V (Logic 1 / MARK)**. Maximum recommended cable length is **15 meters (≈ 50 ft)** at **20 kbps**. ### Conceptual Analogy — RS-232 Imagine two people talking face-to-face across a small desk. One person (the PLC) shouts a message, and the other person (PC/HMI) hears it directly through a single tube (the wire). The message is one-directional in voltage terms and travels a short distance only. If you tried to stretch that tube across a football field, the voice would become too weak and noisy to understand. **RS-232 is exactly that "short desk conversation" — fast, simple, but limited in reach and limited to two devices.** --- ## 1.3 What is RS-485? **RS-485 (also known as EIA-485 or TIA-485)** is a robust differential serial communication standard designed specifically for **multi-drop, long-distance, electrically noisy industrial environments**. Unlike RS-232, it uses **differential voltage signaling** across a **twisted pair** of wires, which provides excellent rejection of common-mode noise. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition — RS-485** > A **differential**, **balanced**, **multi-drop** serial interface operating over a **twisted pair**, supporting up to **32 standard unit loads (or 256 with modern transceivers)** on a single bus, with cable lengths up to **1200 meters (≈ 4000 ft)** at lower baud rates. ### Conceptual Analogy — RS-485 Picture a conference call in a noisy factory. Instead of one person shouting into a single microphone (RS-232), imagine each speaker has **two microphones placed slightly apart**, and the receiver measures the *difference* between them. Background factory noise hits both microphones equally, so the difference cancels out — only the actual voice creates a difference. This is **differential signaling**. Now imagine 32 people (PLCs/drives/HMIs) all sharing the same two-wire line, taking turns to speak. **That is RS-485 — many devices, long cable, one shared "differential" conversation.** --- ## 1.4 Why Networking PLCs? (Engineering Motivation) | Need | How RS-232 / RS-485 Solves It | |------|-------------------------------| | Share I/O data between PLCs | Peer-to-peer or master-slave messaging | | Connect HMIs / SCADA | Direct serial port to PLC | | Replace expensive parallel wiring | Single twisted pair carries dozens of signals | | Operate in electrically noisy plants | RS-485 differential mode rejects noise | | Extend I/O over long distances | RS-485 up to 1200 m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking *why RS-485 is preferred over RS-232 in industrial plants*. Always mention: **(1) Noise immunity from differential signaling, (2) Multi-drop support (32+ nodes), (3) Longer cable reach (1200 m vs 15 m), (4) Half-duplex capability on 2 wires**. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 RS-232 — Detailed Electrical & Protocol Analysis ### 2.1.1 Electrical Characteristics RS-232 uses **single-ended signaling**, meaning the signal voltage is measured between the **signal line** and the **signal ground**. The logic levels are *inverted* with respect to TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) voltages. | Parameter | Value / Range | |-----------|---------------| | Logic 1 (MARK / OFF) | **−3 V to −25 V** | | Logic 0 (SPACE / ON) | **+3 V to +25 V** | | Maximum open-circuit voltage | **±25 V** | | Short-circuit current limit | **±500 mA** | | Standard connectors | **DB-9, DB-25** | | Communication modes | **Full-duplex** (separate TX, RX, RTS, CTS) | | Max cable length | **15 m** at **20 kbps** | | Max baud rate | **Up to 115.2 kbps** (short distances) | | Drivers per line | **1 driver only** | | Receivers per line | **1 receiver only** | ### 2.1.2 RS-232 Frame Structure (Asynchronous) A typical RS-232 character frame looks like this: $$ \text{Idle (1)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Start bit (0)} \;\rightarrow\; D_0, D_1, \dots, D_7 \;\rightarrow\; \text{[Optional Parity]} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Stop bit(s) (1)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Idle (1)} $$ For 1 start, 8 data, no parity, 1 stop bit → **10 bits per character** total. ### 2.1.3 Key RS-232 Handshake Signals - **TX (Transmit)** – Data from DTE to DCE - **RX (Receive)** – Data from DCE to DTE - **RTS (Request To Send)** – DTE requests permission to transmit - **CTS (Clear To Send)** – DCE grants permission - **DTR (Data Terminal Ready)** – DTE is ready - **DSR (Data Set Ready)** – DCE is ready - **DCD (Data Carrier Detect)** – Carrier detected - **RI (Ring Indicator)** – Incoming call (modems) > [!NOTE] > For PLC-to-PLC or PLC-to-HMI links, software handshaking (XON/XOFF) or no handshaking is common. Hardware handshaking is used mainly with modems. --- ## 2.2 RS-485 — Detailed Electrical & Protocol Analysis ### 2.2.1 Differential Signaling Explained RS-485 uses two wires, conventionally labeled **A (inverting / −)** and **B (non-inverting / +)**. The receiver looks at: $$ V_{\text{diff}} = V_A - V_B $$ | Logic State | Condition | |-------------|-----------| | **MARK (1)** | $V_A < V_B$ → $V_{\text{diff}} < -200\;\text{mV}$ | | **SPACE (0)** | $V_A > V_B$ → $V_{\text{diff}} > +200\;\text{mV}$ | The **±200 mV threshold** is critical — it provides the noise margin that lets RS-485 survive industrial EMI. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The ±200 mV hysteresis** ensures that even if both wires pick up **±1 V of common-mode noise** (equal on both A and B), the *difference* $V_A - V_B$ is unaffected. This is why RS-485 works in VFD-rich plants. ### 2.2.2 Bus Topology and Termination RS-485 must be wired as a **daisy-chain bus** — never as a star. - Two termination resistors (typically **120 Ω each**) are placed at the **two extreme ends** of the bus, matching the cable's characteristic impedance. - Stub lengths (drops from main bus to each node) should be kept short: **< 1/10 of the signal rise time × propagation velocity**. ### 2.2.3 Key RS-485 Parameters | Parameter | Value / Range | |-----------|---------------| | Signaling | **Differential, balanced** | | Logic voltage on bus | **±1.5 V to ±6 V** (driver output) | | Receiver sensitivity | **±200 mV minimum** | | Common-mode voltage range | **−7 V to +12 V** | | Max nodes (standard) | **32 unit loads** | | Max nodes (modern) | **256 with high-impedance transceivers** | | Max cable length | **1200 m** at **100 kbps** | | Max data rate | **Up to 10 Mbps** at short distances | | Communication modes | **Half-duplex (2-wire)** or **Full-duplex (4-wire)** | | Connector | Often **screw terminals** or **DB-9** | | Cable | **Twisted pair**, characteristic impedance **120 Ω** | --- ## 2.3 The Cable-Length vs Baud-Rate Trade-off Both RS-232 and RS-485 exhibit an inverse relationship between maximum reliable baud rate and cable length. The empirical rule for RS-485 is: $$ \text{Max Cable Length (m)} \times \text{Baud Rate (bps)} \le 10^{8} $$ For RS-232, the rule is tighter: $$ \text{Max Cable Length (m)} \times \text{Baud Rate (bps)} \le 3 \times 10^{5} $$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision | Formula / Rule | Expression | Use Case | |----------------|------------|----------| | RS-485 length-rate product | $L \times B \le 10^{8}$ | Maximum cable for given baud | | RS-232 length-rate product | $L \times B \le 3 \times 10^{5}$ | Maximum cable for given baud | | Differential voltage | $V_{\text{diff}} = V_A - V_B$ | Logic decision threshold | | Logic 1 condition | $V_{\text{diff}} < -200\;\text{mV}$ | Receiver sees MARK | | Logic 0 condition | $V_{\text{diff}} > +200\;\text{mV}$ | Receiver sees SPACE | | Noise margin | $\vert V_{\text{diff}} \vert > 200\;\text{mV}$ | Reliable detection | | Termination resistance | $R_t = Z_0 = 120\;\Omega$ | Match cable impedance | | Stub length limit | $L_{\text{stub}} < 0.1 \cdot t_r \cdot v_p$ | Avoid reflections | | Frame time (1 char) | $T_f = \dfrac{N_{\text{bits}}}{B}$ | Time per character | | Unit-load definition | $1\;\text{UL} = 12\;\text{k}\Omega$ input impedance | Max 32 UL per bus | | Max nodes (modern) | $N_{\max} = \dfrac{12\;\text{k}\Omega}{R_{\text{input}}}$ | Up to 256 nodes | > [!NOTE] > In the formula table above, $\vert x \vert$ is written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The character used is the vertical bar in math mode only. ### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility - **Automotive assembly lines** — RS-485 networks tie together PLCs controlling robotic welding cells, vision systems, and conveyor PLCs over hundreds of meters. - **Water treatment plants** — Field PLCs spread over a kilometer are linked via RS-485 to a central SCADA. - **Building automation (BACnet MS-TP)** — Uses RS-485 as the physical layer. - **Modbus RTU** — The world's most popular industrial protocol (Modbus RTU) runs natively over RS-485. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Cable Length for RS-485 at a Given Baud Rate **Problem Statement:** A factory requires PLCs to communicate over RS-485 at **115200 bps**. What is the maximum cable length that guarantees signal integrity? **Step 1 — Identify the rule:** The empirical RS-485 length-rate product limit states: $$ L \times B \le 10^{8} $$ where $L$ is length in metres and $B$ is the baud rate in bits per second. **Step 2 — Rearrange for length:** $$ L_{\max} = \frac{10^{8}}{B} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $B = 115200$ bps:** $$ L_{\max} = \frac{10^{8}}{1.152 \times 10^{5}} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate:** $$ L_{\max} = 868.05\;\text{m} $$ **Step 5 — Round down to a practical value:** $$ L_{\max} \approx 850\;\text{m} $$ **Conclusion:** At 115200 bps, the maximum reliable cable length is approximately **850 m**, well below the absolute 1200 m limit, ensuring margin for cable quality and environmental effects. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frame Transmission Time **Problem Statement:** A PLC sends 12 bytes over RS-232 at **9600 bps**, using 1 start, 8 data, 1 stop, no parity. How long does transmission take? **Step 1 — Bits per character:** With 1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 stop bit = **10 bits** per character. **Step 2 — Total bits for 12 bytes:** $$ N_{\text{bits}} = 12 \times 10 = 120\;\text{bits} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the time formula:** $$ T_f = \frac{N_{\text{bits}}}{B} = \frac{120}{9600} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate:** $$ T_f = 0.0125\;\text{s} = 12.5\;\text{ms} $$ **Conclusion:** Each full message frame takes **12.5 ms**, which is the basis for calculating PLC scan and response timing. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Nodes Calculation **Problem Statement:** A new RS-485 transceiver has an input impedance of **48 kΩ**. How many such transceivers can be placed on a single bus, assuming the standard allows **one unit load (1 UL = 12 kΩ input impedance)**? **Step 1 — Unit load definition:** $$ 1\;\text{UL} = \frac{1}{12\;\text{k}\Omega} = 83.33\;\mu\text{S} $$ **Step 2 — Load presented by one new transceiver:** $$ \text{Load} = \frac{1}{48\;\text{k}\Omega} = 20.83\;\mu\text{S} = 0.25\;\text{UL} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum nodes under 32 UL budget:** $$ N_{\max} = \frac{32\;\text{UL}}{0.25\;\text{UL per node}} = 128\;\text{nodes} $$ **Step 4 — Practical ceiling from standard:** The RS-485 standard originally allowed **32 UL**, but modern drivers often allow **256 UL**. Therefore: $$ N_{\max}^{\text{modern}} = \frac{256}{0.25} = 1024\;\text{nodes (theoretical)} $$ $$ N_{\max}^{\text{standard}} = 128\;\text{nodes (practical, from 32 UL budget)} $$ **Conclusion:** Up to **128 transceivers** of 48 kΩ can be reliably connected on a single RS-485 bus, well beyond the legacy 32-device limit. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stub Length Limit **Problem Statement:** A RS-485 driver has a rise time $t_r = 10\;\text{ns}$ and the cable propagation velocity is $v_p = 2 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$ (≈ 0.67 c). What is the maximum stub length? **Step 1 — Empirical rule (1/10 of rise-time distance):** $$ L_{\text{stub,max}} = \frac{t_r \cdot v_p}{10} $$ **Step 2 — Compute rise-time distance:** $$ t_r \cdot v_p = (10 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{s})(2 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}) $$ $$ t_r \cdot v_p = 2\;\text{m} $$ **Step 3 — Divide by 10:** $$ L_{\text{stub,max}} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2\;\text{m} = 20\;\text{cm} $$ **Conclusion:** Each stub (drop) from the main RS-485 bus to a node must be kept **shorter than 20 cm** to prevent signal reflections that corrupt data. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — RS-232 vs RS-485 vs RS-422 | Feature | RS-232 | RS-422 | **RS-485** | |---------|--------|--------|------------| | Signaling | Single-ended | Differential | **Differential** | | Drivers per line | 1 | 1 | **32 (multi-drop)** | | Receivers per line | 1 | 10 | **32** | | Max cable length | 15 m | 1200 m | **1200 m** | | Max data rate | 20 kbps (over 15 m) | 10 Mbps (over 12 m) | **10 Mbps (over 12 m)** | | Duplex mode | Full | Full | **Half (2-wire) or Full (4-wire)** | | Voltage on bus | ±25 V | ±6 V differential | **±6 V differential** | | Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent | **Excellent** | | Connector | DB-9 / DB-25 | Screw / DB | **Screw / DB-9** | | Typical use | PC-COM port | Legacy point-to-point | **Industrial multi-drop** | > [!NOTE] > **RS-422 vs RS-485:** RS-422 supports **1 driver + up to 10 receivers** only (no true multi-drop transmission). RS-485 enables **true bi-directional multi-drop** because each node can be either driver or receiver. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 RS-232 Point-to-Point Link Between PLC and HMI ```mermaid flowchart LR PLC1["PLC CPU
DB-9 Port"] -->|TX Pin 3| RX2["RX Pin 2
HMI"] RX2 -->|TX Pin 3| PLC1 PLC1 -.->|GND Pin 5| RX2 PLC1 -.->|RTS Pin 7| RX2 RX2 -.->|CTS Pin 8| PLC1 style PLC1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style RX2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#663300,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** Only **two nodes** can be on an RS-232 link — PLC acts as DTE, HMI as DCE. Handshake lines (RTS/CTS) optionally manage flow. Cable must be a **null-modem** or **straight-through** depending on DTE/DCE roles. --- ## 4.2 RS-485 Multi-Drop PLC Network (Daisy-Chain Topology) ```mermaid flowchart LR MASTER["Master PLC
Node Address 1
(Master / Scanner)"]:::master PLC2["Slave PLC 2
Node 2"]:::slave PLC3["VFD Drive
Node 3"]:::slave PLC4["Remote I/O
Node 4"]:::slave PLC5["HMI Panel
Node 5"]:::slave TERM1["120 Ohm
Termination"]:::term TERM2["120 Ohm
Termination"]:::term MASTER ---|"Wire A"| PLC2 PLC2 ---|"Wire A"| PLC3 PLC3 ---|"Wire A"| PLC4 PLC4 ---|"Wire A"| PLC5 PLC5 --- TERM2 TERM1 --- MASTER classDef master fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 classDef slave fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef term fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** A single twisted pair (with ground) interconnects all devices in a **daisy chain**. Both ends carry **120 Ω termination resistors** to suppress reflections. The Master polls each slave using a protocol such as **Modbus RTU**. --- ## 4.3 RS-485 Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex Wiring ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HalfDuplex["Half Duplex - 2 Wire"] HD1["Node 1 TXRX"] ---|Pair| HD2["Node 2 TXRX"] end subgraph FullDuplex["Full Duplex - 4 Wire"] FD1["Node 1 TX"] ---|Pair 1| FD2["Node 2 RX"] FD2 ---|Pair 2| FD1 end style HalfDuplex fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000 style FullDuplex fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** Half-duplex uses **one twisted pair** shared for both transmit and receive — devices take turns. Full-duplex uses **two twisted pairs** for simultaneous TX/RX. Most PLC networks use half-duplex for economy. --- ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — PLC Communication Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB APP["Application Layer
Modbus RTU / DF1 / EtherNet/IP"]:::app DATA["Data Link Layer
Addressed Frames
CRC Checking"]:::data PHYS["Physical Layer
RS-485 Driver
Differential Pair
120 Ohm Termination"]:::phys CABLE["Cable Plant
Twisted Pair
Shielded in Noisy Areas"]:::cable APP --> DATA --> PHYS --> CABLE classDef app fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000 classDef data fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000 classDef phys fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 classDef cable fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** RS-485 is the **physical layer only**. Higher layers (Modbus RTU, DF1, etc.) define addressing, error checking, and message structure. RS-485 alone is not a complete protocol — it is the *electrical highway* on which protocols travel. --- ## 4.5 Signal Levels Diagram — Single-Ended vs Differential ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RS232Sig["RS-232 Single Ended"] G1["Signal Wire
MARK -12V or
SPACE +12V"] end subgraph RS485Sig["RS-485 Differential"] A1["Wire A"] B1["Wire B"] DIFF["Vdiff = VA - VB
Less than -200mV = 1
Greater than 200mV = 0"] end A1 --- DIFF B1 --- DIFF style RS232Sig fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000 style RS485Sig fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** RS-232 has only one signal wire whose voltage relative to ground encodes logic. RS-485 measures the **voltage difference** between two wires, rejecting any noise that appears equally on both. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **List any three advantages of RS-485 over RS-232 for industrial PLC networking.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Multi-drop capability** — RS-485 supports up to 32 (or 256 with modern transceivers) devices on a single twisted pair, whereas RS-232 supports only a point-to-point link between 2 devices. *(1 mark)* 2. **Longer cable length** — RS-485 operates reliably up to **1200 m** at lower baud rates, compared to the **15 m** ceiling of RS-232. *(1 mark)* 3. **Superior noise immunity** — RS-485 uses **differential signaling** with a ±200 mV threshold, rejecting common-mode noise from VFDs, motors, and relays, while RS-232 uses single-ended signaling vulnerable to ground shifts. *(1 mark)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define "Unit Load" in RS-485. How is the maximum number of nodes on an RS-485 bus calculated?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Unit Load (UL)** in RS-485 is a measure of the DC load a transceiver presents to the bus, defined as an input impedance of **12 kΩ** (or equivalently, a load of 83.33 µS). The maximum number of nodes is calculated as: $$ N_{\max} = \frac{\text{Total UL Budget}}{\text{UL per Device}} $$ The standard RS-485 bus allows a total load of **32 UL**, hence with 1 UL per standard transceiver, **32 devices** may be connected. Modern transceivers (e.g., 48 kΩ input = 0.25 UL) allow up to **128 nodes** on the same 32 UL bus. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for calculation example)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **A) (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **electrical characteristics of RS-232**, including voltage levels, handshake signals, and frame format. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Voltage Levels (2 marks):** - Logic 1 (MARK) = −3 V to −25 V (typically −12 V) - Logic 0 (SPACE) = +3 V to +25 V (typically +12 V) - Inverted relative to TTL/CMOS logic - Single-ended: signal measured between line and signal ground **(ii) Handshake Signals (2 marks):** - **TX, RX** — Data lines - **RTS, CTS** — Hardware flow control - **DTR, DSR** — Device readiness - **DCD** — Carrier detect - **RI** — Ring indicator (modems) **(iii) Frame Format (2 marks):** A typical RS-232 character frame: $$ \underbrace{\text{Idle 1}}_{\text{prev}} \to \underbrace{\text{Start 0}}_{\text{1 bit}} \to \underbrace{D_0 D_1 \dots D_7}_{\text{8 data bits}} \to \underbrace{\text{[Parity]}}_{\text{optional}} \to \underbrace{\text{Stop 1}}_{\text{1 or 2 bits}} \to \text{Idle 1} $$ **(iv) Limitations (1 mark):** 15 m max cable, 1 driver, 1 receiver, poor noise immunity. **A) (b)** Compare **RS-232 and RS-485** in terms of signaling, cable length, maximum nodes, noise immunity, and typical applications. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** | Parameter | RS-232 | RS-485 | |-----------|--------|--------| | Signaling | Single-ended | Differential | | Cable length | 15 m | 1200 m | | Max nodes | 2 (point-to-point) | 32 (or up to 256) | | Noise immunity | Poor | Excellent (±200 mV threshold) | | Duplex mode | Full-duplex | Half or full-duplex | | Connector | DB-9 / DB-25 | Screw terminals / DB-9 | | Typical use | PC COM port, programming port | Industrial Modbus RTU networks | | Cable | Twisted pair optional | Twisted pair mandatory | | Termination | Not required | 120 Ω at each end | **Conclusion:** RS-485 dominates modern industrial PLC networking due to multi-drop, noise immunity, and distance. RS-232 remains useful for short programming links and legacy HMI connections. *(7 marks — 1 mark per row + 1 for conclusion)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **B) (a)** Explain the principle of **differential signaling** in RS-485 with a suitable diagram. Why is it more noise-immune than RS-232? *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Principle (3 marks):** In RS-485, data is transmitted as the **voltage difference** between two twisted wires A and B: $$ V_{\text{diff}} = V_A - V_B $$ A positive $V_{\text{diff}} > +200\;\text{mV}$ represents Logic 0 (SPACE); a negative $V_{\text{diff}} < -200\;\text{mV}$ represents Logic 1 (MARK). **Diagram (2 marks):** ``` +5V ──┐ ├──[120 Ω]── Term Node A ──────┤ PLC │ twisted pair │ Slave PLC Driver├──Wire A─────────┤Receiver ├──Wire B─────────┤ ──────┤ │ GND ──┘──[120 Ω]── Term Node B ``` **Noise Rejection Mechanism (2 marks):** Any external EMI (e.g., from a VFD) induces a voltage $V_n$ on **both** A and B equally. The receiver computes: $$ V_{\text{diff, observed}} = (V_A + V_n) - (V_B + V_n) = V_A - V_B $$ The noise **cancels out**. RS-232, being single-ended, has no such rejection — noise directly corrupts the signal relative to ground. --- **B) (b)** A factory needs **20 PLCs** distributed over **900 m** to communicate with a master PLC at **38400 bps** using RS-485. Check whether this configuration is feasible and justify the cable termination design. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Verify the length-rate product (2 marks):** $$ L \times B = 900 \times 38400 = 3.456 \times 10^{7} $$ Compare with limit $10^{8}$: $$ 3.456 \times 10^{7} < 10^{8}\;\checkmark $$ The configuration is **feasible** with comfortable margin. **Step 2 — Verify number of nodes (2 marks):** 20 PLCs < 32 UL limit ⇒ **Feasible** without high-impedance transceivers. **Step 3 — Termination design (2 marks):** - Use **120 Ω termination resistors** at the **two physical ends** of the daisy chain bus (typically at the master and the last remote PLC). - Cable type: **Shielded twisted pair (STP)** with characteristic impedance of **120 Ω**. - Stub length to each node should be **< 0.2 m** for 10 ns rise-time transceivers. - Connect shield to ground at **one end only** to prevent ground loops. **Step 4 — Protocol selection (1 mark):** Use **Modbus RTU** over RS-485 with master polling each slave at unique address 1–20. **Conclusion:** 20 PLCs over 900 m at 38400 bps is **feasible** with proper RS-485 wiring, 120 Ω terminations, and Modbus RTU protocol. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warnings — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing RS-422 with RS-485.** RS-422 allows **1 driver + 10 receivers**; RS-485 allows **multiple drivers** (multi-drop with collision avoidance). Don't write "RS-485 is the same as RS-422." > 2. **Forgetting termination resistors.** Examiners *love* asking why RS-485 needs 120 Ω at both ends. Mention **signal reflection and impedance matching** — not just "to prevent noise." > 3. **Wrong voltage levels.** RS-232 is **±12 V**, NOT 5 V. RS-485 differential is **±1.5 V to ±6 V**, NOT ±12 V. > 4. **Mixing up MAX cable lengths.** RS-232 = **15 m**, RS-485 = **1200 m**. Writing "RS-485 up to 15 m" is a guaranteed zero. > 5. **Forgetting stub length rule.** Always mention **stub < 1/10 of rise-time distance** for reflection-free operation. > 6. **Saying RS-485 is a "protocol."** It is a **physical layer standard only**. Pair it with Modbus RTU, DF1, or Profibus DP for a complete communication solution. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **RS-232** = **single-ended, ±12 V, point-to-point, 15 m, DB-9/DB-25, full-duplex, 1 driver + 1 receiver**. - **RS-485** = **differential, ±5 V across twisted pair, multi-drop, 1200 m, 32+ nodes, half/full-duplex**. - The differential threshold is **±200 mV** — this is the noise margin that makes RS-485 robust. - **Termination:** Two **120 Ω resistors** at the bus extremes (NOT at every node) to match cable impedance $Z_0 = 120\;\Omega$. - **Topology:** Strictly **daisy-chain**; never star; keep stubs short. - **Length-Baud product:** $L \times B \le 10^{8}$ for RS-485; $L \times B \le 3 \times 10^{5}$ for RS-232. - **Unit Load:** 1 UL = 12 kΩ input impedance. Standard bus budget = 32 UL → 32 standard nodes max. - **RS-485 alone is a physical layer**; pair it with **Modbus RTU** for a complete industrial network. - **Connectors:** RS-232 typically **DB-9**; RS-485 often **screw terminals** for rugged industrial use. - **Shielding:** Use shielded twisted pair (STP); ground shield at **one end only** to avoid ground loops. - **KTU Favourite Questions:** - Compare RS-232 and RS-485. - Explain differential signaling. - Why two 120 Ω terminations on RS-485? - What is a unit load? - Sketch a multi-drop RS-485 network with master and 3 slaves. - Calculate maximum cable length for a given baud rate.

topics.title: Protocols: Ethernet, Modbus, CANOpen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Protocols: Ethernet, Modbus, CANOpen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Communication Protocol in Industrial Automation?

A **communication protocol** in a Distributed Control System (DCS) or PLC network is a standardized set of **rules, message formats, and procedures** that govern the exchange of data between field devices, controllers, sensors, actuators, and supervisory stations (SCADA/HMI). In a PLC/DCS architecture, hundreds of heterogeneous devices from different vendors must "speak" a common digital language — the protocol is that language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examiner's Preferred Wording):**
> "An industrial communication protocol is a deterministic, layered set of rules defining the syntax, semantics, timing, and error-handling rules for data exchange between intelligent field devices in real-time automation networks."

### Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)
Imagine a busy international airport. Pilots, air traffic controllers, ground crew, and passengers all use **standardized English phraseology** (the protocol). Without it, a German pilot and a Japanese controller could not coordinate. In the same way, a Siemens PLC, a Schneider HMI, and an ABB drive must all agree on a **protocol** (Modbus, CANOpen, Ethernet/IP, etc.) to share data reliably within milliseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Industrial Protocols (as per KTU Module 3):**
> 1. **Ethernet Family** – High-speed, IT-converged, office-to-factory communication.
> 2. **Modbus** – The universal "Esperanto" of industrial devices; simple, master-slave, register-based.
> 3. **CANOpen** – Robust, multi-master, real-time bus derived from automotive CAN standard.

---

## 1.2 Ethernet (Industrial Ethernet)

### 1.2.1 Standard Ethernet
**Ethernet** is a family of **LAN (Local Area Network) technologies** defined by the **IEEE 802.3** standard. Originally developed by Xerox (1973) and standardized by DEC, Intel, and Xerox (the DIX standard), it uses the **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** MAC layer protocol. The classical data rate has evolved from **10 Mbps** to **100 Mbps (Fast Ethernet)**, **1 Gbps (Gigabit)**, and now **10 Gbps/100 Gbps** in data centers.

In automation, the same physical layers are reused but with deterministic enhancements — this gives rise to **Industrial Ethernet**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Industrial Ethernet (IE):**
> Industrial Ethernet refers to the use of standard Ethernet hardware, TCP/IP protocols, and IT infrastructure, augmented with **real-time extensions** to meet the determinism, robustness, and EMC (Electromagnetic Compatibility) requirements of factory-floor and process-control environments.

**Standard Speed & Cable Metrics for KTU Exam:**
| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| IEEE Standard | **802.3** |
| Speed (Classical) | **10/100 Mbps**, 1 Gbps |
| Max Segment (Twisted Pair) | **100 m** |
| Connector | **RJ-45** (industrial variants: M12, M8) |
| Max Nodes per Segment | **1024** (logical limit, practical ~256) |
| Frame Size | **64 – 1518 bytes** (tagged: 1522) |

### 1.2.2 Key Industrial Ethernet Variants (Mandatory for KTU)
- **EtherNet/IP (Ethernet Industrial Protocol)** – Developed by Rockwell (ODVA), uses CIP (Common Industrial Protocol) over TCP/UDP. Common in North America.
- **Profinet** – Developed by Siemens/PNO, real-time (RT) and isochronous real-time (IRT) classes. Dominant in Europe.
- **EtherCAT** – Developed by Beckhoff, "processing-on-the-fly" technique with cycle times < **100 µs**.
- **Modbus TCP** – Modbus protocol encapsulated inside TCP/IP (covered in 1.3).

### Conceptual Analogy for Industrial Ethernet
Think of **standard Ethernet as a city highway** — anyone can enter, but traffic jams (collisions) are possible. **Industrial Ethernet** is the same highway but with **dedicated lanes, traffic police, and priority ambulances** (real-time scheduling, QoS) so that critical messages (e.g., emergency stop) always reach the destination within a guaranteed time.

---

## 1.3 Modbus Protocol

**Modbus** is a **serial communication protocol** developed by **Modicon (Schneider Electric)** in **1979** for use with its PLCs. It has become the **de facto open standard** in industrial automation because it is:
- **Open** (royalty-free, public domain).
- **Simple** (only a handful of function codes).
- **Vendor-neutral** (supported by thousands of devices).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Modbus:**
> Modbus is an application-layer **master/slave (client/server)** messaging protocol positioned at **Layer 7 of the OSI model**, providing client/server communication between devices connected on different buses or networks.

### 1.3.1 The Three Variants of Modbus
| Variant | Physical Layer | Speed | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus RTU** | RS-232 / RS-485 | Up to 115.2 kbps | Field devices, multi-drop |
| **Modbus ASCII** | RS-232 / RS-485 | Up to 115.2 kbps | Diagnostic, text-friendly |
| **Modbus TCP/IP** | Ethernet (TCP) | 10/100 Mbps+ | Modern SCADA, plant network |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Modbus RTU**, data is encoded in **binary** with **CRC-16** error checking, while in **Modbus ASCII**, data is encoded in **hexadecimal ASCII** with **LRC (Longitudinal Redundancy Check)**. Modbus TCP uses the same PDU over **TCP port 502**, with CRC/LRC replaced by TCP's own checksum.

### 1.3.2 Modbus Data Model — Four Register Types
| Register Type | Reference Prefix | Size | Access | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coil** | 0xxxxx | 1 bit | R/W | Digital Output |
| **Discrete Input** | 1xxxxx | 1 bit | Read-Only | Digital Input |
| **Holding Register** | 4xxxxx | 16 bit | R/W | Analog Output |
| **Input Register** | 3xxxxx | 16 bit | Read-Only | Analog Input |

### Conceptual Analogy for Modbus
Imagine a **post-office box system** in a small town. Every house (slave) has a unique PO Box number (slave ID 1–247). The postmaster (master) writes a small standardized form (Modbus frame) and asks for a specific box (register address) to be read or filled. There is only one postmaster, so there is no "argument" between senders.

---

## 1.4 CANOpen Protocol

**CAN (Controller Area Network)** is a robust, multi-master, message-based **serial bus** originally developed by **Bosch GmbH in 1986** for in-vehicle networking. It is standardized as **ISO 11898**. **CANOpen** is a higher-layer protocol built on top of CAN, defined by **CiA (CAN in Automation)** as **EN 50325-4**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — CANOpen:**
> CANOpen is a **CAL (CAN Application Layer) based, multi-master, real-time protocol** providing a standardized communication profile (CiA DS 301), device profiles (e.g., CiA 401 for I/O modules, CiA 402 for drives), and a centralized object dictionary for parameter access.

### 1.4.1 Key Features of CANOpen
- **Multi-master** bus — any node can initiate communication.
- **Message-based** communication (not register-based like Modbus).
- **Object Dictionary (OD)** — every node has a 16-bit indexed, 8-bit sub-indexed parameter table.
- **PDO (Process Data Object)** — high-priority, real-time, 1-to-many broadcast (no protocol overhead).
- **SDO (Service Data Object)** — low-priority, client/server, used for configuration.
- **NMT (Network Management)** — boot-up, heartbeat, node guarding for fault detection.
- **Synchronization (SYNC)** — producer/consumer synchronization for deterministic sampling.

### Conceptual Analogy for CANOpen
Think of **CANOpen as a conference room** where every participant (node) has a **badge with a unique ID (Node ID, 1–127)**. There is no single "boss" — anyone can speak (multi-master). However, all speeches are short (max **8 bytes per CAN frame**). For urgent matters (PDO), speakers just shout the message aloud; for detailed configuration (SDO), they exchange a private letter through a designated mailbox (SDO server).

---

## 1.5 Summary Comparison Table (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Feature | Ethernet (IE) | Modbus (RTU/TCP) | CANOpen |
|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer Focus** | 1, 2, 3, 4, 7 | 7 (Application) | 1, 2, 7 |
| **Topology** | Star, Tree, Ring (Daisy-chain via switches) | RS-485: Daisy-chain | Bus (linear) |
| **Speed** | 10 Mbps – 10 Gbps | RTU: ≤115.2 kbps; TCP: 100 Mbps+ | 10 kbps – 1 Mbps |
| **Max Nodes** | Thousands (logically) | **247** slaves | **127** nodes |
| **Architecture** | Client/Server & Producer/Consumer | Master/Slave | Multi-Master |
| **Frame Size** | 64 – 1518 bytes | RTU: ≤256 bytes; TCP: variable | Max **8 bytes** payload |
| **Error Check** | CRC-32 (Ethernet) | CRC-16 (RTU), TCP checksum | CRC-15 (CAN hardware) |
| **Determinism** | IE variants (RT/IRT) | Polling-based | **High** (priority-based arbitration) |
| **Typical Use** | Plant backbone, SCADA | Field devices, legacy PLCs | Motion control, sub-assemblies |
| **Standard Body** | IEEE 802.3 | Modicon / Modbus-IDA | CiA / EN 50325-4 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ethernet — Layered Operation

### 2.1.1 IEEE 802.3 Frame Structure (Mandatory Diagram for KTU)

| Preamble (7B) | SFD (1B) | Dest MAC (6B) | Src MAC (6B) | Length/Type (2B) | Payload (46–1500B) | FCS (4B) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

- **Preamble (7 bytes)**: `10101010...` pattern for clock synchronization.
- **SFD (Start Frame Delimiter, 1 byte)**: `10101011` — marks start of frame.
- **MAC addresses (6 bytes each)**: Globally unique, 48-bit hardware address.
- **Length/Type (2 bytes)**: If ≤1500 → length; if ≥1536 → EtherType (e.g., 0x0800 = IPv4).
- **FCS (Frame Check Sequence, 4 bytes)**: **CRC-32** polynomial.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** "Explain the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format with the function of each field."

### 2.1.2 CSMA/CD Algorithm (Half-Duplex Classical Ethernet)
1. **Carrier Sense** — Listen to the medium.
2. **If idle** → start transmitting.
3. **If busy** → wait until idle + **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)** of **96 bit-times**.
4. **If collision detected** (voltage > threshold) → transmit **Jam signal (32 bits)**.
5. **Backoff** — wait a random time: $T_{backoff} = r \times 51.2\,\mu s$, where $r \in [0, 2^k - 1]$, $k = \min(n, 10)$, $n$ = number of collisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern full-duplex switched Ethernet **does not use CSMA/CD** because there are no collisions on dedicated point-to-point links.

### 2.1.3 Real-Time Extensions (Industrial Ethernet)
| Class | Cycle Time | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Standard TCP/IP | 100 ms+ | Diagnostics, HMI |
| RT (Real-Time) | 1–10 ms | I/O update |
| IRT (Isochronous RT) | **< 1 ms (down to 31.25 µs)** | Motion control |

Profinet IRT uses **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)** scheduled by the IO Controller — every cycle is divided into a **real-time phase** (deterministic) and an **open phase** (best-effort).

---

## 2.2 Modbus — Protocol Deep-Dive

### 2.2.1 Modbus RTU Frame Structure

| Slave Address (1B) | Function Code (1B) | Data (N bytes) | CRC-16 (2B) |
|---|---|---|---|

- **Slave Address**: 1–247 (0 = broadcast).
- **Function Code (FC)**: 1 byte indicating operation.
- **Data**: N bytes containing addresses, quantities, or values.
- **CRC-16**: 2-byte cyclic redundancy (polynomial `0xA001`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Modbus RTU**, the silent interval between frames must be **≥ 3.5 character times (T₁.₅ = 1.5 char times between bytes)** — this is the "framing" rule that distinguishes RTU from ASCII.

### 2.2.2 Modbus Function Codes (High-Yield for KTU)

| FC | Name | Type | Operates On |
|---|---|---|---|
| 01 | Read Coils | Bit R | 0xxxxx |
| 02 | Read Discrete Inputs | Bit R | 1xxxxx |
| 03 | Read Holding Registers | Word R | 4xxxxx |
| 04 | Read Input Registers | Word R | 3xxxxx |
| 05 | Write Single Coil | Bit W | 0xxxxx |
| 06 | Write Single Register | Word W | 4xxxxx |
| 15 (0x0F) | Write Multiple Coils | Bit W | 0xxxxx |
| 16 (0x10) | Write Multiple Registers | Word W | 4xxxxx |
| 23 (0x17) | Read/Write Multiple Registers | Word R/W | 4xxxxx |

### 2.2.3 Modbus PDU vs ADU
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** = Function Code + Data (max **253 bytes**).
- **ADU (Application Data Unit)** = Address + PDU + Error Check.
- For **Modbus TCP**, the ADU = MBAP Header (7 bytes) + PDU. The MBAP contains **Transaction ID, Protocol ID (0x0000), Length, Unit ID**.

### 2.2.4 Modbus Exception Responses
When a slave cannot process a request, it returns the **FC + 0x80** with an exception code:
- 01 — Illegal Function
- 02 — Illegal Data Address
- 03 — Illegal Data Value
- 04 — Slave Device Failure
- 06 — Slave Device Busy

---

## 2.3 CANOpen — Protocol Deep-Dive

### 2.3.1 CAN 2.0A/2.0B Frame Format

| SOF (1b) | Arbitration (11b or 29b) | Control (6b) | Data (0–8B) | CRC (15b) | ACK (2b) | EOF (7b) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

- **CAN 2.0A**: 11-bit identifier (Standard).
- **CAN 2.0B**: 29-bit identifier (Extended).
- **CANOpen uses 11-bit ID with a fixed COB-ID scheme.**

### 2.3.2 CANOpen COB-ID Allocation (CiA DS 301)
| Object | COB-ID (hex) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| NMT | 0x000 | Network Management |
| SYNC | 0x080 | Synchronization |
| EMCY | 0x080 + NodeID | Emergency |
| TPDO1 | 0x180 + NodeID | Transmit PDO 1 |
| RPDO1 | 0x200 + NodeID | Receive PDO 1 |
| TPDO2 | 0x280 + NodeID | Transmit PDO 2 |
| RPDO2 | 0x300 + NodeID | Receive PDO 2 |
| TPDO3 | 0x380 + NodeID | Transmit PDO 3 |
| RPDO3 | 0x400 + NodeID | Receive PDO 3 |
| TPDO4 | 0x480 + NodeID | Transmit PDO 4 |
| RPDO4 | 0x500 + NodeID | Receive PDO 4 |
| SDO (TX) | 0x580 + NodeID | Server → Client SDO |
| SDO (RX) | 0x600 + NodeID | Client → Server SDO |
| NMT Error Control | 0x700 + NodeID | Heartbeat / Node Guard |

### 2.3.3 Object Dictionary (OD) — The Heart of CANOpen
The OD is a structured table inside every CANOpen device mapping all accessible parameters. Each entry has:
- **Index (16 bits)**: e.g., `0x1000` — Device Type.
- **Sub-Index (8 bits)**: e.g., `0x01` — first sub-parameter.
- **Data type, access type (RO/RW/WO), default value**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory OD entries (CiA DS 301):**
> `0x1000` Device Type, `0x1001` Error Register, `0x1010` Store Parameters, `0x1011` Restore Default, `0x1018` Identity Object, `0x1A00` TPDO mapping, `0x1400` RPDO communication parameters.

### 2.3.4 PDO Communication Modes
| Mode | Trigger | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous (acyclic)** | On SYNC, but only if value changed | Reduces bus load |
| **Synchronous (cyclic)** | Every Nth SYNC | Regular sampling |
| **Asynchronous (RTR)** | Remote Transmission Request | Legacy |
| **Asynchronous (event)** | On internal event (e.g., threshold) | Most common |

### 2.3.5 NMT States
```
Initializing → Pre-operational → Operational
                    ↓                ↓
                Stopped ←——————— (Reset Communication / Reset Node)
```
- **Pre-operational**: SDO and NMT allowed; PDO disabled.
- **Operational**: All communications (SDO + PDO) allowed.
- **Stopped**: Only NMT allowed; node is silent.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modbus RTU frame efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{data}}{N_{data}+5}$ | ratio | Efficiency vs payload |
| 2 | Modbus T₁.₅ inter-byte | $T_{1.5} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 11}{baud}$ | seconds | RTU framing rule |
| 3 | Modbus T₃.₅ silent interval | $T_{3.5} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 11}{baud}$ | seconds | RTU frame separator |
| 4 | Ethernet IFG | $T_{IFG} = \dfrac{96}{bit\_rate}$ | seconds | Inter-frame gap |
| 5 | Slot time (10 Mbps) | $T_{slot} = 51.2\,\mu s$ | seconds | CSMA/CD timing |
| 6 | Binary exponential backoff | $T_{bo} = r \cdot T_{slot}$, $r \in [0, 2^k-1]$ | seconds | Collision recovery |
| 7 | CAN bus bit time | $t_{bit} = t_{SYNC} + t_{TSEG1} + t_{TSEG2}$ | seconds | Bus timing |
| 8 | CAN max throughput (8B payload, 1 Mbps) | $\rho_{max} = \dfrac{8 \times 8}{130\,bits} \cdot 10^6 \approx 492$ kbps | bps | Bus load |
| 9 | CANOpen cycle time | $T_{cyc} = N \cdot t_{bit}$ | seconds | Worst-case bus scan |
| 10 | CRC-16 Modbus polynomial | $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$ | — | RTU error check |
| 11 | CAN CRC polynomial | $G(x) = x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^8 + x^7 + x^4 + x^3 + 1$ | — | CAN frame check |
| 12 | Network delay one-way | $t_{d} = \dfrac{L_{frame}}{R_{bit}}$ | seconds | Single hop latency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where pipe `|` is used in math (e.g., CRC polynomial indices), LaTeX uses `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown tables.**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Industrial Ethernet (EtherCAT, Profinet)**: Used in automotive assembly lines (BMW, Mercedes), semiconductor fabs, and high-speed packaging machines where cycle times < 1 ms are mandatory.
- **Modbus RTU/TCP**: Dominant in water treatment SCADA, HVAC, energy meters, and legacy PLC retrofits.
- **CANOpen**: Used in **motion control** (servo drives CiA 402), medical equipment, mobile machinery, and robotic subsystems.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations and Worked Numerical Problems

### Problem 1 — Modbus RTU Frame Silencing Time (High-Frequency KTU Question)

> **Q [KTU 2022 Model]:** A Modbus RTU network runs at **19,200 baud** with **11 bits per character** (1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop). Calculate the inter-character time $T_{1.5}$ and the inter-frame silent interval $T_{3.5}$.

**Step 1 — Character time**
$$t_{char} = \frac{11 \text{ bits/char}}{19{,}200 \text{ bits/s}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate**
$$t_{char} = \frac{11}{19{,}200} = 5.729 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 572.92\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Inter-character time $T_{1.5}$ (between two bytes of the same frame)**
$$T_{1.5} = 1.5 \times t_{char} = 1.5 \times 572.92\,\mu s = 859.38\,\mu s \approx 0.859\,ms$$

**Step 4 — Inter-frame silent interval $T_{3.5}$ (between two separate frames)**
$$T_{3.5} = 3.5 \times t_{char} = 3.5 \times 572.92\,\mu s = 2005.21\,\mu s \approx 2.005\,ms$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a gap longer than $T_{1.5}$ but shorter than $T_{3.5}$ is detected inside a frame, the receiver discards the message and assumes a new frame start.

---

### Problem 2 — Modbus RTU Frame Efficiency

> **Q:** A master sends a Modbus RTU command to read **10 holding registers** starting at address `0x0010`. Calculate frame efficiency at 19,200 baud. Slave ID = `0x0A`, FC = `0x03`.

**Step 1 — Build the request frame**
| Field | Bytes | Content |
|---|---|---|
| Slave ID | 1 | 0x0A |
| Function Code | 1 | 0x03 |
| Start Address Hi/Lo | 2 | 0x00 0x10 |
| Quantity Hi/Lo | 2 | 0x00 0x0A |
| CRC-16 Lo/Hi | 2 | — |

Total request = **8 bytes**.

**Step 2 — Build the response frame (slave reply)**
| Field | Bytes |
|---|---|
| Slave ID | 1 |
| Function Code | 1 |
| Byte Count | 1 (= 20) |
| Register Data (10 × 2) | 20 |
| CRC-16 | 2 |

Total response = **25 bytes**.

**Step 3 — Calculate useful payload**
Useful payload = 20 bytes (10 register values).

**Step 4 — Frame efficiency**
$$\eta = \frac{N_{data}}{N_{total}} = \frac{20}{25} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

**Step 5 — Total transaction time**
$$N_{total} = 8 + 25 = 33 \text{ bytes} = 33 \times 11 = 363 \text{ bits}$$
$$t_{trans} = \frac{363}{19{,}200} = 18.91\,ms$$

---

### Problem 3 — Ethernet Throughput & Backoff (Worst Case)

> **Q:** On a 10 Mbps Ethernet segment, after 4 successive collisions, what is the maximum backoff time?

**Step 1 — Choose $k$**
$$k = \min(n, 10) = \min(4, 10) = 4$$

**Step 2 — Maximum $r$**
$$r_{max} = 2^k - 1 = 2^4 - 1 = 15$$

**Step 3 — Slot time at 10 Mbps**
$$T_{slot} = 51.2\,\mu s$$

**Step 4 — Maximum backoff time**
$$T_{bo, max} = r_{max} \cdot T_{slot} = 15 \times 51.2\,\mu s = 768\,\mu s$$

---

### Problem 4 — CAN Bus Throughput with 8-Byte Payloads

> **Q:** A CAN bus at 1 Mbps carries 8-byte data frames. Each frame has 47 bits of overhead (SOF, Arbitration, Control, CRC, ACK, EOF, plus 3 IFS bits). What is the maximum data throughput in kbps?

**Step 1 — Total bits per frame**
$$N_{frame} = 47 \text{ (overhead)} + 8 \times 8 \text{ (data)} = 47 + 64 = 111 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Data throughput**
$$\rho = \frac{64 \text{ data bits}}{111 \text{ total bits}} \times 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 576{,}576 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 3 — Express in kbps**
$$\rho \approx 576.6 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the stuffing bits (1 every 5 identical bits) add ~10% overhead, so realistic throughput is **~ 500 kbps** for 1 Mbps CAN. CANOpen further reduces this with PDO bit-packing.

---

### Problem 5 — CANOpen PDO Mapping: Real-Time Update Time

> **Q:** A CANOpen servo drive transmits TPDO1 every 2 ms containing 4 process variables: actual position (32 bits), actual velocity (16 bits), status word (16 bits), and torque (16 bits). Verify that it fits in a single CAN frame (≤ 8 bytes).

**Step 1 — Sum of mapped data**
$$32 + 16 + 16 + 16 = 80 \text{ bits} = 10 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with CAN payload limit**
$$10 \text{ bytes} > 8 \text{ bytes} \Rightarrow \text{Does NOT fit in single CAN frame}$$

**Step 3 — Solution: Split into TPDO1 + TPDO2**
- **TPDO1**: Position (32 bits) + Status (16 bits) = **6 bytes** ✓
- **TPDO2**: Velocity (16 bits) + Torque (16 bits) = **4 bytes** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **classic KTU pitfall** — students often map more than 8 bytes into one PDO. Always verify the sum ≤ **64 bits**.

---

### Problem 6 — Modbus TCP Frame vs. Modbus RTU Frame

> **Q:** Compare the bytes required for an equivalent "Read 1 Holding Register" request in Modbus RTU vs. Modbus TCP.

**Modbus RTU request:**
| Slave ID | FC | Start Hi | Start Lo | CRC Lo | CRC Hi |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 = **6 bytes** |

**Modbus TCP request:**
| Tx ID (2) | Proto ID (2) | Length (2) | Unit ID (1) | FC (1) | Start Hi (1) | Start Lo (1) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MBAP = 7 bytes | | | | PDU = 3 bytes | | |

Total = **10 bytes** (no CRC because TCP has its own checksum).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Choosing a Protocol

```
Start
  │
  ├─ Need > 100 Mbps? ──Yes──> Industrial Ethernet (Profinet / EtherNet/IP)
  │         │
  │         No
  │         │
  ├─ Need multi-master real-time (motion control)? ──Yes──> CANOpen
  │         │
  │         No
  │         │
  └─ Need simple, cheap, register-based polling? ──Yes──> Modbus RTU / TCP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** "Compare and contrast Modbus, CANOpen and Industrial Ethernet for a packaging machine" is a frequently asked **14-mark question**.

## 4.2 Modbus Master-Slave Transaction Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant S1 as Slave ID 1
    participant S2 as Slave ID 2
    participant S5 as Slave ID 5

    M->>S1: Request [Addr=0x01, FC=0x03, Start=0x0000, Qty=0x000A]
    S1-->>M: Response [Addr=0x01, FC=0x03, Data 20B, CRC]
    M->>S2: Request [Addr=0x02, FC=0x06, Reg=0x0001, Val=0x00FF]
    S2-->>M: Echo Response [Addr=0x02, FC=0x06, Reg=0x0001, Val=0x00FF]
    M->>S1: Request [Addr=0x01, FC=0x03, Start=0x0200, Qty=0x0002]
    S1-->>M: Exception [Addr=0x01, FC=0x83, Code=0x02 Illegal Address]
    M->>S5: Broadcast Write [Addr=0x00, FC=0x10, Data, CRC]
    Note over S1,S5: All slaves receive, only address 5 acts<br/>(no response to broadcast)
