# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer, 60–80 words)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between Modbus RTU and Modbus ASCII modes of transmission.**

**Model Answer:**
Modbus RTU encodes each byte as **8-bit binary** with a start bit, optional parity bit, and one/two stop bits (typically 11 bits/char). It uses **CRC-16** for error detection. Modbus ASCII encodes each byte as **two hex characters** separated by `:` and `CR/LF`, with **LRC (Longitudinal Redundancy Check)** for error detection. RTU offers higher throughput (~ 2x) and is more common industrially, while ASCII is preferred for human-readable diagnostics over slow links.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`
**List the four data categories of Modbus and their reference prefixes.**

**Model Answer:**
The four Modbus data tables are: **Coils (0xxxxx)** — 1-bit read/write digital outputs; **Discrete Inputs (1xxxxx)** — 1-bit read-only digital inputs; **Input Registers (3xxxxx)** — 16-bit read-only analog inputs; and **Holding Registers (4xxxxx)** — 16-bit read/write analog outputs or configuration parameters. Each table is addressable from `0` to `65535` (the "x" digits) and supports a defined set of function codes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (With Sub-Parts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **ONE question** out of a choice of two from each module. Both questions below are framed as **complete independent alternatives**.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]`

**Q.A (a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the architecture of the Industrial Ethernet protocol stack. Compare standard Ethernet with Profinet RT and Profinet IRT in terms of cycle time and determinism.**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture:** Industrial Ethernet reuses the IEEE 802.3 physical and data-link layers of standard Ethernet, adds the standard TCP/IP/UDP network and transport layers, and replaces the upper application layer with a real-time-capable protocol such as **Profinet IO**, **EtherNet/IP (CIP)**, or **EtherCAT**. Profinet (defined by PROFIBUS Nutzerorganisation, PNO) defines three classes:

- **NRT (Non-Real-Time)** – used for diagnostics and parameterization; uses standard TCP/IP and UDP/IP; cycle time ~ 100 ms.
- **RT (Real-Time)** – bypasses TCP/IP layers; uses a dedicated Ethernet frame Ethertype `0x8892`; cycle time 1–10 ms; sufficient for I/O updates and standard motion.
- **IRT (Isochronous Real-Time)** – uses hardware-based scheduling (TDMA, time slices) with cut-through switching; cycle time **< 1 ms, down to 31.25 µs** with jitter < 1 µs; mandatory for high-speed motion control such as printing, packaging, and robotics.

**Comparison Table:**
| Property | Standard TCP/IP | Profinet RT | Profinet IRT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle Time | 100 ms+ | 1–10 ms | 0.25–1 ms (down to 31.25 µs) |
| Stack Used | Full TCP/IP | Bypassed, Ethertype 0x8892 | Hardware TDMA, cut-through |
| Jitter | High (ms) | Moderate (~ 1 ms) | < 1 µs |
| Use | Diagnostics, HMI | Distributed I/O | Motion control |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse RT with IRT. RT is **software-based** and uses standard switches; IRT requires **IRT-capable switches** and a sync domain for sub-millisecond determinism.

**Q.A (b)** [7 Marks] **Draw the IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame format and explain the function of each field. Compute the maximum throughput efficiency of a 64-byte frame on a 100 Mbps link.**

**Model Answer:**

**Frame Diagram (each block = 1 byte unless specified):**

| Preamble (7B) | SFD (1B) | Dest MAC (6B) | Src MAC (6B) | Length/Type (2B) | Payload (46–1500B) | FCS (4B) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

**Field Functions:**
- **Preamble (7 bytes):** Pattern `10101010...` — bit-clock synchronization between sender and receiver.
- **SFD (1 byte):** `10101011` — marks the actual start of the frame.
- **Destination MAC (6 bytes):** Hardware address of the intended receiver.
- **Source MAC (6 bytes):** Hardware address of the sender.
- **Length/Type (2 bytes):** If value ≤ 1500 → length of payload; if ≥ 1536 → EtherType protocol identifier (e.g., 0x0800 = IPv4, 0x0806 = ARP).
- **Payload (46–1500 bytes):** Encapsulated data; minimum 46 bytes to ensure the frame is at least 64 bytes from DA to FCS for collision detection.
- **FCS (4 bytes):** CRC-32 polynomial `0x04C11DB7` for error detection.

**Frame size from DA to FCS (minimum) = 64 bytes → 512 bits.**

**Step 1 — Total transmitted bits including preamble/SFD/IFG (8 + 8 + 96 = 112 bits of overhead)**
$$N_{total} = 512 \text{ (frame)} + 64 \text{ (preamble + SFD)} = 576 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — But for efficiency, only count the MAC frame (DA → FCS):**
$$\eta = \frac{\text{payload bits}}{\text{total MAC frame bits}} = \frac{46 \times 8}{64 \times 8} = \frac{368}{512} = 71.875\%$$

**Step 3 — At 100 Mbps with 96-bit IFG:**
$$\eta_{line} = \frac{512}{512 + 96 + 64} = \frac{512}{672} = 76.19\%$$

**Step 4 — Useful throughput:**
$$\rho = 76.19\% \times 100\,\text{Mbps} \approx 76.19\,\text{Mbps}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Style]`

**Q.B (a)** [7 Marks] **Explain the CANOpen protocol stack with reference to PDO, SDO, NMT, and the Object Dictionary. Differentiate between TPDO and RPDO.**

**Model Answer:**

**CANOpen Stack (CiA DS 301):**
CANOpen is built on top of the **CAN 2.0A** (11-bit identifier) data link layer. Above it, CiA DS 301 defines:
- **NMT (Network Management)** — controls node states (Initializing → Pre-operational → Operational → Stopped) and handles boot-up, heartbeat, and node-guarding.
- **SDO (Service Data Object)** — point-to-point, client/server, low-priority (COB-IDs `0x600 + NodeID` for client→server and `0x580 + NodeID` for server→client). Used for **configuration, parameter upload/download** through the Object Dictionary.
- **PDO (Process Data Object)** — broadcast, producer/consumer, high-priority (COB-IDs `0x180 + NodeID` to `0x500 + NodeID`). Used for **real-time cyclic or event-driven process data exchange** (e.g., actual position/velocity of a drive).
- **SYNC** — synchronization object (COB-ID `0x080`) for synchronous PDO triggering.
- **EMCY** — emergency object (COB-ID `0x080 + NodeID`) for error reporting.

**Object Dictionary (OD):** The OD is a 16-bit-indexed, 8-bit-sub-indexed table inside every node containing all accessible parameters. Mandatory entries (e.g., `0x1000` Device Type, `0x1018` Identity, `0x1A00` TPDO mapping, `0x1400` RPDO comm params) ensure interoperability.

**TPDO vs RPDO:**
| Property | TPDO (Transmit PDO) | RPDO (Receive PDO) |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Producer → Consumer | Consumer receives from Producer |
| COB-ID | `0x180 + NodeID` (TPDO1), ..., `0x480 + NodeID` (TPDO4) | `0x200 + NodeID` (RPDO1), ..., `0x500 + NodeID` (RPDO4) |
| Purpose | Broadcasts the node's process data (e.g., actual position) | Receives setpoints from the controller (e.g., target position) |
| Example | Drive sends actual velocity | Drive receives target velocity from PLC |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **T**PDO with **T**ransmit meaning "the node receives TPDO". Correct: **TPDO = the node is the producer and transmits; RPDO = the node is the consumer and receives.**

**Q.B (b)** [7 Marks] **Describe the Modbus RTU frame structure and verify whether the following frame is valid:**
`[0A 03 00 10 00 0A C5 CD]`
Also compute the time to transmit this frame at 19,200 baud, 11 bits/char.

**Model Answer:**

**Modbus RTU Frame Structure:**

| Slave ID (1B) | Function Code (1B) | Data (N bytes) | CRC-16 (2B Lo+Hi) |
|---|---|---|---|

**Frame Analysis:**

| Field | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Slave ID | `0x0A` = 10 | Slave 10 addressed |
| Function Code | `0x03` | Read Holding Registers |
| Start Address Hi | `0x00` | MSB of register = 0 |
| Start Address Lo | `0x10` | LSB = 16 → Register 16 |
| Quantity Hi | `0x00` | MSB of count = 0 |
| Quantity Lo | `0x0A` | LSB = 10 → 10 registers |
| CRC Lo | `0xC5` | CRC low byte |
| CRC Hi | `0xCD` | CRC high byte |

**Step 1 — Verify CRC manually (standard polynomial `0xA001`):**
The polynomial calculation for the first 6 bytes (`0A 03 00 10 00 0A`) should yield CRC = `0xCDC5` (Lo byte first = `0xC5 0xCD`). The provided CRC matches — **the frame is VALID**.

**Step 2 — Compute transmission time:**
$$N_{bytes} = 8$$
$$N_{bits} = 8 \times 11 = 88 \text{ bits}$$
$$t_{trans} = \frac{88}{19{,}200} = 4.583 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.583\,ms$$

**Step 3 — Add T₃.₅ silent intervals (one before + one after) for a complete transaction:**
$$T_{silent} = 2 \times 2.005\,ms = 4.010\,ms$$
$$T_{total} \approx 4.583 + 4.010 = 8.593\,ms$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students forget to include the **T₃.₅ silent interval** at frame boundaries when computing "round-trip" or "transaction" time. Also, in Modbus RTU, **CRC is sent LOW byte FIRST**, not the other way around.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Protocols**

### Ethernet / Industrial Ethernet
- IEEE 802.3 standard; classical data rate 10/100 Mbps, modern 1/10 Gbps.
- Frame: Preamble (7) + SFD (1) + DA (6) + SA (6) + L/T (2) + Data (46–1500) + FCS (4).
- **CSMA/CD** + binary exponential backoff: $T_{bo} = r \times 51.2\,\mu s$, $r \in [0, 2^k - 1]$.
- **Profinet RT** = 1–10 ms cycle; **Profinet IRT** = 31.25 µs – 1 ms cycle (TDMA, hardware-scheduled).
- **EtherCAT** = processing-on-the-fly, sub-100 µs.
- Full-duplex switched Ethernet does **not** use CSMA/CD.

### Modbus
- **Master/slave**, **1 master, up to 247 slaves**, broadcast address = 0.
- **Modbus RTU** = binary + CRC-16, $\geq 3.5$ char silent at frame boundaries.
- **Modbus ASCII** = hex ASCII + LRC, less efficient.
- **Modbus TCP** = port 502, MBAP header (7 bytes) replaces CRC.
- **Data types**: Coils (0x), Discrete Inputs (1x), Input Registers (3x), Holding Registers (4x).
- **Function codes 01–06, 15, 16, 23** are most common.
- Exception responses: FC + 0x80 + exception code (01, 02, 03, 04, 06).
- **PDU max 253 bytes**; ADU varies by variant.

### CANOpen
- Built on **CAN 2.0A (11-bit ID)**; ISO 11898; bit rates 10 kbps – 1 Mbps.
- **Node ID = 1–127**; COB-ID = Function Code + Node ID.
- **NMT** = state machine (Initializing → Pre-Op → Operational → Stopped).
- **PDO** = real-time broadcast (≤ 8 bytes per frame); TPDO `0x18x + NodeID`, RPDO `0x20x + NodeID`.
- **SDO** = client/server configuration; RX `0x600 + NodeID`, TX `0x580 + NodeID`.
- **SYNC** = `0x080`; **EMCY** = `0x080 + NodeID`.
- **Object Dictionary** = 16-bit index, 8-bit sub-index; mandatory entries at `0x1000`, `0x1001`, `0x1010`, `0x1011`, `0x1018`, `0x1A00`, `0x1400`.
- Total mapped PDO bits **MUST ≤ 64 bits (8 bytes)**.
- 5 mandatory PDO communication parameters at `0x1400–0x1404` (RPDO) and `0x1800–0x1804` (TPDO).

### High-Yield Mnemonics for KTU
- **"PRS NE-OPS"** — NMT States: **P**re-Op → **R**unning = **Op**erational → **S**topped.
- **"DTIM"** for Modbus data: **D**iscrete In, **T**ype In, **I**nput Reg, **M**ultiple — or the more memorable **"0-1-3-4"** for coil, DI, IR, HR.
- **CANOpen COB-ID formula:** Function Group (4 bits) + Node ID (7 bits) = 11-bit ID.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OPC - Open Platform Communication, OPC UA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** OPC - Open Platform Communication, OPC UA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is OPC? (Open Platform Communications)

**OPC (Open Platform Communications)** is an interoperability standard for the secure and reliable exchange of data in the industrial automation space. It is developed and maintained by the **OPC Foundation**, a non-profit organization founded in 1994 by a consortium of leading automation hardware and software suppliers (including Fisher-Rosemount, Rockwell Software, Siemens, and Intellution).

Originally, OPC stood for **OLE for Process Control** (Object Linking and Embedded for Process Control), based on Microsoft's OLE/COM/DCOM technology. The acronym was later generalized to "Open Platform Communications" to reflect the technology's expansion beyond its Windows-only origins — particularly with the introduction of **OPC UA (Unified Architecture)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** OPC is an open, vendor-independent standard that enables seamless data exchange between disparate automation devices (PLCs, DCS, SCADA, HMI) and enterprise applications (MES, ERP) by providing a common interface that abstracts device-specific protocols.

### 1.2 Evolution of OPC Standards

The OPC family has evolved through two major generations:

| Generation | Year | Variants | Technology Base |
|------------|------|----------|-----------------|
| **OPC Classic** | 1996–2010 | OPC DA, OPC HDA, OPC AE, OPC DX, OPC Security | COM/DCOM (Windows only) |
| **OPC UA** | 2008–Present | Unified Architecture (single spec) | Cross-platform, service-oriented, TCP/HTTP |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, focus on **OPC DA (Data Access)** for Classic and the full **OPC UA** stack.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of OPC Classic

**(a) OPC Data Access (OPC DA)** — Provides real-time, read/write access to current process data (tags, I/O values, setpoints). This is the most widely deployed OPC specification in the industry.

**(b) OPC Historical Data Access (OPC HDA)** — Provides read access to time-series historical data stored in archives, databases, or historians. Supports aggregation functions (average, min, max, integral).

**(c) OPC Alarms & Events (OPC AE)** — Provides notification of process alarms, operator actions, and system events. Supports acknowledgement, severity filtering, and event subscription.

## 1.4 What is OPC UA? (Unified Architecture)

**OPC UA (OPC Unified Architecture)** is the next-generation, platform-independent successor to OPC Classic. Released in 2008, it is defined by a **single unified specification** that integrates DA, HDA, AE, and DX functionality into one coherent framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Unified Architecture):** OPC UA is a service-oriented, platform-independent industrial communication framework that provides a secure, reliable, and scalable mechanism for transferring data from sensors and machines to enterprise systems through a standardized information model.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Translator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The Universal Translator Analogy**
> 
> Imagine a factory floor where **German robots (Siemens PLCs)** speak only German, **Japanese robots (Mitsubishi PLCs)** speak only Japanese, and the **American SCADA system** speaks only English. Without a translator, they cannot communicate.
> 
> **OPC acts as a universal translator**: Every vendor device "speaks OPC" to a single SCADA/ERP system. The SCADA system doesn't need to know Siemens' S7 protocol, Modbus, EtherNet/IP, or Profinet — it only needs to know OPC.
> 
> **OPC UA** is the upgraded translator that no longer needs a Windows-only phone booth (COM/DCOM) — it works on any device, any operating system, and even adds military-grade encryption (security) and structured semantics (information modeling).

## 1.6 Why OPC / OPC UA? The Interoperability Problem

Before OPC, integrating a PLC with a SCADA system required **custom drivers** for every device-vendor-software combination. If a factory had *n* controllers and *m* applications, **n × m** custom drivers were required (the "Driver Matrix Problem").

OPC reduced this to **n + m** interfaces — one OPC server per device, one OPC client per application.

$$ \text{Without OPC: } n \times m \text{ drivers} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{With OPC: } n + m \text{ interfaces} $$

## 1.7 Core Capabilities of OPC UA

| Capability | Description |
|------------|-------------|
| **Platform Independence** | Runs on Windows, Linux, VxWorks, RTOS, embedded ARM, cloud servers |
| **Information Modeling** | Exposes data with semantic meaning (not just raw numbers) |
| **Security** | Authentication, authorization, encryption, signing, audit logging |
| **Scalability** | From tiny sensors to enterprise-scale MES/ERP systems |
| **Multi-Protocol Transport** | TCP, HTTPS, SOAP/XML, WebSockets, MQTT (via pub/sub) |
| **Service Orientation** | Stateless request/response + publish/subscribe model |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** OPC UA = **One specification + Platform-independent + Secure + Information model + Multi-transport**. Memorize these five keywords for Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 OPC Classic Architecture (Detailed)

OPC Classic follows a strict **Client–Server model** based on **Microsoft COM/DCOM**.

### 2.1.1 Logical Components

1. **OPC Server** — A process (typically a Windows service) that physically interfaces with the field device (PLC, RTU, sensor bus). It exposes a standardized set of COM interfaces to clients.
2. **OPC Client** — A SCADA, HMI, or historian application that consumes data from one or more OPC servers.
3. **COM/DCOM** — The transport mechanism. COM is in-process; DCOM is inter-process across machines using RPC over TCP port 135.
4. **OPC Interfaces** — Standardized COM interfaces (e.g., `IOPCServer`, `IOPCBrowse`, `IOPCSyncIO`, `IOPCAsyncIO`) define the contract.

### 2.1.2 OPC DA — Data Access Working Principle

OPC DA is built on three core COM objects:

- **Server Object** — Manages groups, returns status.
- **Group Object** — Logical container for items; defines update rate, deadband, language.
- **Item Object** — Represents a single tag/variable. Items are **not** objects per se but **identifiers** (ItemID strings) — the server maintains the actual values.

**Synchronous vs Asynchronous Read/Write:**

| Mode | Mechanism | Use Case |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Synchronous (IOPCSyncIO)** | Read blocks until result returns | Simple HMI polling |
| **Asynchronous (IOPCAsyncIO)** | Request returns immediately; data arrives via callback | High-performance SCADA with many tags |
| **Subscription (IOPCGroupStateMgt + IDataObject)** | Server pushes updates automatically | Real-time dashboards |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Update Rate** is the maximum rate at which the server will notify subscribed clients. The **Deadband** is the minimum percent change required to trigger an update (reduces network/CPU load on noisy signals).

## 2.2 OPC UA Architecture (Detailed)

OPC UA is defined across **14 logical parts** (called "Parts 1–14" or "Companion Specifications"). The architecture has **three conceptual layers**:

### Layer 1: Information Modeling (Address Space)
OPC UA exposes data as a graph of **Nodes** in a hierarchical **Address Space**. Every entity — variable, method, object type, event — is a Node with:
- A unique `NodeId` (URI-based)
- A `BrowseName`
- An `Attribute` set (Value, DataType, NodeClass, etc.)
- A set of `References` to other nodes

### Layer 2: Services
OPC UA defines **37 services** grouped into:
- **Discovery Service Set** — FindServers, GetEndpoints
- **SecureChannel Service Set** — OpenSecureChannel, CloseSecureChannel
- **Session Service Set** — CreateSession, ActivateSession, CloseSession
- **NodeManagement Service Set** — AddNode, AddReference (for servers)
- **View Service Set** — Browse, RegisterNodes, TranslateBrowsePathsToNodeIds
- **Query Service Set** — Filter complex queries
- **Attribute Service Set** — Read, Write, HistoryRead, HistoryUpdate
- **Method Service Set** — Call
- **MonitoredItem Service Set** — CreateMonitoredItems, ModifyMonitoredItems
- **Subscription Service Set** — CreateSubscription, Publish, Republish
- **PubSub Service Set** (newer) — Publish/Subscribe over UDP/MQTT

### Layer 3: Transport
OPC UA messages are encoded in one of two formats and transported over one of several protocols:

| Encoding | Transport | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------|-------------|
| **Binary** | TCP (opc.tcp://) | Most efficient; intra-factory |
| **XML** | SOAP/HTTPS | Enterprise/cloud integration |
| **JSON** | HTTPS / WebSockets | Web dashboards, mobile apps |

## 2.3 The OPC UA Information Model

The information model is what makes OPC UA "more than a wire protocol." It provides **semantic context**.

### 2.3.1 Base Node Classes

- **Object Node** — Represents a system/entity (e.g., a pump, a motor, a controller)
- **Variable Node** — Represents a data value (e.g., pump speed, temperature)
- **Method Node** — Represents a callable function (e.g., `StartPump`, `ResetAlarm`)
- **ObjectType Node** — Defines a type (e.g., `MotorType`, `PumpType`) — analogous to a class
- **VariableType Node** — Defines a variable structure (e.g., `AnalogItemType`)
- **ReferenceType Node** — Defines a typed relationship (e.g., `HasComponent`, `HasProperty`)
- **DataType Node** — Defines data types (e.g., `Float`, `String`, `LocalizedText`)

### 2.3.2 Standard Address Space & Companion Specs

The OPC Foundation defines:
- **Base** information model (Objects folder, Types folder, Views folder)
- **Companion Specifications** (previously called "Companion Standards") for industry verticals:
  - **OPC UA for Machinery** (VDMA)
  - **OPC UA for Process Automation** (PI) / **OPC UA PA-DIM**
  - **OPC UA for PLCopen** (IEC 61131-3)
  - **OPC UA for Field Level Communication** (FLC, e.g., IO-Link, Profinet)
  - **OPC UA for Automotive**
  - **OPC UA for Robotics**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You may be asked to name 3 companion specifications or to draw the basic address space. Memorize the **Three Root Nodes**: Objects, Types, Views.

## 2.4 OPC UA Security Model

Security is built into OPC UA from the ground up — a major improvement over OPC Classic which relied entirely on Windows DCOM security.

### 2.4.1 Security Triad

1. **Authentication** — ApplicationInstanceCertificate (X.509), username/password, or anonymous.
2. **Authorization** — Per-user/session access control on nodes/methods.
3. **Confidentiality & Integrity** — Encryption (AES-128/256) + signing (HMAC-SHA256).

### 2.4.2 Security Policies

| Policy URI | Encryption | Signing |
|------------|-----------|---------|
| `None` | None | None |
| `Basic128Rsa15` (deprecated) | AES-128 | RSA-15 |
| `Basic256` (deprecated) | AES-256 | SHA-256 |
| `Basic256Sha256` | AES-256 | SHA-256 |
| `Aes128_Sha256_RsaOaep` | AES-128-CBC + RSA-OAEP | SHA-256 |
| `Aes256_Sha256_RsaPss` | AES-256-CBC + RSA-PSS | SHA-256 |

### 2.4.3 Message Security Modes

- **None** — No security (test only)
- **Sign** — Signed only; integrity without confidentiality
- **SignAndEncrypt** — Both; required for production

## 2.5 OPC UA Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub)

Added in OPC UA Part 14 (PubSub), this complements the classical Client-Server model:

- **Broker-based** — Centralized broker (e.g., MQTT broker) handles fan-out
- **Brokerless** — UDP multicast on the LAN (no broker; lower latency)

Useful for:
- Edge-to-cloud streaming
- High-speed sensor networks
- Time-sensitive networking (TSN) integration

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | OPC Classic | OPC UA |
|-----------|-------------|--------|
| Underlying tech | COM/DCOM (Windows) | Cross-platform, TCP/HTTP |
| Architecture | Client-Server | Client-Server + Pub/Sub |
| Security | Windows DCOM ACLs | Built-in: X.509, AES, signing |
| Information Model | Flat tag list | Rich object-oriented graph |
| Data model | Tags + properties | Nodes, attributes, references |
| Transport | RPC/DCOM | Binary/TCP, XML/SOAP, JSON/HTTPS |
| Platforms | Windows only | Windows, Linux, RTOS, embedded |
| Discoverability | None (manual config) | Discovery Service Set (LDS, LDS-ME) |
| Read/Write | Sync/Async/Subscription | Read/Write + HistoryRead |
| Alarms/Events | OPC AE (separate spec) | Unified (AlarmConditionType) |
| Historical Data | OPC HDA (separate spec) | Unified (HistoryRead service) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** OPC UA is the backbone of the **Industry 4.0 Reference Architecture Model (RAMI 4.0)**. It is the only standard that spans the IT/OT boundary — from sensor to cloud — with a single semantic model.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — OPC UA Connection Establishment

## 3.1 Exhaustive Workflow: Establishing an OPC UA Session

This is the most exam-relevant sequence in OPC UA — examiners love it. We trace every step a client takes to establish a secure, authenticated channel to an OPC UA server.

### Step 1: Discovery of Servers (UDP Multicast on Port 4840)

The client sends a **FindServers** request as a UDP datagram to the well-known multicast address `opc.udp://239.255.255.255:4840`. Servers in the subnet respond with their `ApplicationDescription` (URI, product URI, application name, application type, etc.).

$$\text{FindServers} \rightarrow \text{Multicast 239.255.255.255:4840} \rightarrow \text{List of Application URIs}$$

> **Logic:** UDP multicast ensures zero-configuration discovery on a local network without requiring a directory server.

### Step 2: Endpoint Discovery — GetEndpoints

The client queries a discovered server's `/discovery` endpoint using `opc.tcp://server:4840/discovery`. The server returns a list of **Endpoints**, each describing:
- The **endpoint URL** (e.g., `opc.tcp://server:4840`)
- The **security policy URI** (e.g., `Aes256_Sha256_RsaPss`)
- The **security mode** (None / Sign / SignAndEncrypt)
- The **user identity tokens** supported (Anonymous, Username, Certificate)

### Step 3: SecureChannel Establishment — OpenSecureChannel Request

The client selects an endpoint and sends an `OpenSecureChannelRequest` message over TCP. The request is encoded in OPC UA Binary format. Negotiation parameters include:

- **ProtocolVersion**
- **RequestType** (Issue or Renew)
- **SecurityMode**, **SecurityPolicyUri**
- **ClientNonce** (32-byte random number for deriving keys)
- **ClientCertificate** (X.509 ApplicationInstanceCertificate)

The server responds with its certificate, server nonce, and a `ChannelId`. Both sides now derive **session keys** using:

$$
K_{\text{sign}} = \text{PKCS5\_PBKDF2}(\text{HMAC}_{\text{SHA256}}(K_{\text{derived}}, \text{ServerNonce} \oplus \text{ClientNonce}))
$$

$$
K_{\text{encrypt}} = \text{PKCS5\_PBKDF2}(\text{HMAC}_{\text{SHA256}}(K_{\text{sign}}, \text{ServerNonce} \oplus \text{ClientNonce}))
$$

> **Logic:** The XOR of nonces is hashed twice — first to get a signing key, then to get an encryption key — using PBKDF2 for key stretching. This ensures forward secrecy.

### Step 4: Session Creation — CreateSession Request

The client sends a `CreateSessionRequest` containing:
- `ClientDescription` (URI, locale)
- `ServerUri`
- `EndpointUrl`
- `SessionName`
- `ClientNonce`
- `ClientCertificate`
- `RequestedSessionTimeout`

The server allocates resources, returns a unique `AuthenticationToken` (a random GUID-like value), a `SessionId`, and a `ServerNonce` (used for all subsequent requests of the session to prevent replay).

### Step 5: Session Activation — ActivateSession Request

The client sends `ActivateSessionRequest` carrying:
- **ClientSignature** — signed with the client certificate (proves the client owns the private key)
- **UserIdentityToken** — chosen from the endpoint's supported list:
  - **AnonymousIdentityToken** — empty username/password
  - **UserNameIdentityToken** — username + password (hashed, sent over secure channel)
  - **X509IdentityToken** — user certificate
- **LocaleIds** — preferred language(s)

On success, the server returns a revised `SessionId` and `Nonce`.

### Step 6: Browse the Address Space

The client uses the `Browse` service to walk the address space, starting from the `Objects` folder (NodeId `i=85`) and `Types` folder (NodeId `i=86`). For each node, it requests the `BrowseName`, `DisplayName`, and `NodeClass` attributes.

### Step 7: Read/Write or Subscribe to Data

- **Read service** — Client specifies `NodeId`s and requested `AttributeIds` (e.g., `Value=13`).
- **Write service** — Similar structure; server validates access rights.
- **CreateMonitoredItems** + **CreateSubscription** — For real-time updates with **PublishingEnabled = true**. The server pushes a `Publish` request with the changed data; client responds with `PublishRequest` acknowledgements.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Address Space Navigation

Suppose we want to read the value of `Temperature` from a temperature sensor exposed by an OPC UA server.

**Path:** `Objects → Devices → TemperatureSensor → Temperature`

Each step is a `Browse` request. The following derivation shows the node relationships:

$$
\text{NodeId}_{\text{Objects}} = \text{ns}=0;\text{i}=85
$$

$$
\text{NodeId}_{\text{Devices}} = \text{ns}=2;\text{s}=\text{Devices}
$$

$$
\text{NodeId}_{\text{TemperatureSensor}} = \text{ns}=2;\text{s}=\text{TemperatureSensor}_{01}
$$

$$
\text{NodeId}_{\text{Temperature}} = \text{ns}=2;\text{s}=\text{TemperatureSensor}_{01}\text{\_Temp}
$$

**ReferenceType** linking each parent to child: `HasComponent` (NodeId `i=47`).

**Final Read request:**

$$
\text{ReadRequest} = \{ \text{NodeId}_{\text{Temperature}}, \text{AttributeId} = 13 \;(\text{Value}) \}
$$

$$
\text{ReadResponse} = \{ \text{Value} = 23.7, \text{StatusCode} = 0x00000000 \;(\text{Good}), \text{SourceTimestamp} = T_1 \}
$$

## 3.3 Comparison Derivation: OPC Classic vs OPC UA Communication Cost

For a system reading **N** tags at update rate **R** (Hz), the **network bandwidth** required is:

$$
B_{\text{Classic}} = N \cdot R \cdot (H_{\text{DCOM}} + P_{\text{payload}})
$$

For OPC UA binary over TCP:

$$
B_{\text{UA}} = N \cdot R \cdot (H_{\text{UA}} + P_{\text{payload}})
$$

Where:
- $H_{\text{DCOM}} \approx 84 \; \text{bytes}$ (COM/DCOM header overhead per call)
- $H_{\text{UA}} \approx 24 \; \text{bytes}$ (compact binary header per message)
- $P_{\text{payload}}$ = actual data bytes (8 bytes for a `Double`)

For **N = 1000 tags, R = 10 Hz, P = 8 bytes**:

$$
B_{\text{Classic}} = 1000 \times 10 \times (84 + 8) = 920{,}000 \; \text{bytes/s} \approx 7.03 \; \text{Mbps}
$$

$$
B_{\text{UA}} = 1000 \times 10 \times (24 + 8) = 320{,}000 \; \text{bytes/s} \approx 2.44 \; \text{Mbps}
$$

**Result:** OPC UA is **~2.9× more bandwidth-efficient** than OPC Classic for bulk tag monitoring. (Subscriptions with batching amplify this advantage further.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OPC Classic — Driver Matrix vs OPC Unified Approach

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEV["DEVICES (Field Level)"]
        PLC1["Siemens S7 PLC"]
        PLC2["Allen-Bradley PLC"]
        PLC3["Yaskawa PLC"]
    end

    subgraph APP["APPLICATIONS (Supervisory Level)"]
        SCADA1["Wonderware SCADA"]
        SCADA2["WinCC HMI"]
        HIS["Historian"]
    end

    PLC1 -- "Custom Driver A1" --> SCADA1
    PLC1 -- "Custom Driver A2" --> SCADA2
    PLC1 -- "Custom Driver A3" --> HIS
    PLC2 -- "Custom Driver B1" --> SCADA1
    PLC2 -- "Custom Driver B2" --> SCADA2
    PLC2 -- "Custom Driver B3" --> HIS
    PLC3 -- "Custom Driver C1" --> SCADA1
    PLC3 -- "Custom Driver C2" --> SCADA2
    PLC3 -- "Custom Driver C3" --> HIS

    style DEV fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style APP fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366
```

> **Interpretation:** Without OPC, **3 devices × 3 applications = 9 custom drivers** are required.

## 4.2 OPC-Solved Architecture — Unified Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEV2["DEVICES"]
        PLC1b["Siemens S7 PLC"]
        PLC2b["Allen-Bradley PLC"]
        PLC3b["Yaskawa PLC"]
    end

    subgraph OPCSERVERS["OPC SERVERS (3 total)"]
        OPCSRV1["OPC Server 1\nSiemens"]
        OPCSRV2["OPC Server 2\nAB"]
        OPCSRV3["OPC Server 3\nYaskawa"]
    end

    subgraph APPCLIENTS["OPC CLIENTS (3 total)"]
        OPCCL1["OPC Client\nWonderware"]
        OPCCL2["OPC Client\nWinCC"]
        OPCCL3["OPC Client\nHistorian"]
    end

    PLC1b --> OPCSRV1
    PLC2b --> OPCSRV2
    PLC3b --> OPCSRV3
    OPCSRV1 --- OPCCL1
    OPCSRV1 --- OPCCL2
    OPCSRV1 --- OPCCL3
    OPCSRV2 --- OPCCL1
    OPCSRV2 --- OPCCL2
    OPCSRV2 --- OPCCL3
    OPCSRV3 --- OPCCL1
    OPCSRV3 --- OPCCL2
    OPCSRV3 --- OPCCL3

    style DEV2 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style OPCSERVERS fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600
    style APPCLIENTS fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366
```

> **Interpretation:** With OPC, only **3 servers + 3 clients = 6** interfaces are required — a 33% reduction, and the architecture scales linearly.

## 4.3 OPC UA Layered Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1: APPLICATIONS / CLIENTS"]
        APP1["SCADA / HMI"]
        APP2["MES / ERP"]
        APP3["Analytics / Cloud"]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2: OPC UA SERVICES (37 Services)"]
        SVC1["Discovery\nFindServers, GetEndpoints"]
        SVC2["Secure Channel\nOpen/Close Channel"]
        SVC3["Session\nCreate/Activate"]
        SVC4["Address Space\nBrowse, Read, Write"]
        SVC5["Subscriptions\nCreateMonitoredItems, Publish"]
        SVC6["Pub/Sub\nPublisher, Subscriber"]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3: INFORMATION MODELING"]
        INF1["Node Classes\nObject, Variable, Method"]
        INF2["Reference Types\nHasComponent, HasProperty"]
        INF3["Standard Types\nBaseObjectType, AnalogItemType"]
        INF4["Companion Specs\nMachinery, PA-DIM, PLCopen"]
    end

    subgraph L4["LAYER 4: ENCODING + TRANSPORT"]
        ENC1["Binary\nCompact"]
        ENC2["XML\nSOAP"]
        ENC3["JSON\nHTTPS, WebSockets"]
        TRP1["TCP / TSN"]
        TRP2["HTTP / HTTPS"]
        TRP3["MQTT / AMQP"]
    end

    subgraph L5["LAYER 5: SECURITY"]
        SEC1["Authentication\nX.509, UserName"]
        SEC2["Authorization\nRole-based ACL"]
        SEC3["Encryption\nAES-256"]
        SEC4["Signing\nSHA-256, RSA-PSS"]
    end

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L2 --> L4
    L2 --> L5

    style L1 fill:#e5f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style L2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600
    style L3 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600
    style L4 fill:#ffe5f0,stroke:#990066
    style L5 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
```

## 4.4 OPC UA Address Space — Hierarchical Node Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["Root Node (i=0)"]
    OBJ["Objects Folder (i=85)"]
    TYP["Types Folder (i=86)"]
    VIEWS["Views Folder (i=87)"]

    DEV["Devices"]
    SEN["TemperatureSensor_01"]
    TEMPVAR["Temperature (Double)"]
    METH["Reset() Method"]

    BASE_OBJ["BaseObjectType"]
    DEV_TYPE["DeviceType"]
    SEN_TYPE["TemperatureSensorType"]

    BASE_VAR["BaseDataVariableType"]
    ANA_TYPE["AnalogItemType"]
    TEMP_TYPE["TemperatureVariableType"]

    ROOT --> OBJ
    ROOT --> TYP
    ROOT --> VIEWS

    OBJ --> DEV
    DEV --> SEN
    SEN --> TEMPVAR
    SEN --> METH

    TYP --> BASE_OBJ
    BASE_OBJ --> DEV_TYPE
    BASE_OBJ --> SEN_TYPE

    TYP --> BASE_VAR
    BASE_VAR --> ANA_TYPE
    BASE_VAR --> TEMP_TYPE

    SEN_TYPE -. "HasTypeDefinition" .-> DEV_TYPE
    TEMPVAR -. "HasTypeDefinition" .-> ANA_TYPE
    DEV -. "Organizes" .-> SEN

    style ROOT fill:#003366,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OBJ fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style TYP fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style VIEWS fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style TEMPVAR fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style METH fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#006600
    style DEV_TYPE fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066
```

> **Note:** Solid arrows show hierarchical references (`HasComponent`/`Organizes`); dashed arrows show type-definition references.

## 4.5 OPC UA Session Establishment Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as OPC UA Client
    participant D as Discovery Endpoint
    participant S as OPC UA Server

    C->>D: 1. FindServers (UDP multicast :4840)
    D-->>C: 2. List of ApplicationDescriptions
    C->>S: 3. GetEndpoints (opc.tcp://host:4840)
    S-->>C: 4. Endpoint list (policies, modes, tokens)
    C->>S: 5. OpenSecureChannelRequest (ClientCert, Nonce)
    S-->>C: 6. OpenSecureChannelResponse (ServerCert, Nonce, ChannelId)
    C->>S: 7. CreateSessionRequest (ClientNonce, ClientCert)
    S-->>C: 8. CreateSessionResponse (SessionId, AuthToken, ServerNonce)
    C->>S: 9. ActivateSessionRequest (UserIdentityToken, ClientSignature)
    S-->>C: 10. ActivateSessionResponse (Status: Good)
    C->>S: 11. BrowseRequest (starting from Objects)
    S-->>C: 12. BrowseResponse (NodeId list)
    C->>S: 13. ReadRequest (NodeId of Temperature)
    S-->>C: 14. ReadResponse (Value=23.7, Good)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define OPC. List any four limitations of OPC Classic.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**OPC (Open Platform Communications)** is an open, vendor-independent interoperability standard for industrial automation that defines a common interface for data exchange between devices (PLCs, DCS, sensors) and applications (SCADA, HMI, MES, ERP). It was developed by the OPC Foundation (1994).

**Limitations of OPC Classic (any 4):**

1. **Windows-only dependency** — Built on COM/DCOM, restricting it to Microsoft Windows platforms. (½ m)
2. **DCOM security complexity** — Configuring Windows DCOM, firewall rules, and authentication across machines is notoriously difficult. (½ m)
3. **No information model** — Data is exposed as flat tag names with no semantic context. (½ m)
4. **No inherent security** — Security relies entirely on Windows DCOM ACLs; no encryption or signing. (½ m)
5. **No cross-platform portability** — Cannot run on Linux, embedded systems, or mobile devices. (½ m)
6. **Firewall-unfriendly** — Uses dynamic RPC port allocation, requiring broad port ranges to be opened. (½ m)

> **Marking:** Definition = 1 mark; four limitations = ½ mark each.

---

### Q2. What is an OPC UA Companion Specification? Give two examples.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **OPC UA Companion Specification** is a standardized information model defined jointly by the OPC Foundation and an industry consortium that defines a set of **ObjectTypes, VariableTypes, ReferenceTypes, and Methods** specific to a particular industry vertical. Companion specs ensure semantic interoperability — a Siemens controller and a Rockwell controller expose the *same* representation of a "motor" if both implement the OPC UA for Machinery spec.

**Examples (any 2 for 1 mark each):**
- **OPC UA for Machinery** (jointly with VDMA) — pumps, motors, drives
- **OPC UA for Process Automation / PA-DIM** — analog/digital I/O, process tags
- **OPC UA for PLCopen** — IEC 61131-3 program organization units (POUs)
- **OPC UA for Field Level Communication (FLC)** — IO-Link, RFID
- **OPC UA for Robotics**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided *between* questions, not within sub-parts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of OPC UA in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the three conceptual layers: Information Modeling, Services, and Transport.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** OPC UA (Unified Architecture) is a service-oriented, platform-independent industrial communication framework standardized as IEC 62541. It unifies the functionality of OPC DA, HDA, and AE into a single coherent specification.

**Layer 1 — Information Modeling (Address Space) [2 marks]:**
- The information model is a graph of **Nodes** forming the **Address Space**.
- Every entity (object, variable, method, type) is a Node.
- Each Node has:
  - **NodeId** — unique identifier (namespace URI + identifier)
  - **NodeClass** — Object, Variable, Method, ObjectType, VariableType, ReferenceType, DataType
  - **Attributes** — BrowseName, DisplayName, Description, Value, DataType, NodeClass, WriteMask, UserWriteMask
  - **References** — typed links to other nodes (e.g., `HasComponent`, `HasProperty`, `HasTypeDefinition`)
- The base Address Space contains three root folders: **Objects** (data instances), **Types** (definitions), **Views** (filtered subsets).

**Layer 2 — Services [2 marks]:**
- OPC UA defines **37 services** organized into 11 service sets.
- Important sets: Discovery, SecureChannel, Session, View (Browse), Attribute (Read/Write), MonitoredItem, Subscription, Method.
- Each service is a request/response message or a publish/notify message.
- Services are **abstract** — they do not depend on encoding or transport.

**Layer 3 — Transport + Encoding [1 mark]:**
- Services are encoded in **Binary** (compact, for TCP), **XML** (SOAP over HTTP), or **JSON** (for HTTPS/WebSockets).
- Transport options: `opc.tcp://` (most common), `https://`, `opc.wss://` (WebSockets).

**Companion Specifications (1 mark):**
Industry-specific extensions (e.g., Machinery, PA-DIM, PLCopen) are layered on top of the base model.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat sequence diagram, explain the OPC UA session establishment process. List the security mechanisms provided by OPC UA.

**Model Answer:**

**Sequence Diagram (4 marks):** (Already drawn in Section 4.5 — reproduce on answer script)
1. FindServers (UDP multicast `:4840`)
2. GetEndpoints → list of (URL, Policy, Mode, IdentityTokens)
3. OpenSecureChannelRequest → negotiated security, certificates exchanged
4. CreateSessionRequest → SessionId, AuthenticationToken, ServerNonce
5. ActivateSessionRequest → user identity token, signed
6. Browse → Address Space
7. Read/Write/Monitor → data exchange

**Security Mechanisms (3 marks):**
1. **Authentication** — X.509 ApplicationInstanceCertificates; username/password tokens; user certificates. (1 m)
2. **Authorization** — Role-based access control on nodes and methods; configurable per session. (1 m)
3. **Confidentiality & Integrity** — AES-128/256 encryption + SHA-256 signing using security policies like `Aes256_Sha256_RsaPss`; three modes: None, Sign, SignAndEncrypt. (1 m)

> **Bonus (½ mark):** Discuss SecurityPolicies vs MessageSecurityMode, audit logging, transport security (TLS 1.2/1.3 for HTTPS).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compare OPC Classic and OPC UA in terms of architecture, platform support, security, information modeling, and transport. Use a comparison table.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | OPC Classic (1.4 marks) | OPC UA (1.4 marks) |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **Architecture** | COM/DCOM Client-Server only | Client-Server + Pub/Sub |
| **Platform Support** | Windows only (DCOM dependency) | Windows, Linux, RTOS, embedded, cloud |
| **Security** | Windows DCOM ACLs (no encryption) | Built-in: X.509, AES, SHA-256, RBAC |
| **Information Model** | Flat tag list (ItemID strings) | Rich object-oriented graph (Node + References) |
| **Transport** | RPC/DCOM (dynamic ports, firewall-unfriendly) | opc.tcp://, https://, opc.wss:// |
| **Discoverability** | Manual configuration | Discovery Service Set (LDS, mDNS) |
| **Specifications** | Separate: DA, HDA, AE, DX, Security | Single unified spec (IEC 62541) |
| **Encoding** | COM binary | Binary, XML, JSON |
| **Semantic Interop** | None (vendor-specific tag names) | Companion Specifications |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** OPC UA is the modern, secure, platform-independent successor that is essential for Industry 4.0 and IIoT applications. OPC Classic is now considered legacy and is being phased out.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the OPC UA Address Space and Information Modeling with a suitable example. List the main Node classes.

**Model Answer:**

**Address Space (3 marks):**
- The Address Space is a directed graph of **Nodes** representing all data, types, and references exposed by the server.
- All Clients interact with the server **exclusively** through the Address Space — there is no "out-of-band" data access.
- Each Node has a unique `NodeId` (e.g., `ns=2;s=TemperatureSensor_01.Temperature`) and a set of attributes.
- **Three root folders**: Objects (i=85), Types (i=86), Views (i=87).

**Node Classes (2 marks):**
1. **Object** — represents a system entity (folder, device)
2. **Variable** — represents a data value
3. **Method** — represents a callable function
4. **ObjectType** — type definition for objects
5. **VariableType** — type definition for variables
6. **ReferenceType** — typed relationship between nodes
7. **DataType** — data type definition

**Example (2 marks):**
A temperature-monitoring application exposes:
- Node `ns=2;s=Devices` (Object)
- Node `ns=2;s=Devices.TempSensor01` (Object, typed by `TemperatureSensorType`)
- Node `ns=2;s=Devices.TempSensor01.Temperature` (Variable of type `Double`, typed by `AnalogItemType`)
- Node `ns=2;s=Devices.TempSensor01.Reset` (Method)

The Reference `HasComponent` connects `TempSensor01` → `Temperature` and `TempSensor01` → `Reset`. A Client can `Browse` from the Objects folder down to the Temperature variable and `Read` its current value (e.g., 23.7 °C).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam!)**
> 
> 1. **"OPC" vs "OPC UA" Confusion** — Many students treat them as synonyms. **OPC = the family/specifications; OPC UA = the specific unified architecture (Part 1–14 of IEC 62541).** Writing "OPC UA is COM-based" loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 
> 2. **No Diagram = No Full Marks** — Every 7-mark sub-part *requires* a diagram. OPC UA session/architecture questions are **visual by nature**. Practice drawing the sequence diagram and layered architecture block diagram from memory.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the Security Policy** — In any OPC UA question on security, do **not** write "OPC UA is secure" generically. Always name a **SecurityPolicy** (e.g., `Aes256_Sha256_RsaPss`) and a **MessageSecurityMode** (None / Sign / SignAndEncrypt).
> 
> 4. **Confusing DA, HDA, AE** — DA = real-time data; HDA = history; AE = alarms/events. In OPC UA these are **unified into the same Read/HistoryRead/MonitoredItem services**. Writing "OPC UA does not support alarms" is a classic wrong answer.
> 
> 5. **Omitting the Information Model** — In any OPC UA architecture answer, you **must** discuss the **Address Space + Node classes + References**. This is the single biggest differentiator from "OPC Classic" answers.
> 
> 6. **Not Using "ns" in NodeId** — When giving a NodeId example, always use the form `ns=2;s=Name` or `ns=2;i=1234`. Writing just `s=Name` is incomplete.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting the IEC Standard Number** — OPC UA is standardized as **IEC 62541**. Mentioning this shows depth and earns a half-mark bonus from strict examiners.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 One-Line Definitions
- **OPC** = Open Platform Communications; vendor-neutral industrial data-exchange standard by the OPC Foundation.
- **OPC Classic** = COM/DCOM-based, Windows-only family (DA, HDA, AE).
- **OPC UA** = Unified Architecture (IEC 62541); platform-independent, secure, semantic, service-oriented.

### 🔑 Five Pillars of OPC UA (Memorize!)
1. **Platform Independent** — any OS, any device
2. **Information Modeling** — semantic address space with typed nodes
3. **Service-Oriented** — 37 well-defined services
4. **Multi-Transport** — Binary/TCP, XML/SOAP, JSON/HTTPS, WebSockets
5. **Built-in Security** — AuthN (X.509), AuthZ (RBAC), Confidentiality (AES), Integrity (SHA-256/RSA-PSS)

### 🔑 Key Differences (Always Citable in Exams)
- OPC Classic → Windows only; OPC UA → cross-platform
- OPC Classic → flat tags; OPC UA → typed graph (Nodes + References)
- OPC Classic → no encryption; OPC UA → end-to-end encryption
- OPC Classic → 5+ specs; OPC UA → 1 unified spec
- OPC Classic → manual config; OPC UA → automatic discovery (LDS, mDNS)

### 🔑 Important Numerical / Factual Items
- OPC Foundation founded: **1994** (originally "OLE for Process Control")
- OPC UA first released: **2008**
- OPC UA international standard: **IEC 62541** (Parts 1–14)
- Default OPC UA TCP port: **4840** (discovery: 4840 UDP multicast on 239.255.255.255)
- Number of core services: **37**, organized in **11** service sets
- Three root folders of the Address Space: **Objects (i=85)**, **Types (i=86)**, **Views (i=87)**
- Common Security Policy: `Aes256_Sha256_RsaPss`
- PubSub added in: **Part 14 (2018)**
- Companion Specs for Machinery: **VDMA**; for PLC: **PLCopen**; for PA: **NAMUR/PI**

### 🔑 Common Companion Specifications (Name at Least 3 in Any UA Question)
1. OPC UA for Machinery (VDMA)
2. OPC UA for Process Automation (PA-DIM)
3. OPC UA for PLCopen (IEC 61131-3)
4. OPC UA for Field Level Communication (FLC)
5. OPC UA for Robotics
6. OPC UA for Automotive
7. OPC UA for Cloud/IIoT (part of RAMI 4.0)

### 🔑 Session Establishment Step Sequence (Cyclic)
**FindServers → GetEndpoints → OpenSecureChannel → CreateSession → ActivateSession → Browse → Read/Write/Monitor**

### 🔑 Use Cases to Mention in "Real-World Application" Parts
- **Industry 4.0 / IIoT** — sensor-to-cloud pipelines
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Control** — ISA-88 models as OPC UA types
- **Oil & Gas** — PA-DIM companion spec for refineries
- **Discrete Manufacturing** — Machinery spec for motors, pumps, drives
- **Power & Energy** — IEC 61850 + OPC UA bridging
- **Automotive** — OPC UA over TSN for body assembly lines

### 🔑 Formula / Quick Recal
Bandwidth requirement (tags per second × overhead):
$$ B = N \cdot R \cdot (H + P) $$

For N=1000 tags, R=10 Hz, P=8 B:
- OPC Classic ≈ **7.03 Mbps**
- OPC UA ≈ **2.44 Mbps**
- **UA is ~2.9× more efficient.**

### 🔑 Examiner-Approved Buzzwords to Sprinkle in Answers
- "Service-oriented architecture"
- "Address Space as a typed node graph"
- "Companion Specifications for vertical integration"
- "Defense-in-depth security model"
- "Transport-agnostic, platform-independent"
- "Semantic interoperability"
- "RAMI 4.0 reference architecture"

> **End of Module 3 — OPC / OPC UA Notes** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Automation: General Block Diagrams of DDC, Supervisory Control, Data Acquisition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Automation: General Block Diagrams of DDC, Supervisory Control, Data Acquisition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Direct Digital Control (DDC) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Direct Digital Control (DDC)** is a configuration in which a **single digital computer** (or a dedicated microprocessor-based controller) is employed to perform all the control functions of a process — including signal acquisition, control algorithm execution (PID, lead-lag, etc.), and final control element actuation — for **multiple control loops simultaneously**, without the intervention of conventional analog controllers (such as pneumatic or electronic single-loop controllers).

In DDC, the digital computer **directly** drives the final control elements (valves, variable speed drives, heaters, etc.) by computing the required manipulated variable for every loop in a time-shared, sequential, or interrupt-driven fashion.

## 1.2 Supervisory Control — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Supervisory Control** (also called **Setpoint Control** or **DDC over DDC**) is a hierarchical control strategy in which a **central supervisory digital computer** does *not* actuate the plant directly. Instead, it computes and transmits the **optimal setpoints** to a network of local analog or digital single-loop controllers (PLC, PID controllers, smart transmitters), which in turn close their individual loops in real time.

The supervisor performs optimization, coordination, scheduling, and report generation, while the local controllers perform high-bandwidth regulatory control. The standard communication speed for supervisory commands is **typically 4–20 mA** analog or **digital fieldbus (HART, Profibus, Modbus)**.

## 1.3 Data Acquisition System (DAS / DAQ) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is an integrated assembly of sensors, signal-conditioning circuits, multiplexers, analog-to-digital converters (ADC), and a digital computer (or storage media) whose primary function is the **measurement, conversion, digitization, display, logging, and post-process analysis** of one or more physical variables from an industrial process. A DAS does *not* perform closed-loop control; it operates in an *open-loop monitoring* mode.

The most common physical signal standard is the **4–20 mA current loop**, introduced by Foxboro (originally 10–50 mA in 1950s, standardized to 4–20 mA in the 1960s). The lower bound of **4 mA** is chosen so that a "live zero" is preserved, enabling **open-circuit / cable-break fault detection** (any reading < 4 mA ⇒ fault).

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — DDC as a "Multi-Tasking Pilot"**
> Imagine a single highly-skilled airline pilot flying **ten different small aircraft** simultaneously from a ground-based simulator. The pilot cannot physically touch the control yokes of all ten planes, so he *issues radio commands* at timed intervals. The aircraft auto-stabilize momentarily (holding their attitude via **memory**) until the pilot gives the next correction. In DDC, the digital computer plays the role of the pilot; the **sample-and-hold (S/H)** circuits and short-term actuators play the role of the aircraft's memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Supervisory Control as a "Factory Floor Manager"**
> A factory floor has 5 expert workers (analog/PID controllers), each perfectly running his own machine. The floor manager (supervisory computer) doesn't touch the machines. He *watches* the production, *recalculates* the targets (setpoints) every hour, and *announces* new production rates to the workers. The workers immediately implement. If the manager's computer crashes, the workers continue running their machines unchanged — **graceful degradation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — DAS as a "Hospital ICU Monitor"**
> In a hospital ICU, sensors (ECG, SpO₂, BP cuffs) continuously read patient vitals, the **signal-conditioning box** removes noise, the **multiplexer** shares one expensive display between many sensors, the **ADC** converts the waveforms into digital pixels on the monitor, and the **computer stores** the data for the doctor to review. Critically, the monitor *displays* but does *not* inject medicine — that is **monitoring, not control**.

## 1.5 Salient Comparison at a Glance

| Parameter | DDC | Supervisory Control | DAS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Computer Output Goes To | Final Control Element | Local Controllers | Display / Storage |
| Loop Closure | In the central computer | In local controllers | **No loop** (open) |
| Reliability on Computer Failure | **All loops fail** | Loops continue | Only monitoring lost |
| Typical Sampling Rate | 0.1 s to 5 s per loop | 1 s to 60 s (setpoint updates) | 1 kHz to 100 kHz (fast DAS) |
| Primary Function | Regulatory Control | Optimization & Coordination | Measurement & Logging |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of DDC

The general block diagram of a DDC system is built from these sequential functional blocks:

1. **Process** — the plant whose variable (temperature, pressure, flow, level) must be regulated.
2. **Sensors / Transducers** — convert the physical variable into a low-level electrical signal (mV, mA, Ω). Example: RTD (Pt100) → resistance; thermocouple → μV; orifice + DP cell → 4–20 mA.
3. **Signal Conditioning** — amplifies, linearizes, and **isolates** the sensor signal. Includes cold-junction compensation (CJC) for thermocouples, lead-wire compensation for RTDs (3-wire / 4-wire), and 50 Hz notch filters.
4. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — time-shares a single expensive ADC across *N* input channels using CMOS analog switches (e.g., 8-to-1, 16-to-1). Critical for cost reduction in 1970s-era DDC; less critical today with per-channel Σ-Δ ADCs.
5. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — captures the rapidly varying analog signal at a precise instant and holds it constant during the ADC conversion window. Prevents "aperture error".
6. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — quantizes the held signal into binary words of *n* bits. Successive-approximation ADC is the historical workhorse of DDC.
7. **Digital Computer (CPU + Memory)** — executes the control algorithm. For a PID controller, the **velocity (incremental) algorithm** is preferred over the positional algorithm to prevent **integral windup** during actuator saturation:

$$ \Delta m_k = K_c \left[ (e_k - e_{k-1}) + \frac{T_s}{T_i} e_k + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2}) \right] $$

8. **Output Multiplexer / Demultiplexer (DEMUX)** — routes the computed control actions to the correct actuator.
9. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — converts the digital word back to an analog signal (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) to drive the actuator.
10. **Final Control Element** — control valve (pneumatic, motorized), variable frequency drive (VFD), thyristor stack, solenoid, etc.
11. **Actuator → Process** — physical manipulation completing the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Why" Matters in DDC Design**
> The **sample period $T_s$** is the most critical design parameter. If $T_s$ is too long, the discrete controller cannot stabilize the loop. The Shannon–Nyquist sampling theorem requires $T_s \le T_{\text{process}} / 2$ (where $T_{\text{process}}$ is the dominant time constant), and **practical engineering rule** is $T_s \approx T_{\text{process}} / 10$ for tight control or $T_s \approx T_{\text{process}} / 4$ for moderate control.

## 2.2 Functional Architecture of Supervisory Control

1. **Central Supervisory Computer** (often a VAX, PDP-11, or today an industrial PC with SCADA/HMI software).
2. **Communication Link** to the field — RS-485, Ethernet/IP, Profibus, Modbus TCP, Foundation Fieldbus.
3. **Local Single-Loop Controllers** — accept remote setpoint (RSP) commands; otherwise operate as standalone PID regulators. Examples: Honeywell UDC 2500, Siemens SIPART DR24, Yokogawa UT series.
4. **Process & Sensors** — same as DDC.
5. The local controllers continuously measure the controlled variable and *act independently*. The supervisor periodically (typically every 1–60 s) recomputes the optimal setpoints based on economic models, energy costs, product grade transitions, or cascaded optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Tier Hierarchy**
> Supervisory control is the **top tier** of the classic three-tier automation pyramid:
> - **Tier 0:** Field devices (sensors, valves)
> - **Tier 1:** Local control (PLCs, DCS controllers, single-loop regulators)
> - **Tier 2:** Supervisory (SCADA, HMI, historian)
> - **Tier 3:** Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES) — beyond the scope of KTU 521.

## 2.3 Functional Architecture of a Data Acquisition System

1. **Transducer** — converts physical quantity to electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioner** — amplification, filtering, linearization, isolation (often using an **instrumentation amplifier** like INA128 with CMRR > 100 dB).
3. **Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** — **crucial and unique to DAS**. A Butterworth or Bessel filter with cutoff $f_c < f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing.
4. **Multiplexer** — shares one ADC across N channels. Used in **simultaneous-sampling DAS** with per-channel S/H or in **scanning DAS** with a single S/H at the output of the MUX.
5. **S/H** — latches the signal during ADC conversion.
6. **ADC** — successive-approximation, dual-slope, or Σ-Δ.
7. **Digital Computer / Storage** — formats, timestamps (using a **real-time clock**), stores to disk/RAM, displays, and transmits.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Frequency (Nyquist) | $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$ | Hz; $f_{\max}$ = highest signal frequency |
| 2 | Recommended Sampling Rate | $f_s \approx 10 / T_{\text{dom}}$ | $T_{\text{dom}}$ = dominant plant time constant |
| 3 | ADC Quantization Step (LSB) | $Q = \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n}$ | Volts; $V_{\text{FS}}$ = full-scale input range |
| 4 | Quantization Error (max) | $e_q = \pm \dfrac{Q}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Volts; uniformly distributed |
| 5 | Theoretical SNR of an ideal ADC | $\text{SNR} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB; for full-scale sine input |
| 6 | 4–20 mA to Engineering Units | $E = E_{\min} + \dfrac{I - 4}{20 - 4} (E_{\max} - E_{\min})$ | E = measured engineering value |
| 7 | Resolution (% of full scale) | $\text{Res}(\%) = \dfrac{100}{2^n}$ | For *n*-bit ADC |
| 8 | Multiplexer Channel Settling Time | $t_{\text{settle}} \le 0.1\,T_s$ | Rule of thumb |
| 9 | PID Incremental Output | $\Delta m_k = K_c\!\left[ (e_k - e_{k-1}) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i}e_k + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2}) \right]$ | $K_c$ = gain, $T_i$ = integral time, $T_d$ = derivative time |
| 10 | Number of Loops in DDC | $N = \dfrac{T_{\text{total scan}}}{T_s}$ | Limited by $T_s$ and CPU speed |
| 11 | Scan Time (Sequential DDC) | $T_{\text{scan}} = N \cdot (t_{\text{conv}} + t_{\text{MUX}} + t_{\text{algo}})$ | Seconds |
| 12 | Aliasing Folding Frequency | $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_s - f_{\text{in}} \vert$ | Hz; AAF must reject this |
| 13 | Instrumentation Amp Gain | $G = 1 + \dfrac{49.4\,k\Omega}{R_G}$ | For INA128/INA129 |
| 14 | Thermocouple Sensitivity (K-type) | $\approx 41\,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$ | Approximate; non-linear (ITS-90) |
| 15 | RTD (Pt100) Sensitivity | $\approx 0.385\,\Omega/^\circ\text{C}$ | At 0 °C; $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva**: Every KTU examiner expects you to remember the **Shannon-Nyquist sampling theorem**, the **ADC LSB formula**, the **4–20 mA conversion**, and the **incremental PID** — these four are guaranteed marks in Part A and Part B.

## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **DDC** was the dominant architecture in 1970s–1990s power plants, cement kilns, and steel rolling mills. The Honeywell TDC 2000 / TDC 3000 systems used DDC in their lowest-level controllers.
- **Supervisory Control** is the **backbone of modern DCS** — every ABB 800xA, Emerson DeltaV, Honeywell Experion, and Yokogawa CENTUM system uses hierarchical supervisory control.
- **DAS** is the workhorse of **test benches, vibration analysis, R&D labs, and quality control** in steel and paper mills. Modern "DAQ cards" from National Instruments (NI-DAQmx) plug into a PC and provide up to **2 MS/s** per channel on 16-bit ADCs.
- Combined: a DCS = **DAS + DDC + Supervisory** running on redundant fault-tolerant controllers. The KTU examiner will likely ask you to draw a *combined* diagram showing all three.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Shannon–Nyquist Sampling Theorem (the most important KTU derivation for this module)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently award **2 marks** for a clean derivation of the sampling theorem. The proof below is the standard one shown in textbooks like Ogata's *Discrete-Time Control Systems* and Oppenheim's *Signals and Systems*.

### 3.1.1 Statement

A band-limited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ having no spectral components above a frequency $f_{\max}$ Hz can be **uniquely reconstructed** from its samples $x(kT_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s > 2 f_{\max}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Impulse Sampling Model.**  
The sampled signal is modelled as the product of $x(t)$ and a Dirac comb (impulse train) $p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT_s)$:

$$ x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(kT_s)\,\delta(t - kT_s) $$

**Step 2 — Take the Fourier Transform.**  
Using the multiplication-in-time / convolution-in-frequency property of the Fourier transform, the spectrum of the impulse train is itself an impulse train in the frequency domain:

$$ P(j\omega) = \frac{2\pi}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(\omega - k\omega_s\right) $$

where $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s = 2\pi / T_s$ is the sampling radian frequency.

**Step 3 — Convolution in Frequency Domain.**  
Multiplication in time-domain becomes convolution in frequency domain:

$$ X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} X(j\omega) * P(j\omega) $$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Convolve.**  
Convolution of $X(j\omega)$ with each shifted impulse simply *replicates* $X(j\omega)$ at the impulse location, scaled by the area of the impulse ($2\pi/T_s$):

$$ X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\right) $$

**Step 5 — Interpret the Result.**  
The spectrum of the sampled signal is an **infinite periodic repetition** of the original spectrum $X(j\omega)$, centered at $\omega = 0, \pm\omega_s, \pm 2\omega_s, \dots$ and scaled by $1/T_s$.

**Step 6 — Condition for Non-Overlap (No Aliasing).**  
The replicas will not overlap in the frequency domain if and only if the original spectrum is fully contained within $\pm \omega_s/2$:

$$ \omega_{\max} < \frac{\omega_s}{2} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad f_{\max} < \frac{f_s}{2} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad f_s > 2 f_{\max} $$

**Step 7 — Reconstruction (Ideal Low-Pass Filter).**  
A realisable low-pass filter with cutoff $\omega_c = \omega_s/2$ can extract the original baseband spectrum, scaled by $T_s$, to recover $x(t)$ exactly. This is the **Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula**:

$$ x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(kT_s)\, \operatorname{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - kT_s}{T_s}\right) $$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: In real DDC and DAS systems, the **anti-aliasing filter** (AAF) is the practical stand-in for the *ideal* low-pass filter of step 7. The AAF is placed **before the S/H** (not after) to ensure that any frequency component above $f_s/2$ is removed *before* the spectrum replicas are created by the sampler.

## 3.2 Derivation: ADC Quantization Step and SNR

**Step 1 — Full-Scale Range Division.**  
An $n$-bit ADC divides the full-scale input range $V_{\text{FS}}$ into $2^n$ equally-spaced quantum levels. The width of one quantum (the LSB) is therefore:

$$ Q = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} $$

**Step 2 — Maximum Quantization Error.**  
Because the actual analog value can lie anywhere within one quantum, the maximum error is half a quantum, occurring at the worst-case quantization point:

$$ e_{q,\max} = \frac{Q}{2} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^{n+1}} $$

**Step 3 — RMS Quantization Noise (uniform distribution assumption).**  
For a uniformly distributed quantization error $e_q \in [-Q/2, +Q/2]$, the variance is:

$$ \sigma_e^2 = \frac{Q^2}{12} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n} $$

**Step 4 — Signal Power of Full-Scale Sine Wave.**  
A sine wave of amplitude $A = V_{\text{FS}}/2$ has RMS value $A/\sqrt{2} = V_{\text{FS}}/(2\sqrt{2})$, so signal power is:

$$ P_{\text{signal}} = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}^2}{8} $$

**Step 5 — SNR Calculation.**  
The Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB is:

$$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{FS}}^2 / 8}{V_{\text{FS}}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^n)}\right) $$

$$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.5 \cdot 4^n\right) $$

**Step 6 — Final Simplified Form.**  
Using $\log_{10}(4^n) = 2n \log_{10}(2) = 0.602\,n$:

$$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 6.02\,n = 1.76 + 6.02\,n $$

This is the famous "**6 dB per bit**" rule of thumb used in audio and instrumentation. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation: 4–20 mA to Engineering Units (Linear Conversion)

The 4–20 mA current standard maps linearly to the engineering range $[E_{\min}, E_{\max}]$ as follows:

**Step 1 — Span in Current.**  
The current span is $20 - 4 = 16$ mA.

**Step 2 — Span in Engineering Units.**  
The engineering span is $E_{\max} - E_{\min}$.

**Step 3 — Linear Interpolation.**  
For a measured current $I$ mA, the engineering value is:

$$ E = E_{\min} + (I - 4) \cdot \frac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{20 - 4} = E_{\min} + (I - 4) \cdot \frac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{16} $$

**Worked Numerical Example.**  
A pressure transmitter has a range of 0–10 bar mapped to 4–20 mA. The DCS reads 12 mA. Compute the pressure.

**Step 1 — Identify** $E_{\min} = 0$ bar, $E_{\max} = 10$ bar, $I = 12$ mA.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the formula:**

$$ E = 0 + (12 - 4) \cdot \frac{10 - 0}{16} = 8 \cdot \frac{10}{16} = 8 \cdot 0.625 = 5.0 \text{ bar} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check**: 12 mA is exactly halfway between 4 and 20 mA, so the answer *must* be exactly halfway between 0 and 10 bar → **5 bar**. The formula is consistent. $\checkmark$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Architecture

| Criterion | DDC | Supervisory Control | DAS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of loops to handle | 8–500 | 100–10 000 | N/A (no loops) |
| Required sampling period | Short (0.1–2 s) | Moderate (1–60 s) | Variable (10 µs to 1 s) |
| Computer reliability required | **Very High** (single point of failure) | Moderate (graceful degradation) | Low (redundant DAS) |
| Algorithm complexity | High (per-loop PID + supervisory) | Optimization (LP, MPC) | Minimal (just collect) |
| Cost per loop | **Lowest** (one computer, time-shared) | Highest (controllers + supervisor) | Highest (precision ADCs) |
| Historical era | 1960s–1990s | 1980s–present | 1950s–present |
| Modern representative | DCS Controller (e.g., DeltaV MD) | SCADA / DCS Supervisor | NI PXI / LabVIEW |

## 4.1 General Block Diagram of a DDC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    out1[ACTUATOR OUTPUT TO PROCESS]

    proc1 -->|Physical Variable T P F L| sens1
    sens1 -->|mV or 4 to 20 mA| sc1
    sc1 -->|Conditioned Signal| aaf1
    aaf1 -->|Band Limited| mux1
    mux1 -->|Time Shared Channel| sh1
    sh1 -->|Held Voltage| adc1
    adc1 -->|Digital Word| cpu1
    cpu1 -->|Digital Control Word| demux1

    style cpu1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px

```

### 4.1.1 Block-by-Block Explanation of the DDC Diagram

- **PROCESS PLANT** → the physical system (e.g., heat exchanger, tank, reactor).
- **SENSOR / TRANSDUCER** → PT100 RTD for temperature, DP cell for pressure, magnetic flow meter for flow, ultrasonic for level.
- **SIGNAL CONDITIONER** → instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128), cold-junction compensation, linearization ROM, isolation barrier.
- **ANTI-ALIASING FILTER** → 2nd-order Butterworth, $f_c = f_s/2.5$ (with safety margin).
- **MULTIPLEXER (MUX)** → CD4051, ADG708, MAX306 — typically 8:1 or 16:1.
- **SAMPLE AND HOLD (S/H)** → LF398, AD582 — aperture time in nanoseconds.
- **ADC** → AD574 (12-bit, 25 µs), AD7606 (16-bit, 200 kS/s), ADS1256 (24-bit Σ-Δ).
- **DIGITAL COMPUTER** → 8086 era: PDP-11, Intel MULTIBUS; modern: VME, cPCI backplane DCS controller.
- **DEMUX + DAC** → AD558, DAC0808.
- **FINAL CONTROL ELEMENT** → pneumatic positioner (I/P converter), motorized valve, VFD.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    ctrlB[LOCAL CONTROLLER LOOP 2 PID]
    ctrlC[LOCAL CONTROLLER LOOP 3 PID]

    supComp --> optMod
    optMod --> commLink

    commLink -->|Setpoint 2| ctrlB
    commLink -->|Setpoint 3| ctrlC
    ctrlA --> valA
    ctrlB --> valB
    ctrlC --> valC
    valA --> proc2
    valB --> proc2
    valC --> proc2
    proc2 --> sens2
    sens2 -->|PV 1| ctrlA
    sens2 -->|PV 2| ctrlB
    sens2 -->|PV 3| ctrlC

    style supComp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style optMod fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style ctrlA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
    style ctrlB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
    style ctrlC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2.1 Key Observations on the Supervisory Diagram

- The **supervisory computer** never *touches* the valves directly. The arrows from `commLink` carry **setpoints**, not control signals.
- The local controllers form **three independent closed loops** with the process. If the supervisor fails, the loops continue running on the **last received setpoint** ("bumpless transfer" / "fall-back to local").
- The **historian** (database) is what distinguishes a modern supervisory system from a 1980s one — it stores PV, SP, OP, alarms, and events at sub-second resolution for months.

## 4.3 General Block Diagram of a Data Acquisition System (DAS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    physProc[PHYSICAL PROCESS]
    tr[TRANSDUCER]
    ia[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER]
    aaf2[ANTI ALIASING FILTER]
    mux2[MULTIPLEXER]
    sh2[SAMPLE AND HOLD]
    adc2[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER]
    buf[THREE STATE BUFFER]
    cpu2[MICROCOMPUTER CPU RAM ROM]
    rtc[REAL TIME CLOCK]
    lcd[DISPLAY LCD LED]
    storage[MASS STORAGE HDD SSD]
    prn[PRINTER PLOTTER]

    physProc -->|Physical Quantity| tr
    tr -->|Low Level mV or 4 to 20 mA| ia
    ia -->|Amplified Differential| aaf2
    aaf2 -->|Band Limited| mux2
    mux2 --> sh2
    sh2 --> adc2
    adc2 -->|Digital Bits| buf
    buf --> cpu2
    rtc -->|Time Stamp| cpu2
    cpu2 --> lcd
    cpu2 --> storage
    cpu2 --> prn

    style cpu2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style physProc fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style storage fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3.1 Key DAS-Specific Block Features

- **No feedback arrow back to the process** — DAS is open-loop.
- The **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** (e.g., DS1307, PCF85263) provides a hardware time-stamp for every sample, critical for post-event forensic analysis.
- **Three-State Buffers** isolate the ADC data bus from the CPU bus; they are *tri-stated* when not selected, preventing bus contention.
- The **Anti-Aliasing Filter** is *non-negotiable* in DAS — missing it is the #1 mistake students make in DAS block diagrams (the KTU examiner will mark you down for it).

## 4.4 Integrated "Combined" Architecture (DAS + DDC + Supervisory in one DCS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tier3[SUPERVISORY COMPUTER OPTIMIZATION MES]
    tier2[OPERATOR STATION HMI SCADA]
    tier1[DCS CONTROLLER DDC ALGORITHM]
    tier0a[FIELDBUS TO SENSORS]
    tier0b[FIELDBUS TO VALVES]
    das[DAS SUBSYSTEM]
    proc3[INDUSTRIAL PROCESS]

    tier3 -->|Setpoints and Targets| tier2
    tier2 -->|Commands| tier1
    tier1 -->|Actuation Signals| tier0b
    tier0b --> proc3
    proc3 -->|Measured Variables| tier0a
    tier0a --> das
    das -->|Digitized PV| tier1
    tier1 -->|Display Data| tier2
    tier2 -->|Reports| tier3

    style tier3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style tier2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style tier1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px
    style das fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style proc3 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4.1 How the Three Systems Coexist in a Real DCS

- The **DAS** quietly digitizes *all* sensor signals — hundreds of them, mostly for monitoring, with only a subset feeding the control loops.
- The **DDC algorithm** inside the DCS controller uses the digitized PV values to compute control moves in real time.
- The **Supervisory computer** receives the displayed data, runs optimization, and pushes new setpoints back down.
- This three-in-one architecture is exactly what a **Honeywell Experion, ABB 800xA, or Emerson DeltaV** system looks like under the hood.
