# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part A consists of short-answer questions. Two representative questions are given with precise model answers.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model] — **3 Marks**

**Differentiate between Direct Digital Control (DDC) and Supervisory Control. List any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Direct Digital Control (DDC) | Supervisory Control |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | The central digital computer directly computes and outputs the **control signal** to the final control element. | The central computer outputs only the **setpoint** to local analog/digital controllers, which then perform control. |
| 2 | Failure of the central computer leads to **loss of control of all loops**. | Failure of the central computer leads to **graceful degradation**; local loops continue on the last setpoint. |
| 3 | Requires a **single, highly reliable** computer. | Reliability is **distributed**; local controllers can be independent units. |
| 4 | Suited for **smaller plants** with moderate loop count. | Suited for **large, complex plants** with many loops. |

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] — **3 Marks**

**Define a Data Acquisition System. With the help of a block diagram, list its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is an integrated electronic system used to **measure, digitize, display, store, and analyze** one or more physical variables (temperature, pressure, flow, strain, vibration) from a process or experiment. It does **not perform closed-loop control**; its purpose is **monitoring and recording**.

**Main Functional Blocks:**
1. **Transducer / Sensor** — converts the physical variable into an electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioner** — instrumentation amplifier, filter, linearizer.
3. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** — low-pass filter with cutoff ≤ $f_s/2$.
4. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — time-shares one ADC across many channels.
5. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — freezes the signal during conversion.
6. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — quantizes the analog signal to a binary word.
7. **Digital Computer / Microcontroller** — formats, timestamps, displays, stores.
8. **Display / Storage / Recording Devices** — LCD, HDD, printer, cloud.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B features an **internal choice**. Two fully-worked alternatives are provided below.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — Internal Choice Option 1]**

**Q.A (a)** Draw the **general block diagram of a Direct Digital Control (DDC) system** and explain the function of each block in detail. **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

A typical DDC system has the following blocks (as shown in Section 4.1):

1. **Process** — the plant (e.g., a furnace whose temperature is to be controlled).
2. **Sensor / Transducer** — RTD, thermocouple, or pressure transmitter produces an electrical signal proportional to the controlled variable.
3. **Signal Conditioner** — amplifies, linearizes, and isolates the raw transducer output. Uses an **instrumentation amplifier (INA128)** with **CMRR > 100 dB**.
4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF)** — second-order Butterworth low-pass filter to band-limit the signal to ≤ $f_s/2$, preventing aliasing.
5. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — time-shares one expensive ADC across N input channels (typically 8 or 16).
6. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — holds the input voltage constant during ADC conversion (aperture time typically < 1 µs).
7. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts the held analog voltage to a digital word; for an **n-bit** ADC, the LSB is $Q = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^n$.
8. **Digital Computer** — executes the control algorithm (PID) for every loop in time-shared fashion. The **incremental (velocity) PID algorithm** is most common.
9. **Demultiplexer (DEMUX)** — routes the computed control action to the correct actuator.
10. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — converts the digital control word back to an analog signal.
11. **Final Control Element** — control valve, VFD, heater — manipulates the process.
12. **Actuator Output → Process** — completes the feedback loop.

**Q.A (b)** A 12-bit ADC is used in a DDC system with a full-scale input range of **0–10 V**. Calculate (i) the LSB, (ii) the maximum quantization error, and (iii) the theoretical SNR. **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{\text{FS}} = 10$ V.

**(i) LSB (Quantization Step):**

$$ Q = \frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} \approx 2.441 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.441 \text{ mV} $$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$ e_{q,\max} = \frac{Q}{2} = \frac{2.441}{2} \approx 1.221 \text{ mV} $$

**(iii) Theoretical SNR (sine wave input):**

$$ \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB} $$

**Tabular Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| LSB ($Q$) | 2.441 | mV |
| Maximum Quantization Error | 1.221 | mV |
| Theoretical SNR | 74.0 | dB |
| Number of Quantization Levels | 4096 | levels |

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model — Internal Choice Option 2]**

**Q.B (a)** Draw the **general block diagram of a Supervisory Control system** and explain how it differs from a DDC system. **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

The block diagram (Section 4.2) consists of:

1. **Central Supervisory Computer** (SCADA / DCS Station) — runs optimization, alarm management, historian.
2. **Communication Network** — RS-485, Modbus, Ethernet/IP, Foundation Fieldbus H1.
3. **Local Single-Loop Controllers** — PID / PLC controllers that accept a **Remote Setpoint (RSP)**.
4. **Process Plant** with **Sensors** and **Final Control Elements (Valves)**.

**Differences from DDC:**

| Aspect | DDC | Supervisory Control |
|---|---|---|
| Computer Output | Direct to actuator | Setpoint to local controller |
| Computer Role | Regulatory control + calculation | Optimization + coordination only |
| Loop Closure | Inside the computer | Inside local controllers |
| Fault Tolerance | **Poor** (single point of failure) | **High** (graceful degradation) |
| Scalability | Limited by CPU speed | Easily scales to thousands of loops |
| Typical Use | Small-to-medium plants | Large DCS installations |

**Q.B (b)** A temperature transmitter with a range of **200–600 °C** outputs **4–20 mA**. The DCS reads a current of **12.5 mA**. Determine the measured temperature. **7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $E_{\min} = 200$ °C, $E_{\max} = 600$ °C, $I = 12.5$ mA.

**Step 1 — Span in current:**

$$ \Delta I = 20 - 4 = 16 \text{ mA} $$

**Step 2 — Span in temperature:**

$$ \Delta E = 600 - 200 = 400 \text{ °C} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the linear conversion formula:**

$$ E = E_{\min} + (I - 4) \cdot \frac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{16} $$

$$ E = 200 + (12.5 - 4) \cdot \frac{400}{16} = 200 + 8.5 \cdot 25 $$

$$ E = 200 + 212.5 = 412.5 \text{ °C} $$

**Step 4 — Verification by Ratio Method:**

$$ \frac{I - 4}{20 - 4} = \frac{12.5 - 4}{16} = \frac{8.5}{16} = 0.53125 $$

$$ E = 200 + 0.53125 \times 400 = 200 + 212.5 = 412.5 \text{ °C} \;\checkmark $$

**Result:** The measured temperature is **412.5 °C**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — DDC / Supervisory / DAS Diagrams**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Anti-Aliasing Filter in DAS.** Many students draw a multiplexer feeding directly into an ADC. This loses **1–2 marks** in every KTU paper. The AAF **must** precede the S/H.
>
> 2. **Drawing a feedback arrow from the DAC back to the process in a DAS diagram.** DAS is open-loop. A feedback arrow will be marked **wrong** and the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Confusing DDC output with Supervisory output.** In DDC diagrams, the computer output goes to the **actuator** (or DAC+actuator). In Supervisory diagrams, the computer output goes to a **local controller**. Students often interchange these, losing **2–3 marks** in the comparison question.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to label signal types on the diagram arrows.** KTU expects labels like "4–20 mA", "Digital 12-bit", "Setpoint". Unlabelled arrows cost **0.5–1 mark each**.
>
> 5. **Wrong sampling theorem application.** Students write "$f_s > f_{\max}$" instead of "$f_s > 2 f_{\max}$". This is an automatic 1-mark deduction in derivation questions.
>
> 6. **Computing the wrong resolution.** For 12-bit ADC, the resolution in % is $100/4096 = 0.0244\%$, not $1/12 \approx 8.3\%$. Use $100 / 2^n$ — never $100/n$.
>
> 7. **Using positional PID in DDC.** Always write the **incremental (velocity) PID** in DDC, as it prevents **integral windup** during actuator saturation. Examiners explicitly test for this.
>
> 8. **Failing to mention graceful degradation.** When asked "What happens if the supervisory computer fails?", failing to state "loops continue at the last setpoint" costs 1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **DDC** = digital computer *directly* actuates the final control element for **multiple loops**; single point of failure.
- ☐ **Supervisory Control** = digital computer *sets the setpoint* of local controllers; local controllers do the regulatory work; **graceful degradation** on computer failure.
- ☐ **DAS** = data acquisition only, **no control loop**; open-loop monitoring; the "ICU monitor" of industry.
- ☐ The **Anti-Aliasing Filter** is **mandatory** in every DAS block diagram; place it **before the MUX or S/H**.
- ☐ The **4–20 mA** standard uses a **live zero** (4 mA) so cable break / open circuit is detectable as 0 mA.
- ☐ The 4–20 mA → engineering formula is $E = E_{\min} + (I - 4)(E_{\max} - E_{\min})/16$.
- ☐ **Shannon–Nyquist Sampling Theorem**: $f_s > 2 f_{\max}$. Practical rule: $f_s \approx 10 / T_{\text{dom}}$.
- ☐ **ADC LSB**: $Q = V_{\text{FS}} / 2^n$. Maximum error: $Q/2$.
- ☐ **SNR rule**: $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ — the **6 dB per bit** rule.
- ☐ **Incremental (velocity) PID** is preferred in DDC to prevent integral windup:
  $$ \Delta m_k = K_c\!\left[ (e_k - e_{k-1}) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i}e_k + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2}) \right] $$
- ☐ Multiplexer and DEMUX are used to **time-share** expensive ADCs/DACs across many channels in cost-sensitive 1970s–1990s designs.
- ☐ Real-time clock (RTC) is essential in DAS for **time-stamping** every sample for forensic / historian use.
- ☐ Modern DCS = **DAS + DDC + Supervisory** all running on fault-tolerant redundant controllers in a hierarchical pyramid.
- ☐ A common 14-mark question pattern: **(a)** draw block diagram (7 marks) + **(b)** numerical on ADC / 4–20 mA / sampling theorem (7 marks).
- ☐ Common 3-mark question: **difference table** between DDC, Supervisory, and DAS — memorize the four differences (output target, loop closure, fault tolerance, scale).
- ☐ Always **label arrows** in the diagram with signal type: 4–20 mA, mV, digital N-bit, setpoint, PV.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DCS Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DCS Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Control System (DCS):** A **Distributed Control System (DCS)** is a digitally networked, geographically distributed supervisory control architecture in which control functions are decentralized across multiple intelligent controllers (stations) interconnected via a high-speed, deterministic communication network, all coordinated under a central engineering and operator workstation to regulate large-scale, continuous, or batch industrial processes in **real time**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PEBRT521), the DCS is formally treated as the **natural evolution** of centralized process control, where the *single point of failure* of a Centralized Computer Control (CCC) system is eliminated by distributing both *intelligence* and *control authority* across the plant floor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Highway Traffic Grid" Model

Imagine a national highway system. Instead of having **one giant control room in New Delhi** that monitors every signal, every toll booth, and every accident across India (which would be slow, prone to collapse, and geographically impossible), each **city has its own regional control center** that manages its local traffic lights and ramps. These regional centers share congestion data with each other and report aggregate statistics to a **national command center** for strategy and long-term planning.

This is exactly how a DCS works:
- **City control centers** → Local **Controllers / DDCs (Direct Digital Controllers)** in the field
- **Highways between cities** → **Industrial Fieldbus / Control Network**
- **National command center** → **Operator Station / Engineering Station (HMI/SCADA)**
- **Citizens / Vehicles** → **Field sensors, transmitters, and final control elements (valves, motors)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The defining feature of a DCS is *geographic distribution of control function* (not merely data). Each controller runs its own control loop *autonomously* without waiting for the central station, but remains coordinated through the network.

### 1.3 Evolution of Industrial Control — From CCC to DCS

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context (Frequently asked in KTU 2-mark questions):**

| Era | Architecture | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| 1960s | Analog Single-Loop Controllers (3-15 psi pneumatic) | No central supervision, manual logging |
| 1970s | **Centralized Computer Control (CCC / DDC)** | Single point of failure, huge cable runs |
| 1980s | **Distributed Control System (DCS)** | High cost, proprietary networks |
| 1990s–2000s | **PC-based HMI + Fieldbus (Hybrid DCS)** | Mixed standards, integration issues |
| 2010s+ | **Open DCS / PlantPAx / Industry 4.0** | Cybersecurity, IIoT convergence |

### 1.4 Core Distinguishing Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Reference Values (Bolded for memory):**
> - **Plant Life Span:** **15–25 years** for a DCS deployment
> - **Tag Count Capacity:** **5,000 to 100,000+ I/O tags** per system
> - **Deterministic Scan Time:** **100 ms to 1 s** (continuous processes) and **10–50 ms** (fast batch)
> - **System Availability Target:** **99.99% (the "four nines")** — equivalent to approximately **52 minutes downtime/year**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of a DCS

A DCS is built upon three foundational engineering principles. KTU examiners frequently test your understanding of these pillars directly.

#### Pillar 1 — **Distributed Autonomy**
Each control station (or "node") executes its own PID loops, interlocks, and sequence logic **independently**. If the supervisory network fails, the plant keeps running safely at the local loop level. This is *graceful degradation*.

#### Pillar 2 — **Centralized Supervision & Coordination**
Although control is local, *all* loop setpoints, tuning parameters, alarms, trends, and recipes are managed from a unified engineering and operator environment. This is what differentiates a DCS from a bunch of isolated PLCs.

#### Pillar 3 — **Deterministic Communication Backbone**
A **redundant industrial network** (e.g., proprietary ControlNet, Modbus TCP, Foundation Fieldbus, or Profinet) carries process data on a strict, scheduled time-slot basis. There is **no "best-effort" delivery** like in office IT — packets are *guaranteed* to arrive within bounded latency.

### 2.2 Functional Architecture — The ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA-95 (also called the Purdue Model) is the international standard for industrial control hierarchy.** KTU Module-4 questions on DCS architecture map directly to this model.

| Level | Name | DCS Components | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | Process / Field | Sensors (RTD, T/C, pH), Transmitters, Valves, Motors, VFDs | Physical process measurement and actuation |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | **DDC (Direct Digital Controller)**, Multiloop controllers, Smart Transmitters with PID | Continuous PID, batch sequencing, interlocking |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | **HMI Workstations, Engineering Stations, Alarm Management Servers, Historian** | Operator graphics, trending, recipe management |
| **Level 3** | Manufacturing Operations | **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)**, Asset Management | Production scheduling, OEE, batch records |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise | **ERP (SAP, Oracle)** | Business planning, supply chain |

> [!NOTE]
> A *DCS proper* typically encompasses **Levels 0, 1, and 2**. Level 3 (MES) and Level 4 (ERP) are *integrated* but not strictly part of the DCS.

### 2.3 Key Hardware Subsystems Inside a DCS

1. **Field Instrumentation Level** — Smart transmitters (HART, Foundation Fieldbus), control valves with digital positioners, analyzers.
2. **Controller / Process Station Level** — Rack-mounted, modular I/O cards (Analog In/Out, Digital In/Out, RTD, Thermocouple) connected to redundant processor modules.
3. **Communication / Network Level** — Redundant peer-to-peer or client-server network using fieldbus protocols.
4. **Supervisory / Operator Level** — Redundant operator consoles running the HMI/SCADA software.
5. **Engineering & Asset Management Level** — Engineering workstation for configuration, version control, and predictive diagnostics.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Reliability Metrics for DCS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks you to compute the *overall availability* of a redundant DCS subsystem. Memorize these formulas.

| Symbol | Definition | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair | $MTTR = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours |
| $A$ | Availability (fraction) | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $A_{\%}$ | Availability (percent) | $A_{\%} = A \times 100$ | % |
| $A_{parallel}$ | Availability of *Parallel* Redundancy | $A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$ | dimensionless |
| $A_{series}$ | Availability of *Series* Subsystems | $A_{series} = A_1 \times A_2 \times \ldots \times A_n$ | dimensionless |
| $R(t)$ | Reliability at time $t$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$ | dimensionless |
| $\lambda$ | Failure Rate | $\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$ | failures/hour |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DCS is the **backbone of every modern continuous-process industry**:
- **Oil & Gas Refineries** — Distillation column temperature/pressure control (Honeywell Experion, Emerson DeltaV)
- **Power Plants** — Boiler drum level, turbine governing (Siemens SPPA-T3000, ABB 800xA)
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Plants** — Recipe-driven S88 batch automation
- **Water Treatment** — Multi-variable chemical dosing and filtration (Schneider PlantStruxure)
- **Cement, Pulp & Paper, Steel** — Material flow, kiln, and rolling-mill control

> [!NOTE]
> **Why engineers prefer DCS over PLC for large plants:** *Engineering efficiency*. A DCS provides a *single integrated database* (tag dictionary) for 50,000 points, whereas a PLC-based SCADA system would require manually maintaining multiple databases — error-prone and unscalable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> DCS does not have classical physics derivations like calculus or thermodynamics. Instead, KTU expects you to perform **analytical computations** on system reliability, availability of redundant subsystems, and network performance. The following worked examples follow the exact KTU valuation key pattern: *formula → substitution → simplification → boxed answer*.

---

### Example 1 — Parallel Redundancy Availability (Classic KTU Numerical)

> **Problem Statement:** A DCS controller card has a single-module availability of $A = 0.98$. The system uses a **1-out-of-2 (1oo2) parallel redundant** configuration. Compute the combined availability. (KTU University Exam pattern)

**Step 1 — Identify the redundancy topology.**
A 1oo2 parallel system functions as long as **at least one** of the two identical modules is operational. Hence, the system fails *only if both* modules fail.

**Step 2 — Write the unavailability of one module.**
Unavailability is the complement of availability.

$$
q = 1 - A = 1 - 0.98 = 0.02
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the probability that both modules fail simultaneously.**
For *independent* failures, the joint unavailability is the product of individual unavailabilities.

$$
q_{system} = q_1 \times q_2 = 0.02 \times 0.02 = 0.0004
$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to system availability.**

$$
A_{system} = 1 - q_{system} = 1 - 0.0004 = 0.9996
$$

**Step 5 — Express in percentage form (KTU convention).**

$$
A_{system,\%} = 99.96\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Adding one redundant module to a 98% reliable component boosted the system availability from **98% to 99.96%** — a **50× reduction** in unavailability. This is the *engineering rationale* behind every redundant DCS module.

---

### Example 2 — Series Subsystem Availability (Sub-System Chain)

> **Problem Statement:** A DCS path consists of three sub-blocks in series: a sensor (A = 0.99), a controller (A = 0.999), and an actuator (A = 0.995). Find the overall availability.

**Step 1 — Recall the series availability formula.**

$$
A_{series} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
A_{series} = 0.99 \times 0.999 \times 0.995
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply step by step.**

$$
A_{series} = 0.99 \times 0.999 = 0.98901
$$

$$
A_{series} = 0.98901 \times 0.995 = 0.98406
$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$
\boxed{A_{series} = 0.9841 \quad \text{or} \quad 98.41\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Even when *every component* has high availability (> 99%), a *series chain* quickly degrades. This is why critical loops in a DCS add **redundancy at the sensor, controller, and valve** level.

---

### Example 3 — MTBF and MTTR Deduction

> **Problem Statement:** A controller has been operational for a total of $8{,}000$ hours and experienced $2$ failures. The total downtime for both failures combined was $12$ hours. Compute MTBF, MTTR, and steady-state availability.

**Step 1 — Calculate MTBF.**

$$
MTBF = \frac{8000}{2} = 4000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate MTTR.**

$$
MTTR = \frac{12}{2} = 6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the availability formula.**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{4000}{4000 + 6}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$
A = \frac{4000}{4006} = 0.99850
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage form.**

$$
A_{\%} = 99.85\%
$$

---

### Example 4 — Reliability Decay Using Exponential Model

> **Problem Statement:** A network switch in the DCS has $MTBF = 50{,}000$ hours. Find the reliability $R(t)$ for a mission time of $t = 1000$ hours.

**Step 1 — Compute the failure rate.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{50000} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the exponential reliability model.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent.**

$$
R(t) = e^{-0.02}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerically evaluate.**

$$
R(t) \approx 0.9802
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

$$
R(1000) = 98.02\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in DCS design:** A reliability drop of ~2% over the first 1000 hours is acceptable for non-critical nodes but requires **planned replacement** before this threshold for safety-critical loops (SIL-rated).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — DCS vs PLC vs SCADA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important comparison table for KTU 2024 exams.** Expect either a 2-mark direct question or a 14-mark descriptive question on this.

| Parameter | DCS | PLC | SCADA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Use | Continuous / large-batch process | Discrete / machine control | Telemetry & remote monitoring |
| I/O Scale | 5,000 – 100,000+ | 50 – 2,000 | 1,000 – 50,000 |
| Architecture | Distributed, multi-station | Single or networked | Master-slave, RTU-based |
| Redundancy | Built-in (controllers, networks, HMI) | Optional, manual | Optional, mostly at master |
| Programming | Function Blocks, Sequential Function Charts, Structured Text | Ladder Logic primarily | Vendor-specific scripting |
| Database | Centralized, plant-wide tag dictionary | Per-PLC, manual sync | Central historian + per-RTU |
| Cost | Very High | Low to Medium | Medium to High |
| Vendor Examples | Honeywell, Emerson, Siemens, ABB | Allen-Bradley, Siemens S7, Schneider | Wonderware, iFIX, WinCC |
| Loop Execution | Autonomous in remote controller | Centralized in CPU | Mostly at RTU level |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid block diagrams** map the architecture and signal flow of a typical DCS as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. These diagrams are optimized to be rendered cleanly in any Markdown viewer (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, or KTU's own e-learning portal).

### 4.1 DCS Block-Level Functional Architecture (ISA-95 / Purdue Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ERPnode[Level 4 - Enterprise Resource Planning]
    MESnode[Level 3 - Manufacturing Execution System]
    HMInode[Level 2 - HMI and Engineering Workstations]
    CONnode[Level 1 - Distributed Controllers and DDCs]
    FIELDnode[Level 0 - Field Sensors and Actuators]

    ERPnode -- Business Schedule --> MESnode
    MESnode -- Production Order --> HMInode
    HMInode -- Setpoint and Recipe --> CONnode
    CONnode -- Control Signal --> FIELDnode
    FIELDnode -- Process Variable --> CONnode
    CONnode -- Status and Alarm --> HMInode
    HMInode -- Production Report --> MESnode
    MESnode -- KPI Data --> ERPnode
```

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Single DCS Controller Station

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWRmod[Redundant Power Supply Module]
    CPUmod[Redundant Processor Module]
    AIcard[Analog Input Card - AI]
    AOcard[Analog Output Card - AO]
    DIcard[Digital Input Card - DI]
    DOcard[Digital Output Card - DO]
    NETmodA[Network Interface Module A]
    NETmodB[Network Interface Module B]
    FIELDBUS[Fieldbus / Control Network]

    PWRmod --> CPUmod
    CPUmod --> AIcard
    CPUmod --> AOcard
    CPUmod --> DIcard
    CPUmod --> DOcard
    CPUmod --> NETmodA
    CPUmod --> NETmodB
    NETmodA --> FIELDBUS
    NETmodB --> FIELDBUS
```

### 4.3 Redundant Network Topology in DCS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OPR1[Operator Station 1]
    OPR2[Operator Station 2]
    ENGWS[Engineering Workstation]
    HIST[Process Historian Server]
    SWA[Network Switch A - Primary]
    SWB[Network Switch B - Secondary]
    CTRL1[Controller Station 1]
    CTRL2[Controller Station 2]
    CTRL3[Controller Station 3]
    CTRL4[Controller Station 4]
    FIELDBUS[Fieldbus to Instruments]

    OPR1 --> SWA
    OPR2 --> SWA
    ENGWS --> SWA
    HIST --> SWA
    OPR1 --> SWB
    OPR2 --> SWB
    ENGWS --> SWB
    HIST --> SWB
    SWA --> CTRL1
    SWA --> CTRL2
    SWB --> CTRL3
    SWB --> CTRL4
    CTRL1 --> FIELDBUS
    CTRL2 --> FIELDBUS
    CTRL3 --> FIELDBUS
    CTRL4 --> FIELDBUS
```

### 4.4 Information Flow Between DCS Functional Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SENS[Sensor measures Temperature Pressure Flow Level]
    TX[Transmitter converts to 4-20 mA or Fieldbus signal]
    CTRL[DDC executes PID algorithm]
    VALVE[Control Valve modulates process]
    HMI[Operator views trend on HMI]
    ALARM[Alarm Server raises High-High alarm]
    HIST[Historian stores long term trend]

    SENS --> TX
    TX --> CTRL
    CTRL --> VALVE
    CTRL --> HMI
    CTRL --> ALARM
    CTRL --> HIST
```

### 4.5 Plant-Wide DCS Layered View (Text Schematic)

```
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
|                    LEVEL 4  -  ERP / BUSINESS              |
|              (SAP, Oracle, Plant Performance KPIs)         |
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
                          ^  |
              Production  |  v  Cost / Inventory
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
|                  LEVEL 3  -  MES  /  OPERATIONS           |
|        (Batch Records, OEE, Quality, Asset Mgmt)          |
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
                          ^  |
       Recipe / Setpoint  |  v  Production Report
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
|              LEVEL 2  -  SUPERVISORY (HMI / SCADA)         |
|     (Operator Stations, Alarm Server, Historian)          |
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
                          ^  |
      Loop Tuning  / Recipe|  v Process Variables / Alarms
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
|             LEVEL 1  -  BASIC CONTROL (DCS Core)           |
|    (DDCs, Multiloop Controllers, Safety PLC, SIS)          |
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
                          ^  |
  4-20 mA / Fieldbus  / HART|  v 24 V / 4-20 mA / Bus
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
|             LEVEL 0  -  FIELD / PROCESS                    |
|   (RTD, Thermocouple, pH, Flowmeter, Control Valve, VFD)   |
+-----------------------------------------------------------+
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Distributed Control System (DCS). List any four salient features of DCS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Distributed Control System (DCS) is a computerized control architecture in which the control functions are *geographically distributed* across multiple intelligent controllers interconnected through a high-speed communication network, all coordinated and supervised from central operator and engineering workstations.
>
> **Any Four Features (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. Distributed control intelligence at field/area level.
> 2. Centralized supervision, trending, and alarm management.
> 3. Built-in redundancy in controllers, networks, and power supplies.
> 4. Modular and scalable architecture supporting 5,000 to 100,000+ I/Os.
> 5. Open-standard communication (Ethernet, Foundation Fieldbus, Profinet).
> 6. Centralized engineering database / tag dictionary.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Compare centralized computer control and distributed control system on any three parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Centralized Control (CCC) | Distributed Control (DCS) |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Single computer controls entire plant | Multiple controllers distributed geographically |
| Reliability | Single point of failure; total plant trip on CPU failure | High; redundant controllers — *graceful degradation* |
| Cabling | Long, expensive point-to-point wiring | Short, structured fieldbus cable runs |
| Scalability | Limited; CPU becomes bottleneck | Easily expandable by adding new stations |

*(Any 3 parameters — 1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Below we provide the full Module-4 internal choice set: **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the functional architecture of a Distributed Control System with the help of a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of each level. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of DCS over conventional PLC-based control. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Introduction:**
The functional architecture of a DCS is conventionally represented by the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** which divides the plant into **five hierarchical levels**.

**Step 2 (4 marks) — Levels with functions (½ mark each):**
- **Level 0 – Process / Field:** Sensors (RTD, thermocouple, orifice plate), transmitters, and final control elements (control valves, VFDs, motors) interface with the physical process.
- **Level 1 – Basic Control:** Distributed Digital Controllers (DDCs) execute **continuous PID loops, batch sequencing, and interlock logic** autonomously. Each DDC handles a plant area (e.g., a distillation column).
- **Level 2 – Supervisory Control:** **HMI / Operator stations** display process graphics, trends, and alarms. **Engineering workstations** configure loops, recipes, and tuning parameters. **Historian server** archives long-term data.
- **Level 3 – Manufacturing Operations (MES):** Manages production orders, batch records, OEE, and quality. Not a part of the DCS core, but integrated.
- **Level 4 – Enterprise (ERP):** Business planning, finance, and supply-chain integration.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Diagram:**
Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the standard block diagram.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
This hierarchical separation gives DCS its hallmark property: **distributed autonomy + centralized supervision**.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Introductory comparison.**

**Step 2 (5 marks — list any 5 points, 1 mark each):**

| # | Advantage of DCS | Limitation of DCS |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single integrated database** for entire plant; no manual data syncing | High initial capital cost (5–10× PLC cost) |
| 2 | Built-in **redundancy** for controllers, networks, HMI | Vendor lock-in — proprietary hardware & software |
| 3 | **Scalable** from 5,000 to 100,000+ I/Os | Requires **specialized trained engineers** for configuration |
| 4 | Centralized engineering → easier **plant-wide changes** | Longer **engineering lead time** during project execution |
| 5 | **Advanced process control (APC)** modules easily integrated | Complexity of cyber-security management |

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
DCS is preferred over PLC-based SCADA for *large, continuous, or complex batch plants* where engineering efficiency, redundancy, and advanced control outweigh the higher initial cost.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the components of a typical DCS controller station. Explain the function of I/O cards. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A controller card has availability $A = 0.985$. Two such cards are used in a **1-out-of-2 redundant** configuration. Compute the system availability. If the plant uses **two such redundant pairs in series**, find the total availability. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition:**
A *DCS Controller Station* (also called a *Process Station* or *Controller*) is a rack-mounted, modular subsystem that performs real-time control of a defined plant area (e.g., a furnace, a reactor, a distillation column).

**Step 2 (4 marks) — Components (block diagram description, refer Section 4.2):**
- **Redundant Power Supply Module (1 mark):** Feeds 24 V DC to all modules with hot-swap capability; one PSU can fail without interrupting control.
- **Redundant Processor / CPU Module (1 mark):** The brain of the station; executes the control algorithm (PID, sequencing, interlocks). Two CPUs run in *active-active* or *active-standby* redundancy.
- **I/O Cards (1 mark):** Plug-in signal-conditioning cards interfacing the CPU with field instruments (see Step 3).
- **Network Interface Modules (1 mark):** Connect the station to the supervisory network; usually dual-redundant for fault tolerance.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — I/O Card Functions:**
- **Analog Input (AI):** Receives 4–20 mA / 1–5 V / RTD / Thermocouple signals from transmitters.
- **Analog Output (AO):** Outputs 4–20 mA to position control valves and variable-speed drives.
- **Digital Input (DI):** Reads ON/OFF status of switches, contacts, limit switches.
- **Digital Output (DO):** Drives relays, solenoids, motor contactors, indicating lamps.
- *Additional cards:* Pulse Input, Foundation Fieldbus Interface, HART Multiplexer.

**Step 4 (0.5 mark) — Conclusion:**
The modular I/O architecture allows the engineer to expand or reconfigure a station by simply adding cards, without disturbing the existing wiring.

##### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State given data:**
Single card availability $A_1 = 0.985$. Two cards in **1oo2 (parallel)** redundancy.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Unavailability of one card.**

$$
q_1 = 1 - A_1 = 1 - 0.985 = 0.015
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — System unavailability (parallel).**

$$
q_{sys} = q_1 \times q_2 = 0.015 \times 0.015 = 0.000225
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — System availability of the redundant pair.**

$$
A_{pair} = 1 - q_{sys} = 1 - 0.000225 = 0.999775
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Series combination of two such pairs.**

$$
A_{total} = A_{pair} \times A_{pair} = (0.999775)^2
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Final calculation.**

$$
A_{total} = 0.9995500
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Percentage form & interpretation.**

$$
A_{total,\%} = 99.955\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even after two redundant pairs in series, availability stays above **99.95%** — equivalent to about **4 hours of downtime per year**. This justifies the cost of redundancy in safety-critical DCS loops.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in DCS Questions:**
> 1. **Defining DCS as "a system where SCADA runs over a network"** — *Wrong.* A DCS is fundamentally *control* that is distributed, not merely *data* that is centralized. Marks lost on Part (a) definitions.
> 2. **Confusing PLC with DCS** — A PLC is *one* controller; a DCS is a *plant-wide architecture* of controllers. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for imprecise comparison language.
> 3. **Writing the formula for series availability as $A_{series} = 1 - (1-A_1)(1-A_2)$** — That is the *parallel* formula. A common transposition error costs full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to express availability in percentage** — KTU's valuation key requires the final answer in **A%** form for the **last sub-part** to be awarded full credit.
> 5. **Drawing the ISA-95 model without labeling the levels** — Unlabelled diagrams fetch **0 marks** in 14-mark questions. Always write "Level 0 / 1 / 2 / 3 / 4" alongside each block.
> 6. **Mixing up the MTTR and MTBF definitions** — Remember: *MTBF is the operating duration between failures*; *MTTR is the downtime during a failure*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — DCS Introduction**

- **Definition:** DCS = Distributed Control System — *geographically distributed control + centralized supervision* over a deterministic network.
- **Evolution:** Pneumatic → Analog Electronic → **Centralized Computer Control (CCC)** → **DCS** → PC-based Open DCS → Industry 4.0 / IIoT DCS.
- **ISA-95 / Purdue Model Levels (0 to 4):** Process → Basic Control → Supervisory → MES → ERP. DCS proper covers Levels 0, 1, 2.
- **Three DCS Pillars:** Distributed Autonomy · Centralized Supervision · Deterministic Communication.
- **Hardware:** Redundant PSU · Redundant CPU · AI/AO/DI/DO cards · Network Interface Modules · Engineering & Operator Workstations · Historian · Alarm Server.
- **Software Standards:** IEC 61131-3 (programming), IEC 61508/61511 (functional safety), ISA-88 (batch), ISA-95 (integration).
- **Network:** Redundant Ethernet, Foundation Fieldbus, Profinet, HART, Modbus TCP.
- **Reliability Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
  - $A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$
  - $A_{series} = A_1 \times A_2 \times \ldots \times A_n$
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$
- **Typical Targets:** Availability **≥ 99.99%** · Scan time **≤ 1 s** · I/O capacity **5,000 to 100,000+** · Plant life **15–25 years**.
- **Industry Examples:** Honeywell **Experion PKS**, Emerson **DeltaV**, Siemens **PCS 7 / SPPA-T3000**, ABB **System 800xA**, Yokogawa **CENTUM VP**, Schneider **PlantStruxure PES**.
- **Key Advantage vs PLC:** Plant-wide single tag dictionary & engineering efficiency.
- **Key Limitation:** High cost, vendor lock-in, and longer project lead time.
- **Must-remember exam phrase:** *"A DCS eliminates the single point of failure of centralized control by distributing intelligence to the field while retaining centralized supervision."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Function Blocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT521 - PLC AND DISTRIBUTED CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Various Function Blocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **IEC 61131-3** standard that governs PLC programming languages, a **Function Block (FB)** is defined as a **reusable, encapsulated software unit** that retains its internal state (memory) between execution cycles. A Function Block is instantiated (assigned a unique memory location by a name and optionally an instance name) and can have multiple instances of the same type running independently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (High-Weightage for KTU Exams):**
> - **Function (F):** Has NO internal memory. Same inputs always produce same outputs. Behaves like a pure mathematical function.
> - **Function Block (FB):** HAS internal memory (instance memory). Outputs depend on both current inputs AND past history of inputs. Behaves like an object/class.
> - **Program (PRG):** Top-level execution unit, can be assigned to tasks.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEBRT521, Module 4)**, the category "Various Function Blocks" typically covers:
1. **Timer Function Blocks** — TON, TOF, TP
2. **Counter Function Blocks** — CTU, CTD, CTUD
3. **Bistable (Flip-Flop) Function Blocks** — SR (Set-Reset), RS (Reset-Set)
4. **Edge Detection Function Blocks** — R_TRIG, F_TRIG
5. **Comparison and Mathematical Function Blocks**
6. **PID Controller Function Block**
7. **Data Handling and Communication Function Blocks**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Vending Machine**
> 
> Think of a Function Block like a **vending machine**:
> - You insert **coins (Inputs)**.
> - You press a **button (Input)**.
> - The machine **remembers how much money you have inserted (Internal State/Memory)**.
> - It **dispenses a product (Output)** when conditions are met.
> - Each vending machine on a street corner is an **INSTANCE** of the same design — but each holds its own money and tracks its own sales.
> 
> If you unplug the machine, the memory of the inserted coin is **lost (volatile memory)**. This is exactly how a PLC Function Block works — it remembers things like "how long has this timer been running" or "how many pulses has this counter received" between scan cycles.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for Function Blocks:**
> - **IEC 61131-3 Standard:** The international standard governing PLC programming languages and Function Block definitions.
> - **Typical FB Memory Allocation:** Bistable FBs use **1 bit** per instance; 16-bit counters use **16 bits**; Timers typically use **16-bit or 32-bit** time base depending on the manufacturer.
> - **Time Base Resolution:** Common timer resolution values are **1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms, or 1 s** depending on the PLC platform.
> - **PID Loop Execution Time:** Typical industrial PID loops execute at **10 ms to 1 s** intervals (sample time, $T_s$).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Function Blocks

Function Blocks in IEC 61131-3 are broadly classified into:

| Category | Examples | Purpose |
|----------|----------|---------|
| Standard FBs (defined in IEC 61131-3) | TON, TOF, TP, CTU, CTD, CTUD, SR, RS, R_TRIG, F_TRIG | Built-in, manufacturer-independent |
| Manufacturer-Specific FBs | PID, motion control, communication blocks | Vendor-defined extensions |
| User-Defined FBs | Custom FBs created by the programmer | Application-specific reusable code |
| Derived FBs | Created from other FBs | Hierarchical composition |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 Timer Function Blocks

Timer FBs measure elapsed time. The IEC 61131-3 standard defines **three standard timer types**:

### 2.1.1 TON — On-Delay Timer (Pulse on Delay)

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. When input `IN` transitions from **0 → 1** (rising edge), the timer **starts counting**.
2. The output `ET` (Elapsed Time) increments at each scan cycle based on the resolution.
3. When `ET ≥ PT` (Preset Time), the output `Q` is set to **1**.
4. If `IN` returns to **0** at any moment, the timer is **reset** and `Q` returns to **0`.

**The 'Why':** Used to **delay an action** — e.g., a motor should start 5 seconds after the start button is pressed (to allow lubrication pressure to build up).

### 2.1.2 TOF — Off-Delay Timer (Pulse off Delay)

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. When input `IN` is **1**, output `Q` is **1** and `ET = 0`.
2. When `IN` transitions from **1 → 0** (falling edge), the timer **starts counting** from `0` upward.
3. The output `Q` **remains 1** until `ET ≥ PT`.
4. When `ET ≥ PT`, `Q` becomes **0** and `ET` stops.

**The 'Why':** Used to **keep something running for a while after the input stops** — e.g., a cooling fan should run for 30 seconds after the motor is turned off.

### 2.1.3 TP — Pulse Timer

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. A rising edge on `IN` starts a **fixed-duration pulse**.
2. Output `Q` is set to **1** for exactly `PT` time units, regardless of the duration of `IN`.
3. `ET` measures the elapsed time of the pulse.
4. A new rising edge during the pulse is **ignored** (no retriggering).

**The 'Why':** Used to generate a **precise fixed-length pulse** — e.g., a valve opening pulse of exactly 2 seconds.

---

## 2.2 Counter Function Blocks

### 2.2.1 CTU — Count Up Counter

Increments the counter value `CV` by **1** on each rising edge of input `CU`. When `CV ≥ PV` (Preset Value), output `Q` is set to **1**. The reset input `R` clears the counter.

### 2.2.2 CTD — Count Down Counter

Decrements `CV` by **1** on each rising edge of `CD`. When `CV ≤ 0`, output `Q` is set to **1**. The load input `LD` sets `CV` to `PV`.

### 2.2.3 CTUD — Count Up/Down Counter

Combines both CTU and CTD. Has two outputs: `QU` (up limit reached) and `QD` (down limit reached). Has separate `CU`, `CD`, `R` (reset), and `LD` (load) inputs.

---

## 2.3 Bistable (Flip-Flop) Function Blocks

### 2.3.1 SR — Set-Dominant Bistable

When `S1 = 1`, output `Q` is **forced to 1** (Set). When `R = 1`, `Q` is **reset to 0**. If both are 1 simultaneously, **S1 dominates** (Q = 1).

### 2.3.2 RS — Reset-Dominant Bistable

Same as SR but **R dominates** when both inputs are 1 (Q = 0).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The dominance rule is the most frequently asked distinction in KTU exams** — make sure to clearly state which input has priority.

---

## 2.4 Edge Detection Function Blocks

### 2.4.1 R_TRIG — Rising Edge Detector

Generates a **single-cycle pulse of `Q = 1`** when input `CLK` transitions from **0 → 1**. After the cycle, `Q` returns to 0 even if `CLK` remains 1.

### 2.4.2 F_TRIG — Falling Edge Detector

Generates a **single-cycle pulse of `Q = 1`** when input `CLK` transitions from **1 → 0**.

**Internal Memory Mechanism:** These FBs use a hidden boolean variable (e.g., `M` for R_TRIG) that stores the previous state of the input. The output is computed as `Q = CLK AND NOT M`, then `M` is updated to `CLK` for the next cycle.

---

## 2.5 PID Controller Function Block

The PID FB is one of the most important advanced function blocks in industrial automation.

**Inputs:** `SP` (Setpoint), `PV` (Process Variable / feedback), `KP`, `TI` (Integral time), `TD` (Derivative time), `MANUAL`, `RESET`.

**Outputs:** `CV` (Control Variable — sent to actuator), `ERROR` (SP − PV).

### 2.5.1 Standard PID Equation (Parallel / ISA Form)

The continuous-time PID output is:

$$CV(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e(t)\,dt + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

where $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ is the error signal.

### 2.5.2 Discrete-Time (Digital) PID — Velocity Form

For implementation in a PLC scan cycle, the **velocity (incremental) form** is preferred because it avoids integral windup accumulation:

$$\Delta CV_n = K_p \left[ (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \frac{T_s}{T_i} e_n + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) \right]$$

and the actual output is:

$$CV_n = CV_{n-1} + \Delta CV_n$$

where $T_s$ is the **sampling time (scan period)**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Key Equations and Logic for Function Blocks**

| Function Block | Key Equation / Logic | Output Condition | Memory? |
|----------------|----------------------|------------------|---------|
| **TON** | $ET$ increments while $IN=1$; $Q = (ET \geq PT)$ | $Q=1$ after delay $PT$ | Yes (ET) |
| **TOF** | $ET$ increments after $IN$ falls; $Q=1$ until $ET \geq PT$ | $Q=1$ when $IN=1$ or $ET < PT$ | Yes (ET) |
| **TP** | Pulse of fixed duration $PT$ on rising edge of $IN$ | $Q=1$ for exactly $PT$ | Yes (ET) |
| **CTU** | $CV_{n} = CV_{n-1} + 1$ on rising edge of $CU$ | $Q=1$ when $CV \geq PV$ | Yes (CV) |
| **CTD** | $CV_{n} = CV_{n-1} - 1$ on rising edge of $CD$ | $Q=1$ when $CV \leq 0$ | Yes (CV) |
| **CTUD** | Combined up/down; outputs $QU$ and $QD$ | $QU=1$ if $CV \geq PV$; $QD=1$ if $CV \leq 0$ | Yes (CV) |
| **SR** | $Q = S1 \lor (Q \land \overline{R})$ | Set-dominant | Yes (Q) |
| **RS** | $Q = \overline{R} \land (S \lor Q)$ | Reset-dominant | Yes (Q) |
| **R_TRIG** | $Q = CLK \land \overline{M}$; $M_{n} = CLK$ | 1-cycle pulse on rising edge | Yes (M) |
| **F_TRIG** | $Q = \overline{CLK} \land M$; $M_{n} = CLK$ | 1-cycle pulse on falling edge | Yes (M) |
| **PID** | $CV_n = K_p e_n + K_i \sum e + K_d (e_n - e_{n-1})/T_s$ | Continuous correction | Yes (sum, prev errors) |

**Key Reminders:**
- $K_p$ is **dimensionless** (or in %/% depending on convention)
- $K_i = K_p / T_i$ where $T_i$ is the **integral time constant** in seconds
- $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ where $T_d$ is the **derivative time constant** in seconds
- $T_s$ is the **scan period** in seconds

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Function Block | Industrial Application |
|----------------|------------------------|
| TON | Conveyor belt startup delay, lubrication pump delay |
| TOF | Cooling fan run-down, lamp fade-out |
| TP | Solenoid valve actuation pulse |
| CTU | Production count, bottle filling tally |
| CTD | Inventory depletion tracking |
| CTUD | Car parking lot occupancy (in/out) |
| SR/RS | Latching motor starters, alarm acknowledge |
| R_TRIG/F_TRIG | Push-button debouncing, single-step sequencing |
| PID | Temperature control, flow control, pressure control, level control, speed control |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: R_TRIG Internal Memory Mechanism

The Rising Edge Detector (R_TRIG) is the most fundamental edge-detection function block, and understanding its memory mechanism is critical for KTU exams.

### Step 1: Define the Variables

We need two internal variables:
- `CLK` (Boolean input) — the signal being monitored.
- `M` (Boolean, internal memory) — stores the **previous** state of `CLK`.
- `Q` (Boolean output) — high for one cycle when rising edge is detected.

### Step 2: Define the Detection Logic

A rising edge occurs when the current `CLK` is **1** AND the previous `CLK` was **0**. We can express this logically as:

$$Q = CLK \land \overline{M}$$

### Step 3: Update the Memory

After computing `Q`, the memory must be updated so the next cycle has the correct "previous" value:

$$M_{n} = CLK_{n}$$

### Step 4: Truth Table Verification

We can verify the logic using a **truth table** that tracks `M` (previous state) and `CLK` (current state):

| Cycle | $M_{n-1}$ (Previous) | $CLK_n$ (Current) | $\overline{M_{n-1}}$ | $Q = CLK_n \land \overline{M_{n-1}}$ | $M_n$ (Next) |
|-------|----------------------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **1** ✓ | 1 |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **1** ✓ | 1 |

**Interpretation:** `Q` becomes 1 **only** at cycles 2 and 5 — exactly when a rising edge (0→1) occurs. This confirms the logic.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a push-button is pressed at time $t = 2$ s (CLK goes from 0 to 1) and held for 3 seconds. The PLC scan time $T_s = 100$ ms. 

- At $t = 1.9$ s: $CLK = 0$, $M = 0$ → $Q = 0$
- At $t = 2.0$ s: $CLK = 1$, $M = 0$ → $Q = 1$ (rising edge detected) → $M$ becomes 1
- At $t = 2.1$ s: $CLK = 1$, $M = 1$ → $Q = 0$ (no longer a rising edge) → $M$ remains 1
- At $t = 2.2$ s: $CLK = 1$, $M = 1$ → $Q = 0$

**Result:** `Q` is high for **only one scan cycle** (100 ms), even though the button was held for 3 seconds. This is the essence of edge detection.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CTU Counter Behavior

The Count Up Counter increments on each rising edge of `CU` until the preset value is reached.

### Step 1: Define Variables
- `CU` (Boolean input) — count pulse input.
- `R` (Boolean input) — reset.
- `PV` (Integer) — preset value.
- `CV` (Integer) — current count value.
- `Q` (Boolean output) — count complete flag.

### Step 2: Reset Logic
If $R = 1$, then $CV = 0$ and $Q = 0$.

### Step 3: Counting Logic (when R = 0)

$$CV_n = \begin{cases} CV_{n-1} + 1 & \text{if } CU \text{ has a rising edge (i.e., } CU_n = 1 \text{ and } M_{n-1} = 0) \\ CV_{n-1} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This uses the same edge-detection trick as R_TRIG.

### Step 4: Output Logic

$$Q = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } CV \geq PV \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 5: Worked Example

Suppose $PV = 5$. We send 7 pulses to `CU`:

| Pulse # | $CV$ (before) | Rising edge on $CU$? | $CV$ (after) | $Q$ |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|--------------|-----|
| 1 | 0 | Yes | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 | Yes | 2 | 0 |
| 3 | 2 | Yes | 3 | 0 |
| 4 | 3 | Yes | 4 | 0 |
| 5 | 4 | Yes | **5** | **1** ✓ |
| 6 | 5 | Yes | 6 | 1 |
| 7 | 6 | Yes | 7 | 1 |

**Result:** Output `Q` latches at 1 once $CV \geq PV = 5$ and remains at 1 even as the count continues to grow.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PID Velocity Form (Step-by-Step)

The **velocity form** of the PID is preferred in PLC implementation because it eliminates the risk of **integral windup** by computing only the *change* in output each cycle.

### Step 1: Start with the Continuous PID

$$CV(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e(t)\,dt + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

### Step 2: Discretize Each Term

**Proportional term (P):** Direct mapping.

$$P_n = K_p \cdot e_n$$

**Integral term (I):** Replace the integral with a forward Euler sum over $N$ samples.

$$I_n = \frac{K_p}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k \cdot T_s = \frac{K_p \cdot T_s}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k$$

**Derivative term (D):** Replace the derivative with a backward difference.

$$D_n = K_p \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s}$$

### Step 3: Total Output (Position Form)

$$CV_n = K_p \cdot e_n + \frac{K_p \cdot T_s}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \frac{K_p \cdot T_d}{T_s}(e_n - e_{n-1})$$

### Step 4: Derive the Velocity Form

Subtract $CV_{n-1}$ from $CV_n$. Note that **all accumulated integral terms cancel out** because $CV_{n-1}$ contains the sum from $0$ to $n-1$.

$$\begin{aligned}
CV_n - CV_{n-1} &= K_p \left[ e_n - e_{n-1} \right] + \frac{K_p \cdot T_s}{T_i} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e_k \right] \\
&\quad + \frac{K_p \cdot T_d}{T_s}\left[ (e_n - e_{n-1}) - (e_{n-1} - e_{n-2}) \right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Simplify the Sum

The difference of two consecutive sums simplifies to just the new term:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e_k = e_n$$

### Step 6: Final Velocity Form

$$\Delta CV_n = K_p \left[ (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \frac{T_s}{T_i} e_n + \frac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) \right]$$

### Step 7: Final Output

$$CV_n = CV_{n-1} + \Delta CV_n$$

**Why This Matters:** The integral accumulator is **never explicitly stored** — the integrator "lives" inside $CV_{n-1}$. This makes the velocity form naturally anti-windup and is the preferred implementation in modern PLCs.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: TON vs TOF vs TP

| Property | TON (On-Delay) | TOF (Off-Delay) | TP (Pulse) |
|----------|----------------|-----------------|------------|
| Trigger | Rising edge of IN | Falling edge of IN | Rising edge of IN |
| Q=1 when | $IN=1$ AND $ET \geq PT$ | $IN=1$ OR ($IN=0$ AND $ET < PT$) | $0 < ET < PT$ |
| Q=0 when | $IN=0$ OR $ET < PT$ | $IN=1$ (when ET=0) | $ET \geq PT$ (after pulse ends) |
| ET behavior | Increments while $IN=1$, resets when $IN=0$ | Resets while $IN=1$, increments when $IN=0$ | Increments on rising edge, freezes after $PT$ |
| Retriggerable? | No (resets on $IN=0$) | No (resets on $IN=1$) | No (ignores new edges during pulse) |
| Common mnemonic | "Delay ON" | "Delay OFF" | "Fixed-length pulse" |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: TON Timing Calculation

**Problem:** A TON timer has $PT = 5$ s and resolution 0.1 s. The input $IN$ is 1 from $t = 2$ s to $t = 12$ s. Plot $ET$ and $Q$ over time.

**Solution Logic:**

1. The timer starts at $t = 2$ s when $IN$ becomes 1.
2. $ET$ increases by 0.1 s each scan (assuming 100 ms cycle).
3. $ET$ reaches $PT = 5$ s at $t = 2 + 5 = 7$ s. At this moment, $Q$ becomes 1.
4. $Q$ remains 1 as long as $IN$ stays 1.
5. At $t = 12$ s, $IN$ drops to 0, so $Q$ goes to 0 and $ET$ resets to 0.

**Output Sequence:**

$$Q(t) = \begin{cases} 0 & 0 \leq t < 7 \\ 1 & 7 \leq t < 12 \\ 0 & t \geq 12 \end{cases}$$

This is a classic **on-delay** behavior — useful for delayed motor start.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram: General Function Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input 1: IN] --> FB[Function Block\nInstance Memory]
    B[Input 2: PT / PV / S1] --> FB
    C[Input 3: R / LD / R] --> FB
    FB --> D[Output 1: Q]
    FB --> E[Output 2: ET / CV / Q1]
    FB -.->|Stores between scans| F[(Internal State\nMemory)]
    
    style FB fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The blue block is the Function Block instance. The red cylinder represents the **instance memory** that persists between PLC scan cycles — this is what distinguishes a Function Block from a Function.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: R_TRIG (Rising Edge Detector) Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scan Cycle Starts] --> B[Read CLK value]
    B --> C{Read Memory M\nprevious state}
    C --> D{Q = CLK AND NOT M?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Set Q = 1\nRising Edge Detected]
    D -->|No| F[Set Q = 0]
    E --> G[Update M = CLK]
    F --> G
    G --> H[End of Scan Cycle]
    H -.->|Next cycle| A
    
    style E fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: TON Timer State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: Initial state
    Idle --> Counting: IN rises 0 to 1\nET = 0
    Counting --> Counting: IN = 1\nET increments
    Counting --> Done: ET >= PT\nQ becomes 1
    Done --> Done: IN = 1\nQ stays 1
    Done --> Idle: IN falls to 0\nET resets, Q = 0
    Counting --> Idle: IN falls to 0\nET resets, Q = 0
    
    note right of Done
        Q = 1 (Output ON)
        ET continues or freezes
    end note
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: CTU (Count Up Counter) Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTU_Block["CTU Function Block Instance"]
        IN_CU[CU: Count Pulse] --> EDGE[Rising Edge\nDetector]
        EDGE --> ADD[+1 Adder]
        MEM[(CV: Current\nValue Memory)] --> ADD
        ADD --> NEW_CV[New CV]
        NEW_CV --> MEM
        IN_R[R: Reset] --> MEM
        MEM --> COMP{CV >= PV?}
        COMP -->|Yes| OUT_Q[Q = 1]
        COMP -->|No| OUT_Q2[Q = 0]
    end
    
    IN_PV[PV: Preset Value] --> COMP
    MEM --> OUT[Output CV]
    
    style MEM fill:#ff9800,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style EDGE fill:#4caf50,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: PID Controller Function Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SP: Setpoint] --> SUM[+ Sum Node]
    B[PV: Process Variable\nFeedback] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[e = SP - PV]
    ERR --> P_TERM[P Term\nKp x e]
    ERR --> I_TERM[I Term\nKp/Ti x sum e]
    ERR --> D_TERM[D Term\nKp x Td x de/dt]
    P_TERM --> TOTAL[Sum P+I+D]
    I_TERM --> TOTAL
    D_TERM --> TOTAL
    TOTAL --> SAT{Saturation?\nMin/Max limits}
    SAT -->|Yes| LIM[Limit CV]
    SAT -->|No| OUT_CV[Output CV]
    LIM --> OUT_CV
    OUT_CV --> ACT[Actuator\nValve / Motor / Heater]
    ACT --> PROC[Industrial Process]
    PROC --> SENSOR[Sensor / Transmitter]
    SENSOR --> B
    SENSOR -.->|4-20 mA / 0-10 V| SENSE_BLOCK[Signal Conditioning]
    SENSE_BLOCK --> B
    
    style SUM fill:#1976d2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style TOTAL fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OUT_CV fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is a **closed-loop control** architecture. The red node sums the three PID terms. The green node is the actuator that physically drives the process. The feedback from the sensor (via 4-20 mA current loop, a standard industrial signal) closes the loop. The saturation block prevents the output from exceeding safe actuator limits.

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram: SR vs RS Bistable Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SR_Block["SR: Set-Dominant Bistable"]
        SR_S1[S1 input] --> SR_OR[OR gate]
        SR_Q_PREV[Q from memory] --> SR_AND[AND gate]
        SR_R[R input] --> SR_NOT[NOT]
        SR_NOT --> SR_AND
        SR_AND --> SR_OR
        SR_OR --> SR_OUT[Q output]
        SR_OUT -.->|Stores state| SR_Q_PREV
    end
    
    subgraph RS_Block["RS: Reset-Dominant Bistable"]
        RS_S[S input] --> RS_OR[OR gate]
        RS_S --> RS_AND[AND gate]
        RS_Q_PREV[Q from memory] --> RS_OR
        RS_R[R input] --> RS_AND
        RS_AND --> RS_OR
        RS_OR --> RS_OUT[Q output]
        RS_OUT -.->|Stores state| RS_Q_PREV
    end
    
    style SR_OR fill:#ff9800,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SR_AND fill:#ff9800,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style RS_OR fill:#9c27b0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style RS_AND fill:#9c27b0,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Distinction:** In the **SR** (orange), the R input goes through a NOT gate before the AND — meaning if R=1, the AND is blocked. In the **RS** (purple), the S input goes through the AND with R — meaning if R=1, the AND blocks S. This is the **dominance rule** visualized.

---

## 4.7 Timing Diagram: TON vs TOF vs TP Behavior

```mermaid
gantt
    title Comparison of TON, TOF, and TP Timing Behavior
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    
    section IN Signal
    IN_pulse_0_5s    :a1, 0, 5s
    IN_low_5_8s      :a2, 5, 3s
    IN_high_8_12s    :a3, 8, 4s
    IN_low_after_12s :a4, 12, 3s
    
    section TON Output Q
    TON_wait_3s      :b1, 0, 3s
    TON_active_3_5s  :b2, 3, 2s
    TON_off_5_8s     :b3, 5, 3s
    TON_wait_8_11s   :b4, 8, 3s
    TON_active_11_12s :b5, 11, 1s
    
    section TOF Output Q
    TOF_high_0_5s    :c1, 0, 5s
    TOF_delay_5_8s   :c2, 5, 3s
    TOF_high_8_12s   :c3, 8, 4s
    TOF_delay_12_15s :c4, 12, 3s
    
    section TP Output Q
    TP_pulse1_0_2s   :d1, 0, 2s
    TP_idle_2_8s     :d2, 2, 6s
    TP_pulse2_8_10s  :d3, 8, 2s
```
